summary: To improve living conditions in Toronto apartment buildings, City Council has updated fees related to its RentSafeTO program. Building owners will now pay a new, small fee to re-issue a colour-coded sign and face higher charges if the City needs to fix problems on their property, encouraging better maintenance and accountability.

title: 105-109 Vanderhoof Avenue and 10 Brentcliffe Road - Official Plan Amendment Application - Appeal Report
summary: City Council is preparing to challenge a developer's proposal to change how land can be used at 105-109 Vanderhoof Avenue and 10 Brentcliffe Road. The developer wants to allow mixed-use development instead of current employment-only zoning, and the City Solicitor will present the city's opposition at the provincial land tribunal while also trying to reach a compromise.

title: Review of Toronto's Street Vending and Artists By-laws

summary: Toronto City Council has updated its rules for street vendors and artists, allowing mobile food vendors to operate for up to 12 hours and lifting a ban on new food permits in Wards 10, 11, and 13. This move modernizes street vending regulations across the city, though older curb lane vending permits will be phased out by January 2029.

title: Review of Toronto's Street Vending and Artists By-laws
Applegrove Community Complex: Update on Relocation Options
summary: Toronto City Council has approved a confidential plan to acquire a new property for the Applegrove Community Complex. This decision will allow the complex to relocate and continue providing important community services in the Toronto-Danforth area. The City will also seek external funding, likely from the provincial government, to help cover future renovation and setup costs.

title: The Electrification Advantage
summary: Toronto is moving forward with a strategy to significantly boost the use of renewable energy and electric technologies like rooftop solar and battery storage across the city. This plan aims to accelerate climate action, reduce carbon emissions, and help residents access programs and funding for cleaner, more efficient energy.

title: Congestion Management Plan - 2026 Spring Update
summary: City Council is updating how it manages road disruptions, which affect traffic across Toronto. This involves clarifying rules and fees for street work, making it easier for community street events, and exempting eligible affordable housing projects from certain road disruption fees to help their development. The goal is to reduce congestion and support the creation of more affordable homes.

title: 2775 Jane Street - Zoning By-law Amendment - Decision Report - Approval
summary: A proposal to build a 27-storey apartment building with 420 new homes at 2775 Jane Street was recently considered by a local community council. The council did not recommend approving the necessary zoning changes for this development, which means City Council will now make the final decision on whether this significant project proceeds.

title: Winter Surge Capacity Snow Shovellers
Pothole Blitz and Local Road Repair Strategy
Pothole Blitz and Local Road Repair Strategy
summary: Toronto City Council has approved a new strategy to tackle potholes across the city, aiming to make roads safer and smoother for residents. This plan includes an ongoing "pothole blitz" informed by data, exploring smart technology to detect road damage, and improving how residents can report potholes.

title: Update on Auditor General's Office 2026 Work Plan Related to City Council Request for SmartTrack Investigation
Update on Auditor General's Office 2026 Work Plan Related to City Council Request for SmartTrack Investigation
summary: City Council is moving to increase accountability and oversight for the SmartTrack transit program and future major city projects. They have asked Metrolinx to provide information for an audit by Toronto's Auditor General and requested the Auditor General of Ontario also audit the SmartTrack program. These measures aim to ensure taxpayer money is well-managed by including audit rights in future capital agreements where the city provides significant funding.

title: Grocery Store Pilot Project - by Councillor Anthony Perruzza, seconded by Councillor Mike Colle
Grocery Store Pilot Project - by Councillor Anthony Perruzza, seconded by Councillor Mike Colle
Grocery Store Pilot Project - by Councillor Anthony Perruzza, seconded by Councillor Mike Colle
Grocery Store Pilot Project - by Councillor Anthony Perruzza, seconded by Councillor Mike Colle
summary: Toronto City Council has approved a pilot project to open four city-run grocery stores, with one in each Community Council district. These stores will operate as not-for-profits to provide affordable food options, especially in neighborhoods with limited access to full-service grocery stores and lower incomes.

title: One Country, One Flag; Celebrating the Canadian Flag - by Councillor Jon Burnside, seconded by Councillor Michael Thompson
One Country, One Flag; Celebrating the Canadian Flag - by Councillor Jon Burnside, seconded by Councillor Michael Thompson
One Country, One Flag; Celebrating the Canadian Flag - by Councillor Jon Burnside, seconded by Councillor Michael Thompson
summary: Toronto City Council has decided to stop flying flags of foreign nations, or flags depicting them, on courtesy poles at City Hall and civic centres, effective December 1, 2026. This change alters how the city officially recognizes diverse communities through flag displays and will lead to a new review for a broader community recognition program.

title: Evaluating Changes to Mute Swan Management in Etobicoke-Lakeshore - by Councillor Amber Morley, Seconded by Councillor Josh Matlow
summary: Responding to community concerns about local wildlife, City Council will assess changes to the mute swan population management program in Etobicoke-Lakeshore, including studying the feasibility of leaving some viable eggs in nests. Council also requested the federal government to review the scientific basis for mute swan population control, which could impact future wildlife management policies in Toronto.

title: Addressing Recycling in Public Spaces - by Councillor Lily Cheng, seconded by Councillor Paula Fletcher
Addressing Recycling in Public Spaces - by Councillor Lily Cheng, seconded by Councillor Paula Fletcher
summary: Toronto is rethinking public space recycling by directing staff to consider replacing public recycling bins with litter-only bins, encouraging residents to take recyclables home. This change aims to reduce litter and improve recycling effectiveness, with a new strategy for public spaces due in 2027 that could include targeted high-tech bins and incentive programs. Additionally, the Council is urging the provincial government to reinstate public space recycling obligations and require retailers to manage packaging waste.

title: Fighting for Ontario Place and Ontario's Democracy - by Councillor Josh Matlow, seconded by Deputy Mayor Ausma Malik
Fighting for Ontario Place and Ontario's Democracy - by Councillor Josh Matlow, seconded by Deputy Mayor Ausma Malik
summary: City Council decided to seek permission to get involved in a Supreme Court of Canada appeal concerning Ontario Place. This action allows the city to join a legal challenge to protect the public's interest in the future of the iconic waterfront site.

title: Protecting Chinatown as a Cultural District - by Councillor Dianne Saxe, seconded by Deputy Mayor Ausma Malik
Protecting Chinatown as a Cultural District - by Councillor Dianne Saxe, seconded by Deputy Mayor Ausma Malik
summary: Toronto City Council officially recognized West Chinatown and the remaining strip of First Chinatown as a Cultural District, emphasizing its cultural importance. The City requested the provincial government to introduce commercial rent control and give local businesses and current tenants a first right to rent or purchase properties. These measures aim to protect the area's unique character and support its small businesses and residents.

title: Commemorative Naming Process for Future Toronto Ferries - by Deputy Mayor Ausma Malik, seconded by Councillor Paula Fletcher
Commemorative Naming Process for Future Toronto Ferries - by Deputy Mayor Ausma Malik, seconded by Councillor Paula Fletcher
summary: Toronto City Council has approved a plan to develop a process for naming future Toronto Island ferries. This means a report will be prepared by late 2027 outlining how new ferries will get their names, ensuring public input and consistency with existing city naming policies.

title: Authorization to Release Section 37 Funds to Commemorate the Bathhouse Raids - by Councillor Chris Moise, seconded by Mayor Olivia Chow
summary: Toronto City Council has approved $10,000 to fund a new heritage plaque commemorating the historic Bathhouse raids. This plaque, paid for by community contributions from a local development, will be installed at 231 Mutual Street to recognize an important event in the city's LGBTQ+ history.

title: No ICE in Toronto - by Mayor Olivia Chow, seconded by Councillor Paul Ainslie
No ICE in Toronto - by Mayor Olivia Chow, seconded by Councillor Paul Ainslie
summary: Toronto City Council has officially opposed any involvement of U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) in the city, especially during the 2026 FIFA World Cup. This decision directs city staff to not assist ICE and asks federal and provincial governments to also reject the agency's presence in Toronto.

title: Renewal of Various Boat Club Leases
summary: Toronto City Council has approved new leases for various boat clubs situated along the city's waterfront. These renewed agreements will require the clubs to offer more public benefits, including greater community access to programs, affordable participation rates, or economical storage options for non-members. This initiative aims to make the waterfront more accessible for all Toronto residents.

title: Ravine Strategy 2026 Implementation Update
summary: Toronto City Council has adopted an update to its Ravine Strategy, expanding efforts to protect and enhance the city's natural spaces. This includes permanently establishing a program for community-led restoration projects and allowing the city to accept donations for capital improvements to the Loop Trail. Council also approved joining 'Cities With Nature,' an international initiative focused on urban nature.

title: Tree By-law Review Report
summary: City Council has endorsed a future reduction in the minimum size of trees protected on private property, changing from 30 cm to 20 cm in 2027, which could make it easier for residents to remove smaller trees. Alongside this, a pilot program will offer financial incentives to property owners for maintaining "distinctive" trees. These decisions also include updates to Toronto's tree and ravine protection by-laws.

title: Standing in Solidarity with Iranian-Canadians - Declaring Persian Heritage Month at the City of Toronto - by Councillor Lily Cheng, seconded by Mayor Olivia Chow
summary: Toronto City Council officially declared March 2026 as Persian Heritage Month, recognizing the cultural contributions of Iranian-Canadians and expressing solidarity with the community. To further acknowledge their pursuit of freedom and human dignity, the Toronto sign in Nathan Phillips Square will be lit in green, white, and red during April.

title: 895 Lawrence Avenue East - Ontario Land Tribunal Hearing - Request for Directions
summary: Toronto City Council has agreed to a settlement offer for a development proposal at 895 Lawrence Avenue East, directing the City Solicitor to support it at the Ontario Land Tribunal. This decision sets out the city's position on the project and requires the developer to cover all costs for necessary engineering reports before the Tribunal issues its final order.

title: 1684, 1698, 1700 and 1702 Queen Street East - Official Plan and Zoning By-law Amendment - Decision Report - Approval
summary: Toronto City Council has approved changes to the Official Plan and Zoning By-law for properties at 1684, 1698, 1700, and 1702 Queen Street East. This decision allows a new development to proceed, with the requirement that the owner include three affordable rental housing units as part of the project.

title: Review of Applicability of Type G Loading to Address Specialized Housing Types - by Councillor Josh Matlow, seconded by Councillor Chris Moise
summary: City Council has directed city staff to review existing rules for loading spaces, which are used for deliveries and waste collection, specifically for housing projects that include a mix of different living arrangements. This initiative aims to ensure these rules are appropriate for diverse housing types, potentially supporting the development of more varied housing options across Toronto.

title: Addressing Urgent Parking Capacity Gap at 175, 185 and 205 Cummer Avenue (Cummer Lodge, Willowdale Manor, and Seniors Supportive Housing) - by Councillor Lily Cheng, seconded by Councillor Vincent Crisanti
summary: Toronto City Council is addressing an urgent parking shortage at three seniors' living facilities on Cummer Avenue. Council has directed city staff to conduct a detailed study of current and future parking needs, including public transit options, and to explore solutions like better enforcement and adding more parking capacity. This move aims to improve accessibility for residents, visitors, and staff at Cummer Lodge, Willowdale Manor, and Seniors Supportive Housing.

title: Safer Subways: Making Public Safety a Priority and Restoring Confidence to Toronto Transit Commission Riders Across Toronto - by Councillor Brad Bradford, seconded by Councillor James Pasternak
Safer Subways: Making Public Safety a Priority and Restoring Confidence to Toronto Transit Commission Riders Across Toronto - by Councillor Brad Bradford, seconded by Councillor James Pasternak
summary: To boost safety and confidence for TTC subway riders, City Council has approved a plan to develop a strategy for deploying uniformed police officers in all stations. The plan also commits to installing platform edge doors, with a multi-station pilot project beginning by late 2027, and calls for improved cleanliness standards, updated lighting, and safety design audits across the subway system.

title: Toronto Must Have a Say in Waterfront Decisions - by Deputy Mayor Ausma Malik, seconded by Mayor Olivia Chow
Toronto Must Have a Say in Waterfront Decisions - by Deputy Mayor Ausma Malik, seconded by Mayor Olivia Chow
Toronto Must Have a Say in Waterfront Decisions - by Deputy Mayor Ausma Malik, seconded by Mayor Olivia Chow
summary: Toronto City Council has formally requested the provincial and federal governments to include local residents and their elected officials in all major decisions about the city's waterfront. This decision opposes any unilateral government land seizures and directs city lawyers to explore legal options to protect Toronto's ownership of critical waterfront properties, including the Billy Bishop Airport lands.

title: Report on Councillor Chris Moise's Comments to a Constituent
summary: Toronto City Council recently reviewed a report from the Integrity Commissioner concerning comments made by Councillor Chris Moise to a constituent. To promote professional conduct and responsible communication, Council also requested that the Integrity Commissioner provide guidance to all councillors on how to properly respond to hateful, discriminatory, or harassing speech.

title: 444 to 468 Eglinton Avenue West - Official Plan and Zoning By-law Amendment Application - Ontario Land Tribunal Hearing - Request for Directions
summary: City Council has agreed to a settlement offer regarding a proposed development at 444-468 Eglinton Avenue West, directing the City Solicitor to support this plan at the Ontario Land Tribunal. This decision will shape the future of this site, allowing for new construction once the developer meets specific conditions related to design, engineering, and transportation.

title: Cracking Down on Bad Landlords
Cracking Down on Bad Landlords
summary: Toronto City Council is stepping up efforts to hold negligent landlords accountable. They've approved creating a public database to track rental property complaints and will coordinate city divisions to proactively investigate all properties owned by repeat offenders. This initiative aims to improve living conditions and ensure proper maintenance for renters across the city.

title: Toronto Wins Bloomberg Philanthropies 2025 - 2026 Mayors Challenge
summary: Toronto City Council has approved accepting a grant of over $1.6 million from Bloomberg Philanthropies. This funding will be used to develop and implement a universal school food program, aiming to provide nutritious meals for students across the city.

title: Winter Surge Capacity Snow Shovellers
summary: Toronto City Council has approved creating a new paid program for sidewalk snow shovelling. This program will bring in extra help during heavy winter snowfalls, drawing on successful models from cities like New York and Montreal, to ensure sidewalks are cleared for residents by the 2026-2027 winter season.

title: Addressing Evictions through Disaggregated Race-Based Data in Social Housing
summary: City Council has adopted recommendations to address the rising number of evictions from Toronto Community Housing. This includes requesting TCHC to consider a temporary halt on evictions for rent arrears until a comprehensive plan is developed, and to create new pathways to rehouse and support tenants who have been evicted, with a focus on Black, Indigenous, and racialized residents. The City also declared an escalating housing crisis, recognizing the critical role of social housing for many residents.

title: Award of Request for Proposal Doc5386487782 to ClaimsPro LP for the Provision of Insurance Adjusting Services
summary: City officials have approved a five-year contract with ClaimsPro LP to provide insurance adjusting services for the City of Toronto. This agreement ensures the city can effectively manage its own insurance claims, helping to assess liabilities and protect public funds.

title: 2256 Lake Shore Boulevard West - Official Plan Amendment and Zoning By-law Amendment Application - Decision Report - Approval
summary: City Council has approved planning rule changes for 2256 Lake Shore Boulevard West, allowing for a new development in the area. This approval requires the developer to provide a new public park on the site, measuring at least 556 square metres.

title: 2125 Lawrence Avenue West - Inclusion on the Heritage Register
summary: Toronto City Council has decided to add the property at 2125 Lawrence Avenue West to the city's Heritage Register. This means the building is now officially recognized for its historical or architectural value, providing a layer of protection against demolition or major alterations and helping preserve local heritage.

title: 2026 Operating and Capital Budget
summary: Toronto City Council has formally adopted the city's 2026 Operating and Capital Budget. This decision sets out how the city will fund all its services and investments for the upcoming year, including minor adjustments to support local economic development, cultural events, and community initiatives.

title: Budget Implementation Including Property Tax Rates, User Fees and Related Matters
Budget Implementation Including Property Tax Rates, User Fees and Related Matters
Budget Implementation Including Property Tax Rates, User Fees and Related Matters
Budget Implementation Including Property Tax Rates, User Fees and Related Matters
summary: Toronto City Council has approved the property tax rates and overall budget implementation for 2026, setting new tax ratios for different property types. Homeowners will see a 0.7% increase in their property tax bills, while a new special levy will collect nearly $75 million dedicated to fund priority transit and housing projects across the city.

title: Blue Bin Litter
summary: City Council received an inquiry and a response about the issue of litter found in blue recycling bins across the city. After reviewing the information, Council decided not to take any further action on the matter at this time, meaning specific initiatives to address blue bin litter are not moving forward.

title: Visitor Parking Requirements for New Development
summary: Toronto City Council has directed city staff to review and study various parking requirements for new developments across the city. This includes examining visitor parking and accessible parking needs, especially in areas near major transit, along with loading zones and the feasibility of expanding city-owned parking lots to support growing neighborhoods.

title: Scarborough Centre Secondary Plan - Final Report
summary: Toronto City Council has approved an updated long-term plan for the Scarborough Centre area, known as Official Plan Amendment 871. This plan will guide how the area grows and develops in the coming years, shaping future decisions on housing, businesses, and public spaces to create a vibrant urban hub.

title: 353 to 355 Sherbourne Street and 157 Carlton Street - Alterations to a Designated Heritage Property under Section 42 of the Ontario Heritage Act and Authority to Enter into a Heritage Easement Agreement
summary: City Council has approved alterations to the historic buildings at 353-355 Sherbourne Street and 157 Carlton Street. This decision allows for the construction of a new 48-storey residential tower and a 4-storey building with shops and homes, while also ensuring the heritage features of the existing property are protected through a legal agreement.

title: 353-355 Sherbourne Street and 157 Carlton Street - Official Plan and Zoning By-law Amendment Application - Decision Report - Approval
summary: City Council has approved zoning and planning changes for properties at 353-355 Sherbourne Street and 157 Carlton Street. This decision permits a new development on these sites, shaping what can be built and how it impacts the surrounding neighbourhood, from housing to local density.

title: Toronto Builds - 1631 and 1641 Queen Street East, and 1080 (Portion) and 1090 Eastern Avenue - Official Plan and Zoning By-law Amendment - Decision Report - Approval
summary: Toronto City Council has approved changes to the planning rules for properties on Queen Street East and Eastern Avenue. This decision will allow for the development of new housing as part of the Toronto Builds program, and will also explore integrating community spaces and adjusting local permit parking.

title: Preserving Employment Lands and Protecting Local Jobs
summary: To protect Toronto's economy and job market, City Council reaffirmed its request to the provincial government to approve Official Plan Amendment 804, a key plan for preserving vital industrial and commercial areas. City staff will also work with businesses, labour groups, and other partners to develop a shared strategy across governments to protect these lands, boost productivity, and grow manufacturing jobs. This decision aims to secure dedicated spaces for businesses and ensure local employment opportunities.

title: 1212 to 1238 Kingston Road - Zoning By-law Amendment - Decision Report - Approval
summary: City Council has approved zoning changes for the properties at 1212-1238 Kingston Road, allowing for a new development in the area. The decision also requests that funds collected from the developer be used for local park improvements in Ward 20. Furthermore, this new development will not be eligible for on-street permit parking if it's introduced on nearby streets in the future.

title: Ontario Line - Construction Update - Fourth Quarter 2025
summary: Toronto City Council has directed its transit office to press Metrolinx for greater transparency and mitigation efforts related to Ontario Line construction. Council is requesting Metrolinx to immediately publish its community benefits report, re-evaluate an acoustic shelter for Queen/Spadina Station, and work with the City to reduce construction impacts near Exhibition Station during the 2026 FIFA World Cup. These actions aim to ensure Metrolinx better addresses noise, local business support, and community engagement.

title: 2026 Capital and Operating Budgets
summary: Toronto's Budget Committee has made recommendations to the Mayor for the upcoming 2026 City Budgets, focusing on strategic investments and future financial planning. This includes exploring opportunities for increased community safety, advocating for youth violence prevention, and supporting a plan to boost property tax discounts for small businesses from 15% to 20%, funded by an adjustment to other commercial property taxes without impacting overall city revenue.

title: 10 Glencairn Avenue - Application to Remove a Private Tree
summary: After an appeal, North York Community Council allowed a homeowner at 10 Glencairn Avenue to remove a privately owned tree, despite city forestry staff initially deeming the tree healthy. To maintain Toronto's tree canopy, the resident must provide five replacement trees, either by planting them or by paying a fee in place of planting.

title: Making life more affordable for families by asking luxury-home buyers to chip in more
Making life more affordable for families by asking luxury-home buyers to chip in more
Making life more affordable for families by asking luxury-home buyers to chip in more
Making life more affordable for families by asking luxury-home buyers to chip in more
summary: Toronto City Council has approved increasing the land transfer tax for luxury homes, with new, higher rates for properties valued over $3 million. This change aims to generate more revenue for the city, potentially helping to fund services like transit and housing, and reduce the city's financial shortfall, with the revised rates taking effect on April 1, 2026.

title: Securing More Good Jobs in Toronto: Support for Hosting the Defence, Security and Resilience Bank Headquarters - by Councillor Brad Bradford, seconded by Councillor James Pasternak
Securing More Good Jobs in Toronto: Support for Hosting the Defence, Security and Resilience Bank Headquarters - by Councillor Brad Bradford, seconded by Councillor James Pasternak
summary: Toronto City Council has formally expressed its support for the new Defence, Security and Resilience Bank to establish its headquarters in the city. This move is intended to strengthen Toronto's economy and create thousands of good jobs for residents. The Council will now communicate this position to federal and provincial government leaders.

title: 199 College Street Community Benefits Charge, College Streetscape - by Councillor Dianne Saxe, seconded by Councillor Jamaal Myers
summary: City Council sent a proposal about developer contributions for the 199 College Street development and related streetscape improvements to a local community council for further review. This means the plan to collect funds for local services and enhance the College Street area will be discussed locally before potentially returning to City Council.

title: Showing Respect for Transit Customers: Implementing a Toronto Transit Commission Money-Back Guarantee - by Councillor Brad Bradford, seconded by Councillor Vincent Crisanti
Showing Respect for Transit Customers: Implementing a Toronto Transit Commission Money-Back Guarantee - by Councillor Brad Bradford, seconded by Councillor Vincent Crisanti
Showing Respect for Transit Customers: Implementing a Toronto Transit Commission Money-Back Guarantee - by Councillor Brad Bradford, seconded by Councillor Vincent Crisanti
Showing Respect for Transit Customers: Implementing a Toronto Transit Commission Money-Back Guarantee - by Councillor Brad Bradford, seconded by Councillor Vincent Crisanti
summary: Toronto City Council has asked the TTC board to study the possibility of implementing a money-back guarantee for transit riders. If approved, customers could receive refunds for TTC delays of 15 minutes or more, which aims to improve accountability and rider experience.

title: TransformTO Net Zero Strategy: Action Plan (2026-2030)
TransformTO Net Zero Strategy: Action Plan (2026-2030)
TransformTO Net Zero Strategy: Action Plan (2026-2030)
summary: Toronto has adopted its TransformTO Net Zero Strategy Action Plan for 2026-2030, setting out how the city will work towards its ambitious climate goals. This plan directs staff to develop a detailed financial analysis and policy recommendations by 2028, outlining the estimated funding needed to reach net zero greenhouse gas emissions by 2040 and shaping future city investments.

title: Creating More Accessible and Disability Inclusive Events
summary: Toronto City Council has approved a new initiative to make events across the city more accessible for people with disabilities. City staff will develop an "Accessibility and Inclusion Handbook" to guide event organizers, ensuring events held on city property and elsewhere are welcoming to all residents.

title: Expanding the Diversity Youth Fellowship Program
summary: Toronto City Council recently approved a plan to explore creating a part-time fellowship program for youth living with disabilities. This initiative aims to provide valuable work experience within City Hall offices, and city staff will now prepare a report outlining how best to implement the program.

title: Accepting a Donation and Entering into Agreements with the Chinese Cultural Centre of Greater Toronto for an Asian Garden Project at Cynthia Lai Park
summary: City Council has approved accepting an in-kind donation of up to $15 million from the Chinese Cultural Centre of Greater Toronto to build a new Asian Garden at Cynthia Lai Park. This decision allows for the creation and long-term management of a significant cultural green space, enhancing local park amenities for residents.

title: Housing Action Plan: Avenues Policy Review Phase Two: Initial Study of Wards 9 and 11
summary: Toronto City Council has approved changes to planning rules for major roads (called "Avenues") in Wards 9 and 11, especially near transit stations. These updates to the city's Official Plan and zoning bylaws aim to encourage more diverse housing development and growth along these important corridors.

title: Permanent Closure of a Portion of the Public Laneway at 66-66Y Third Street
Permanent Closure of a Portion of the Public Laneway at 66-66Y Third Street
summary: City Council has approved the permanent closure of a portion of the public laneway located at 66-66Y Third Street in the Etobicoke-Lakeshore area. This decision means that part of the laneway will no longer be accessible to the public, potentially affecting local property access or pedestrian routes for nearby residents.

title: 847-855 Kingston Road - Ontario Land Tribunal Hearing - Request for Directions
summary: Toronto City Council has approved a settlement for the development proposal at 847-855 Kingston Road, directing City staff to support the revised plans at the Ontario Land Tribunal. This decision also requires staff to consult with local Indigenous communities regarding the project and the nearby Glen Stewart ravine.

title: 847-855 Kingston Road - Ontario Land Tribunal Hearing - Request for Directions
444 Logan Avenue - Making Quality of Life and Environmental Improvements for Seniors
summary: City Council has approved an agreement with WoodGreen Community Housing to support 160 affordable homes for seniors at 444 Logan Avenue. This agreement includes a 40-year property tax exemption for the project, helping ensure quality housing for seniors in the community.

title: Bill 60, Fighting Delays, Building Faster Act, 2025, and Renter Protections - Legal Advice
Bill 60, Fighting Delays, Building Faster Act, 2025, and Renter Protections - Legal Advice
summary: Toronto City Council is preparing for a provincial law called Bill 60, the "Fighting Delays, Building Faster Act, 2025." Council decided to let its legal team join any public lawsuits challenging this bill to protect the city's interests, especially regarding housing security and preventing homelessness.

title: Moving Forward with Transform Yonge: Visionary Public Realm and Cycling Gains, Without Losing a Lane - We Want it All! - by Councillor Lily Cheng, seconded by Mayor Olivia Chow
summary: City Council has directed city staff to study new design options for the 'Transform Yonge' project. They want to explore ways to improve public spaces and add bike lanes on Yonge Street without removing any existing car lanes, and also look into extending the plan further north to Steeles Avenue. This decision aims to find a compromise that benefits pedestrians, cyclists, and drivers, with a report expected by early 2027.

title: Safety on all City Sidewalks; E-bikes Enforcement - by Councillor Jon Burnside, seconded by Councillor Rachel Chernos Lin
summary: City Council has asked city staff to explore ways to enforce the rules prohibiting e-bikes and e-scooters from being ridden on sidewalks. This action aims to improve safety for pedestrians and will also include recommendations on how to better inform the public about existing rules for these devices.

title: Advancing the West Scarborough Rail Trail - Connecting Scarborough, Enhancing Mobility Options - by Councillor Parthi Kandavel, seconded by Councillor Jamaal Myers
summary: Toronto councillors have approved a plan to advance a new West Scarborough Rail Trail, initiating a study for its design, property needs, and preliminary cost estimates. This future multi-use path would improve walking and cycling connections for residents and may be included in the city's cycling network plan for 2028-2030, pending future funding.

title: FIFA Traffic Management
summary: Toronto City Council has asked its Executive Committee to look into how traffic will be managed during the upcoming FIFA World Cup. This item, originally an inquiry from a Councillor, addresses a crucial issue that will affect how residents travel and get around the city during the major event.

title: Seeking Clarity on the City of Toronto's Application to the Community Sport and Recreation Infrastructure Fund
summary: Toronto City Council has sent a request for more information about the city's application for funding from the Community Sport and Recreation Infrastructure Fund to its Infrastructure and Environment Committee. This means the committee will now review how the city is seeking grants to improve local sports and recreation facilities.

title: Speeding Up Light Rail Transit and Streetcars - by Mayor Olivia Chow, seconded by Councillor Jamaal Myers
Speeding Up Light Rail Transit and Streetcars - by Mayor Olivia Chow, seconded by Councillor Jamaal Myers
summary: To make public transit faster and more reliable, City Council has approved measures to speed up light rail transit and streetcars across Toronto. This includes immediately implementing more aggressive traffic signal priority for the Eglinton and Finch West LRTs, and developing detailed plans by early 2026 to improve streetcar speeds through signal adjustments, traffic agents, and potentially parking or turn restrictions.

title: Review of the Order Paper
summary: City Council formally approved the list of items they would discuss during their December 16 and 17, 2025 meetings. This process of reviewing and adopting the "Order Paper" establishes the agenda and priorities for Council's upcoming decisions.

title: Towards Implementing a Maximum Indoor Temperature Requirement for Rental Units and Cooling Rooms
Towards Implementing a Maximum Indoor Temperature Requirement for Rental Units and Cooling Rooms
summary: Toronto City Council has approved new rules requiring apartment buildings to provide a common cooled space, kept at or below 26 degrees Celsius, from June 1 to September 30 each year. This applies to buildings with existing amenity spaces where individual units lack air conditioning, helping tenants stay safe during heatwaves. City staff will also develop a proposal for maximum temperature limits within rental units themselves.

title: Strengthening the City's Heat Relief Strategy
summary: Toronto City Council has approved a plan to update and improve the city's approach to extreme heat. This includes finalizing the 2026 Heat Relief Strategy, making cooling centres more accessible and better promoted, and improving the application process for hardship funds to help vulnerable residents. These changes aim to better protect Toronto residents during hot weather.

title: Reimbursing Certain Reasonable Legal Expenses of Shelter Consultants - by Councillor Paula Fletcher, seconded by Councillor James Pasternak
summary: Toronto City Council has decided to take a firm stance against lawsuits that aim to halt new housing and shelter developments. To support this, the city will offer reimbursements of up to $50,000 to consultants involved in these cases to cover their legal fees not paid by insurance, helping advance critical projects.

title: 4050 Chesswood Drive - Designation of a Portion of the Property Used by North York Harvest Food Bank Toronto as a Municipal Capital Facility
summary: City Council has approved a plan to support the North York Harvest Food Bank, recognizing a portion of its Chesswood Drive location as a key community facility. This decision exempts the food bank from paying property taxes, helping it focus more resources on providing vital social and health services to residents. The City will also explore providing a loan guarantee to help finance upgrades to the food bank's distribution facility.

title: Motion without Notice - Further Recommendations to Speed Up Streetcars - by Chair Jamaal Myers, seconded by Commissioner Josh Matlow
summary: The TTC has approved exploring new strategies to speed up streetcar service across Toronto. This initiative will look at options like removing on-street parking, restricting left turns during peak hours, and reviewing stop spacing and internal speed policies to make commutes faster and more reliable for riders.

title: Motion without Notice - An Urgent Need to Speed Up New LRT Routes - by Chair Jamaal Myers, seconded by Vice-Chair Joe Mihevc
summary: Toronto's transit agency has directed its CEO to quickly improve the speed and reliability of the new Line 6 (Finch West LRT) and the upcoming Line 5 (Eglinton Crosstown LRT). This aims to make these crucial new light rail lines more appealing than private vehicles, reducing traffic congestion and better serving the thousands of residents who depend on them.

title: Electricity and Toronto's Climate Commitments
summary: The Infrastructure and Environment Committee has referred a proposal to an executive director for consideration regarding Toronto's electricity supply. The proposal argues that the provincial electricity plan for Toronto does not align with the city's climate goals, which include phasing out gas-fired power and increasing local renewable energy to achieve net-zero emissions by 2040.

title: Tenancy Update with Beech Hall Housing Co-operative and Alexandra Park Housing Co-operative
summary: Toronto City Council has approved a two-year extension for the lease agreements of Beech Hall Housing Co-operative and Alexandra Park Housing Co-operative. This decision allows these vital affordable housing providers to continue operating under their current terms while new, long-term agreements are finalized with the City.

title: Mount Dennis Community For All Action Plan
summary: Toronto City Council adopted the Mount Dennis Community for All Action Plan, a strategy to guide development and support residents in the Mount Dennis area over the next 10 years. This plan focuses on community well-being, addressing potential displacement, and includes initiatives like a grant program for anti-displacement education.

title: Toronto Zoo Collective Bargaining
Toronto Zoo Collective Bargaining
summary: The Toronto Zoo Board of Management has adopted confidential instructions for its upcoming collective bargaining negotiations with the union representing Zoo employees. These negotiations will determine future wages, benefits, and working conditions for staff, impacting the Zoo's operational costs and services.

title: 2026 Budget Allocation for Social Development Plan Implementation
summary: A city committee has recommended that the Mayor consider allocating dedicated funding in the 2026 budget for Social Development Plans across Toronto's priority neighbourhoods. These plans aim to boost safety, well-being, and community resilience, particularly benefiting Black, Indigenous, and racialized residents through local initiatives.

title: Application for Approval to Expropriate a Portion of 20 Humberview Crescent and 50 St. Phillips Road - Stage 1
Application for Approval to Expropriate a Portion of 20 Humberview Crescent and 50 St. Phillips Road - Stage 1
summary: To complete an important gap in the Humber River Trail, City Council has authorized continued negotiations to acquire portions of two properties on Humberview Crescent and St. Phillips Road. If negotiations are unsuccessful, the City will begin the process to acquire this private land for public use, allowing for the construction of a new multi-use path, bridges, and a boardwalk.

title: Respecting Local Authority Over Road Design - by Councillor Alejandra Bravo, seconded by Dianne Saxe
summary: Toronto City Council has formally opposed a new provincial bill (Bill 60) that could limit the city's authority over local road design. Council is asking the Province to confirm that Toronto retains the power to make decisions on road layouts, such as bike lanes and traffic flow, to best serve residents' interests and improve safety.

title: Call to Order
2 Tippett Road - Ontario Land Tribunal Hearing - Request for Directions
summary: City Council has approved a settlement regarding a development project at 2 Tippett Road, directing staff to support this agreement at the Ontario Land Tribunal. This decision sets the conditions for the development to proceed, requiring the owner to finalize design plans and address various engineering and environmental concerns before construction can begin.

title: Protecting Playgrounds and Parks: Ensuring Safety for Children and Families - by Councillor Brad Bradford, seconded by Councillor Jon Burnside
Protecting Playgrounds and Parks: Ensuring Safety for Children and Families - by Councillor Brad Bradford, seconded by Councillor Jon Burnside
Protecting Playgrounds and Parks: Ensuring Safety for Children and Families - by Councillor Brad Bradford, seconded by Councillor Jon Burnside
Protecting Playgrounds and Parks: Ensuring Safety for Children and Families - by Councillor Brad Bradford, seconded by Councillor Jon Burnside
Protecting Playgrounds and Parks: Ensuring Safety for Children and Families - by Councillor Brad Bradford, seconded by Councillor Jon Burnside
summary: To enhance safety in public spaces, City Council updated its rules for managing homeless encampments. The new policy prioritizes removing encampments within 200 metres of schools, daycares, or playgrounds, aiming for removal within 24 hours of being reported. It also limits shelter offers to three before proceeding with removal in these specific areas, intending to ensure these spaces are safe for children and families.

title: Comments on the Request for a Minister's Zoning Order to Restrict Building Heights - 1875 Steeles Avenue West to Protect Operational Viability of the Sanofi Pasteur Limited Manufacturing Facilities and Headquarters - by Councillor James Pasternak, seconded by Councillor Rachel Chernos Lin

Comments on the Request for a Minister's Zoning Order to Restrict Building Heights - 1875 Steeles Avenue West to Protect Operational Viability of the Sanofi Pasteur Limited Manufacturing Facilities and Headquarters - by Councillor James Pasternak, seconded by Councillor Rachel Chernos Lin
summary: City Council has approved a request to the province for a special zoning order that would limit building heights at 1875 Steeles Avenue West. This decision aims to protect the operations of Sanofi Pasteur's biomanufacturing facilities and headquarters nearby, ensuring a major local employer can continue to operate effectively.

title: 712 Eglinton Avenue East - Request for City Solicitor to Attend at the Toronto Local Appeal Body - by Councilor Rachel Chernos Lin, seconded by Councillor Lily Cheng
summary: Toronto City Council has decided to challenge a development approval for 712 Eglinton Avenue East. The City's legal team will attend the Toronto Local Appeal Body to oppose the proposed development, seeking to prevent or change it. This ensures the city's position on local development is heard and considered.

title: Community Within Reach: Expanding Housing Options in Neighbourhoods (EHON) Neighbourhood Retail and Services Study - Phase Three Final Report
Community Within Reach: Expanding Housing Options in Neighbourhoods (EHON) Neighbourhood Retail and Services Study - Phase Three Final Report
Community Within Reach: Expanding Housing Options in Neighbourhoods (EHON) Neighbourhood Retail and Services Study - Phase Three Final Report
Community Within Reach: Expanding Housing Options in Neighbourhoods (EHON) Neighbourhood Retail and Services Study - Phase Three Final Report
summary: Toronto City Council has updated zoning rules to allow more local retail shops and services on major streets within residential neighbourhoods. This change aims to make daily errands more convenient for residents and create more complete communities.

title: Metrolinx Ontario Line - Cosburn Station, Bain Emergency Exit Building and Gerrard Portal - Temporary Road Closures and Transportation Impacts
summary: To facilitate the construction of the Metrolinx Ontario Line's Cosburn Station, City Council approved multi-year temporary closures of a local laneway, parts of Pape Avenue's roadway and sidewalk, and changes to parking rules in the area. These significant disruptions will affect traffic, pedestrians, and parking along Pape Avenue from early 2026 until the end of 2030.

title: Automated Speed Enforcement Program Update
summary: Toronto City Council has asked the Province of Ontario to provide funding for critical road safety initiatives. This includes $35.1 million to maintain crossing guards and traffic safety police, and $210 million for road safety infrastructure projects like traffic calming and lights, ensuring these vital programs continue.

title: Following Up on Plans to Advance the Extension of the Finch West Light Rail Transit  - by Councillor Vincent Crisanti, seconded by Councillor Stephen Holyday
summary: City Council has requested updates from the Province of Ontario and Metrolinx on plans to extend the Finch West Light Rail Transit line further west to Woodbine and Pearson International Airport. This decision aims to ensure that the extension aligns with City Council's vision and that necessary planning is underway, including ensuring emergency access for police services along the route.

title: Driving Efficiencies at Toronto Parking Authority to Reduce 2026 Budget Pressures - by Mayor Olivia Chow, seconded by Councillor Frances Nunziata
Driving Efficiencies at Toronto Parking Authority to Reduce 2026 Budget Pressures - by Mayor Olivia Chow, seconded by Councillor Frances Nunziata
Driving Efficiencies at Toronto Parking Authority to Reduce 2026 Budget Pressures - by Mayor Olivia Chow, seconded by Councillor Frances Nunziata
summary: To find efficiencies and reduce budget pressures, City Council has restructured the Toronto Parking Authority's Board to be made up entirely of senior City officials. The City Manager will now conduct a full review of the Authority's operations, finances, and staffing to explore potentially integrating it more directly into the City or another agency. This aims to optimize how parking services are run and contribute to the 2026 city budget.

title: Soft Drink Sponsorship Agreement - Coca-Cola Canada
summary: City Council approved a five-year agreement making Coca-Cola Canada the exclusive soft drink provider for Exhibition Place, including the Enercare Centre and Automotive Building. This means visitors to these major Toronto venues will primarily find Coca-Cola products. Council also requested Exhibition Place to continue pursuing enhanced sustainability practices across all its food and beverage operations.

title: Proposed Naming of the North West Baseball Diamond at Martingrove Gardens Park to Jim Horton Baseball Diamond
summary: City Council has approved naming the north west baseball diamond at Martingrove Gardens Park the “Jim Horton Baseball Diamond”. This decision honors Jim Horton, a dedicated long-time volunteer coach recognized for his significant contributions to Martingrove Baseball since 1987.

title: Clonmore Drive and Queensbury Avenue/Private Access - Traffic Control Signals
summary: To improve safety and traffic flow, Toronto has approved installing new traffic lights at the intersection of Clonmore Drive and Queensbury Avenue/Private Access. This decision will also remove the existing pedestrian crossing nearby, integrating pedestrian safety into the new signalized intersection for residents in Scarborough Southwest.

title: Birchmount Road and Rolark Drive/Private Access - Traffic Control Signals and Traffic Regulations
summary: Toronto City Council has approved installing new traffic lights at Birchmount Road and Rolark Drive, also banning right turns on a red light at this intersection. These changes aim to improve safety and traffic flow for drivers and pedestrians in the area, alongside removing a specific southbound right-turn lane on Birchmount Road.

title: Results of Collective Bargaining Negotiations between the City of Toronto and Canadian Union of Public Employees Local 2998
summary: Toronto City Council has approved a new labour agreement with CUPE Local 2998, a union representing some city employees. This decision means changes to employee pay and benefits, which will affect the city's operating budget for 2025 and beyond.

title: Impacts of Provincial Legislation that Weakens Rental Protections
Impacts of Provincial Legislation that Weakens Rental Protections
summary: Toronto City Council has formally opposed recent provincial legislation (Bill 60) that aims to weaken protections for renters, including security of tenure and rent control. The city is requesting Ontario reinstate rent control for newer buildings and provide $596 million to help build more rental housing, while also exploring legal challenges to the new provincial rules.

title: Understanding the Impacts of Bill 60 on Health and Well-Being in Toronto
summary: Toronto City Council has formally expressed its opposition to the provincial Bill 60, also known as the Fighting Delays, Building Faster Act. The City believes that proposed changes to tenant security and rent control within the bill could undermine stable housing, which is crucial for residents' overall health and well-being.

title: Understanding the Impacts of Bill 60 on Homelessness in Toronto
summary: Toronto City Council is concerned about how the provincial Bill 60 might impact housing stability and homelessness. Council has asked the Association of Municipalities of Ontario to organize a province-wide research effort to forecast the potential increase in demand for shelter and housing services due to this legislation. This action aims to help the city better understand and prepare for future challenges related to housing insecurity.

title: Respecting Local Authority Over Road Design - by Councillor Alejandra Bravo, seconded by Dianne Saxe
2025 Winter Maintenance Program Review Update
summary: Toronto City Council has approved changes to its winter maintenance program, which include significantly increasing fines for parking on or blocking streetcar tracks during snowstorms. The city will also work on improving public access to real-time snow clearing updates and modernizing its PlowTO tracking map, while exploring requirements for corporate property owners to clear their sidewalks.

title: Call to Order
summary: City Council formally opened its November 2025 meeting, marking the beginning of its deliberations. During the opening, members observed moments of silence for several individuals and delivered a land acknowledgement.

title: Reimagining Weston Lions Arena through a Nominal Lease with MLSE LaunchPad
Reimagining Weston Lions Arena through a Nominal Lease with MLSE LaunchPad
Reimagining Weston Lions Arena through a Nominal Lease with MLSE LaunchPad
summary: Toronto City Council has approved a long-term lease agreement for the Weston Lions Arena with the Youth Centre for Sports Development (MLSE LaunchPad), allowing the organization to redevelop and operate the facility. This decision aims to revitalize the arena with new design and programming, particularly for youth sports, while ensuring a smooth transition for current users like the Weston Lions Club and exploring options for additional ice facilities in the area.

title: St. Clair-Old Weston Station and 2025 GO Expansion Program Updates
St. Clair-Old Weston Station and 2025 GO Expansion Program Updates
St. Clair-Old Weston Station and 2025 GO Expansion Program Updates
summary: Toronto City Council has approved a plan for the new St. Clair-Old Weston Station to be served by the Union-Pearson Express (UPX) line, potentially offering faster transit for nearby residents. This approval is conditional on the Province fully covering all capital costs for this change and expanding the One Fare program to include UPX service, ensuring more affordable and integrated fares.

title: 1138 Islington Avenue - Zoning By-law Amendment Application - Decision Report - Approval
summary: Toronto City Council has approved new development rules for the property at 1138 Islington Avenue, paving the way for a new project. The developer is required to ensure and pay for any necessary upgrades to local city services, such as water and drainage, that are needed for the development.

title: 3418 Lake Shore Boulevard West and 0 Skeens Lane - Official Plan Amendment and Zoning By-law Amendment Application - Decision Report - Approval
summary: Toronto City Council has approved changes to the city's long-term development plan and zoning rules for the land at 3418 Lake Shore Boulevard West and 0 Skeens Lane. This decision paves the way for new development or significant alterations to how this property can be used, impacting the surrounding neighbourhood.

title: 5280 Dundas Street West - Zoning By-law Amendment Application - Decision Report - Approval
summary: City Council approved a zoning change for 5280 Dundas Street West, allowing for new development at this location. This decision also sets the fees developers will pay if they choose not to provide the required bicycle parking spaces on-site, with these funds potentially directed towards other cycling infrastructure.

title: Response to City Council Direction on EX.25.4 RapidTO: Transit Priority on Dufferin Street and Bathurst Street
summary: Following the implementation of transit priority lanes (RapidTO) on Dufferin and Bathurst Streets, City Council is directing city staff to address community concerns. This includes developing solutions for traffic calming on local roads, making changes to on-street permit parking, and working with religious institutions to accommodate funeral processions. These actions aim to mitigate the impact of the transit changes on local residents and businesses.

title: Auditor General's Office 2026 Work Plan and Budget Highlights
summary: Toronto's City Council approved the Auditor General's 2026 work plan, outlining planned investigations into city operations. Council also asked the Auditor General to consider adding reviews of the SmartTrack project and TTC Subway Maintenance to the 2026 schedule. This ensures independent oversight of major city services, helping to improve efficiency and accountability for residents.

title: Creating Community Opportunities through the 4610 Finch Avenue East Long-Term Care Home Project
summary: Toronto City Council has approved plans for the new long-term care home at 4610 Finch Avenue East to prioritize specialized care for Black, Caribbean, and African diaspora seniors. This initiative aims to improve access and outcomes for these communities, and staff will also explore how the project can benefit the local Brimley Forest neighbourhood.

title: 2024-2025 Annual Progress Report - HousingTO 2020-2030 Action Plan and Housing Action Plan (2022-2026)
summary: Toronto has requested significant funding from federal and provincial governments to advance its housing plans. These funds are earmarked to create thousands of new affordable and supportive homes, and to protect existing rental units for residents across the city.

keyAmounts.label: Federal funding for new rental homes

title: Application for Approval to Expropriate a Portion of 20 Humberview Crescent and 50 St. Phillips Road - Stage 1
Progress Report on the Toronto Ferry Service
summary: City Council received an informational report detailing the ongoing progress of the Toronto Ferry Service. The update covers the continued fabrication of new electric ferries, improvements to operational maintenance and safety, and plans to enhance the customer experience at the Jack Layton Ferry Terminal. These efforts ensure a reliable and modern ferry service for residents and visitors traveling to the Toronto Islands.

title: 10-Year Circular Economy Road Map
10-Year Circular Economy Road Map
summary: City Council has adopted a 10-year 'Circular Economy Road Map' to help Toronto reduce waste, reuse resources, and become more sustainable. This plan allows city staff to partner with businesses, non-profits, and other governments to implement initiatives and secure funding to achieve these environmental goals.

title: Amendment to Blanket Contract 47025543 with OpenText Corporation for Enterprise Document and Records Management Products and Professional Services Leveraging the Provincial Volume Licensing Agreement
summary: Toronto City Council has extended and significantly increased a contract with OpenText Corporation for enterprise document and records management services. This ensures the city can continue to efficiently store, organize, and access its vast amount of electronic information, which is crucial for city operations and public transparency.

title: Amendment to Purchase Order Number 6048288 with AECOM CANANDA ULC for Professional Engineering Services for the Dufferin Staff Facility Project
summary: A city committee has approved adding over $1.6 million to an existing contract with AECOM CANADA ULC for engineering services on the Dufferin Staff Facility Project. This extra funding is needed to revise designs, meet updated city guidelines, and prepare for construction, bringing the total cost for these services to over $3.6 million.

title: 171 Neville Park Boulevard - Application to Remove a Private Tree
committee: Toronto and East York Community Council
summary: The Toronto and East York Community Council denied a resident's request to remove a large, healthy red oak tree from their private property at 171 Neville Park Boulevard. This decision upholds the city's Tree By-laws, which aim to protect mature and maintainable trees, reflecting the council's commitment to preserving Toronto's urban forest.

title: Strathdee Drive - Traffic Calming (Speed Humps)
summary: Etobicoke York Community Council recently decided not to pause the installation of speed humps on Strathdee Drive. This means the city will proceed with adding traffic calming measures to the street, despite a new petition from some residents asking for a delay due to withdrawn support for the project.

title: Summary of Legal Disputes Update
summary: The Toronto Parking Authority received an update on its ongoing legal disputes, which involve issues like supplier disagreements, personal injury claims, and property tenancy matters related to its parking operations, tenanted properties, and Bike Share Toronto. The Board decided to keep the details of these specific disputes confidential, as they involve sensitive legal advice and potential litigation. This regular update helps the Authority manage legal risks in its operations.

title: Introducing Colour-Coded Signage for RentSafeTO
Introducing Colour-Coded Signage for RentSafeTO
summary: City Council has approved new colour-coded signs for apartment buildings under the RentSafeTO program. Starting June 15, 2026, building owners must display these signs, which will reflect how well the building meets City bylaws and maintenance standards, providing clearer information for current and future tenants. Owners will also be required to inform tenants about their building's rating and include contact information for reporting issues.

title: Expanding Toronto Community Crisis Response to the Toronto Transit Commission
Expanding Toronto Community Crisis Response to the Toronto Transit Commission
Expanding Toronto Community Crisis Response to the Toronto Transit Commission
summary: To enhance safety and support riders, City Council approved expanding the Toronto Community Crisis Response Service to the Toronto Transit Commission (TTC) for a pilot program lasting through 2026. This initiative will provide non-police mental health and crisis support on transit, while also requesting reports on a police deployment strategy for TTC stations.

title: Taking Back Control of Our City Services to Deliver Higher Standards, Lower Costs and Greater Accountability - by Councillor Josh Matlow, seconded by Mayor Olivia Chow
Taking Back Control of Our City Services to Deliver Higher Standards, Lower Costs and Greater Accountability - by Councillor Josh Matlow, seconded by Mayor Olivia Chow
summary: City Council has approved a plan to reduce Toronto's reliance on external consultants. This initiative aims to improve city services by having departments prioritize using internal staff for consulting work, potentially leading to lower costs and increased accountability. The city will also look into bringing contracted services in-house and building up its own expertise.

title: Promoting and Delivering School Based Vision Testing in Selected Toronto High Needs Schools - by Councillor James Pasternak, seconded by Councillor Lily Cheng
summary: City Council has asked the Board of Health to plan for a pilot program that offers mobile eye testing to school children in high-needs areas by early 2026. This initiative aims to ensure all students have access to vision care, helping them to perform better in school.

title: Promoting and Delivering School Based Vision Testing in Selected Toronto High Needs Schools - by Councillor James Pasternak, seconded by Councillor Lily Cheng
180 Queens Drive - Request for City Solicitor to Attend at the Toronto Local Appeal Body - by Councillor Frances Nunziata, seconded by Councillor Paula Fletcher
summary: Toronto City Council has directed its legal team to participate in an appeal hearing regarding a proposed development at 180 Queens Drive. The City's lawyers will advocate against the project, supporting a prior decision that refused the development and aiming to preserve the neighbourhood's existing character.

title: 226 Olive Avenue - Request for City Solicitor to Attend at the Toronto Local Appeal Body - by Councillor Lily Cheng, seconded by Councillor James Pasternak
summary: City Council has directed its legal team to appeal a previously approved development at 226 Olive Avenue, aiming to stop or modify the project. This decision means the city will officially oppose the proposed development before the Toronto Local Appeal Body, signaling support for residents' concerns in Willowdale.

title: Micromobility Update
summary: Toronto City Council has approved several measures to enhance micromobility safety and public awareness throughout the city. This includes ongoing public education campaigns and enforcement blitzes with police, exploring ways to require vendors to inform customers about device rules, and considering a licensing system for motorized micromobility devices. These actions aim to make city streets and parks safer for all residents by improving compliance with current rules.

title: Revised Free-Floating Car-Share Program
summary: Toronto City Council has approved an expansion of the free-floating car-share program. This means car-share vehicles will now be allowed to operate and park in more city areas, specifically those that do not have resident permit parking. This change aims to make car-sharing more widely available and convenient for residents across Toronto.

title: Tree Maintenance Review Update 2025
summary: Toronto City Council has decided to gradually increase the amount of tree maintenance work performed by city staff, aiming for an even split between in-house work and services provided by outside contractors. This phased approach will change how the city's trees are cared for and managed.

title: Improving the Speed and Reliability of the 7 Bathurst
summary: To improve the speed and reliability of the 7 Bathurst bus, City Council has approved new traffic rules along Bathurst Street. These changes include creating no-stopping zones on the east side during peak hours and restricting left turns at Davenport Road and Dupont Street. City staff will monitor these measures to ensure they deliver better transit service for residents.

title: 2 Whitney Avenue (135 Glen Road) - Application to Erect a Structure in the North Rosedale Heritage Conservation District - Refusal
summary: City Council has rejected a proposal to build a new structure adjacent to 2 Whitney Avenue, located within the North Rosedale Heritage Conservation District. This decision upholds the historic character of the neighbourhood, aligning with protections under the Ontario Heritage Act.

title: An Updated Pickleball Play Assessment - by Deputy Mayor Ausma Malik, seconded by Councillor Paula Fletcher
summary: Toronto City Council has asked its Parks and Recreation department to research how other cities manage noise from pickleball courts. This review will explore best practices for reducing noise, including court placement, sound barriers, and different court surfaces, to address community concerns about temporary and seasonal facilities.

title: Shining a Light on Intimate Partner Violence: Empowering Survivors Through Awareness, Support and Prevention - by Councillor Lily Cheng, seconded by Mayor Olivia Chow
summary: Toronto is launching a citywide public education campaign in 2026 and 2027 to address intimate partner and gender-based violence. This initiative will highlight support services for survivors and promote prevention efforts, helping residents access crucial resources and learn how to combat violence in the community.

title: Honouring Dr. Jane Goodall and Dr. Anne Innis Dagg: Getting More Kids to the Toronto Zoo - by Mayor Olivia Chow, seconded by Councillor Neethan Shan
summary: City Council has approved plans to develop a new "Legacy of Learning" program, which aims to provide free weekday field trips to the Toronto Zoo for students in Toronto schools. This initiative will specifically focus on ensuring equitable access for all students, especially those in underserved communities, and will honour the legacies of renowned scientists Dr. Jane Goodall and Dr. Anne Innis Dagg.

title: Milner Avenue and Executive Court/Mid-Dominion Acres - Pedestrian Crossing Protection
summary: Toronto City Council has approved installing new traffic lights at Milner Avenue and Executive Court/Mid-Dominion Acres in Scarborough North. This decision aims to improve pedestrian safety by providing a controlled crossing, and will also restrict crossings in nearby sections of Milner Avenue to guide people to the new lights.

title: 2 College Street - Area-Specific Amendment to the Sign By-law
summary: Toronto City Council recently refused an application to allow a large, third-party electronic roof sign at 2 College Street. This decision means the proposed digital billboard will not be installed, helping to preserve the current visual character of the neighbourhood.

title: Optimizing the Automated Speed Enforcement Program to Stop Speeding
Optimizing the Automated Speed Enforcement Program to Stop Speeding
Optimizing the Automated Speed Enforcement Program to Stop Speeding
Optimizing the Automated Speed Enforcement Program to Stop Speeding
summary: City Council adopted new measures to make Toronto's speed camera program fairer and more effective at stopping speeding. This includes giving drivers a 7-day grace period between tickets from the same camera, installing larger warning signs, and piloting high-visibility wraps on cameras to make them more noticeable. The city will also inform residents and provincial politicians about local speed camera data and road safety to highlight the program's importance.

title: Making our Communities Safe by Supporting a Common Sense Approach to Speed Cameras - by Councillor Josh Matlow, seconded by Councillor Jamaal Myers
Making our Communities Safe by Supporting a Common Sense Approach to Speed Cameras - by Councillor Josh Matlow, seconded by Councillor Jamaal Myers
summary: City Council approved a review of Toronto's speed camera program to improve road safety and fairness for drivers. City staff will report on the program's impact on reducing collisions, ticketing practices, and ways to ensure clearer signage and a reasonable speed threshold before tickets are issued. This decision aims to make speed camera enforcement more transparent and effective in protecting our communities.

title: Toronto Parking Authority 2024 Environmental, Social and Governance (ESG) Report
summary: City Council has reviewed the Toronto Parking Authority's 2024 report on its environmental, social, and governance performance. This report provides residents with an overview of how the city-owned parking authority is addressing sustainability, social responsibility, and ethical practices in its operations.

title: Asset Management - International Association of Public Transport (UITP) Peer Review
summary: The Toronto Transit Commission (TTC) has decided to publicly release a report from an external review of its subway and streetcar maintenance programs and assets. This report, which will be redacted to remove sensitive business information, aims to improve how the TTC manages its critical transit infrastructure and provides transparency to residents.

title: Authorization for Payment of Additional Legal Expense relating to Integrity Commissioner's Report of July 7, 2025 - by Chair Jamaal Myers, seconded by Vice-Chair Joe Mihevc
summary: The TTC Board has approved an additional payment for legal fees incurred by its Chair, Jamaal Myers. This covers expenses from an Integrity Commissioner investigation into a confidential information leak, where Chair Myers was found to have made a minor technical breach of conduct but faced no penalty.

title: Nominal Sublicence Agreement with Foodshare Toronto for Use of a Portion of the East Flemingdon Park Hydro Corridor as a Community Garden
summary: A City committee has indefinitely postponed a decision on a proposed agreement that would allow FoodShare Toronto to continue and expand a community garden in the East Flemingdon Park Hydro Corridor. This deferral means the urban farm, which serves the Flemingdon Park neighbourhood, cannot move forward with its expansion plans for the time being.

title: 258 Glengrove Avenue West - Application to Remove a Private Tree
summary: North York Community Council has approved a homeowner's request to remove one privately owned tree at 258 Glengrove Avenue West, despite a previous denial by City forestry staff. This decision requires the homeowner to replace the tree by either planting five new trees on their property or by paying a fee to the City.

title: 87 Ethel Avenue and 2595 St. Clair Avenue West - Fee Removal for Lifting of Hold Applications
summary: To help build more housing, Toronto City Council approved waiving certain development fees for properties at 87 Ethel Avenue and 2595 St. Clair Avenue West. This decision aims to expedite the creation of nearly 2,000 new homes, including 141 affordable units, in Ward 5.

title: Appointment of Public Members to the Toronto Atmospheric Fund Board
summary: City Council has appointed three new public members – Jehad Aliweiwi, Elizabeth McCallion, and Jean Olemou – to the Toronto Atmospheric Fund Board. These individuals will serve until October 2029, helping to guide the fund's efforts to reduce carbon emissions and improve air quality across Toronto.

title: Advancing Six Sites for the Homelessness Services Capital Infrastructure Strategy (HSCIS) - City-Initiated Official Plan Amendment and Six Zoning By-law Amendments - Decision Report - Approval
Advancing Six Sites for the Homelessness Services Capital Infrastructure Strategy (HSCIS) - City-Initiated Official Plan Amendment and Six Zoning By-law Amendments - Decision Report - Approval
Advancing Six Sites for the Homelessness Services Capital Infrastructure Strategy (HSCIS) - City-Initiated Official Plan Amendment and Six Zoning By-law Amendments - Decision Report - Approval
summary: Toronto City Council has approved changes to the city's land use rules for six properties across Toronto. This decision allows the city to move forward with its strategy to build or expand facilities that provide services for people experiencing homelessness at these locations.

title: Speeding up Transit in Ward 11 - by Councillor Dianne Saxe, seconded by Councillor Alejandra Bravo
summary: To improve transit speed and reliability in Ward 11, the City has asked two departments to propose changes. The Toronto Parking Authority will look at adjusting parking rates on busy transit routes, while Transportation Services will suggest traffic changes like parking, loading, and turn restrictions, particularly for the afternoon northbound Bathurst bus.

title: Review of Automated Speed Enforcement (ASE) Ticket Appeals Process - by Councillor James Pasternak, seconded by Councillor Anthony Perruzza
summary: A proposal to review how residents appeal tickets issued by automated speed cameras was sent to the Infrastructure and Environment Committee for future discussion. This means City Council did not make a final decision on changing the appeals process at this meeting, and it will be debated by the committee first.

title: Taking Action to Protect Tenants - by Councillor Josh Matlow, seconded by Mayor Olivia Chow
Taking Action to Protect Tenants - by Councillor Josh Matlow, seconded by Mayor Olivia Chow
Taking Action to Protect Tenants - by Councillor Josh Matlow, seconded by Mayor Olivia Chow
summary: To boost transparency for renters, Toronto City Council approved a new colour-coded rating system for apartment buildings. This system will rate buildings based on their maintenance and compliance, requiring owners to display a visible sign near the entrance and on the RentSafeTO online map, helping residents make informed housing choices.

title: 42 Donalda Crescent - Request for City Solicitor to Attend at the Toronto Local Appeal Body - by Councillor Nick Mantas, seconded by Councillor Paul Ainslie
summary: City Council decided not to send the City's lawyer to represent its interests at an appeal regarding a planning application at 42 Donalda Crescent. This means the City will not officially argue its position at the Toronto Local Appeal Body, which reviews local planning decisions that affect neighbourhoods.

title: Working Collaboratively Towards a Positive Outcome for the Future Indigenous Women and Children's Shelter at 68 Sheppard Avenue West by Councillor Lily Cheng, seconded by Councillor Alejandra Bravo
summary: City Council has approved a plan to further develop the new Indigenous Women and Children's Shelter at 68 Sheppard Avenue West. This includes exploring acquiring adjacent land to expand the site and assessing the optimal size and operations to ensure long-term sustainability and effective support for residents.

title: Building Faster: Streamlining Housing Delivery and Strengthening the City's Development Capacity
Building Faster: Streamlining Housing Delivery and Strengthening the City's Development Capacity
Building Faster: Streamlining Housing Delivery and Strengthening the City's Development Capacity
summary: Toronto City Council has adopted a new "Toronto Builds" framework aimed at significantly speeding up housing construction across the city. This plan involves creating detailed workplans, streamlining development processes, and prioritizing building permits to make it faster and easier to deliver new housing projects. The goal is to boost housing supply by improving coordination among city agencies and fast-tracking approvals for housing developments.

title: From Concept to Construction: Creating More Homes Across the Housing Continuum
From Concept to Construction: Creating More Homes Across the Housing Continuum
summary: To address the housing shortage, City Council has adopted a strategy to create more homes across Toronto, specifically by authorizing capital funding and incentives for new affordable rental developments. While certain financial details remain confidential to protect ongoing negotiations with federal and provincial partners, this decision aims to increase housing options for residents.

title: Missing Middle and Midrise Housing Implementation Initiative
Missing Middle and Midrise Housing Implementation Initiative
summary: To boost housing options, City Council approved a plan to help residents build multiplexes and mid-rise homes more easily across Toronto. The City will develop and make publicly available pre-approved designs for four- and six-unit homes by mid-2026, simplifying the construction process for property owners.

title: Creating More Affordable Housing Options in Our Neighbourhoods - by Councillor Jamaal Myers, seconded by Councillor Josh Matlow
Creating More Affordable Housing Options in Our Neighbourhoods - by Councillor Jamaal Myers, seconded by Councillor Josh Matlow
summary: To encourage more housing options, City Council removed certain fees for building additional homes on single lots. This means that the second to sixth residential units (and some laneway/garden suites) will no longer pay development charges or parkland fees, making it cheaper to create duplexes, triplexes, and other multi-unit homes in Toronto neighbourhoods.

title: Expanding Housing Options in Neighbourhoods - Garden Suites Monitoring Program - Final Report
Expanding Housing Options in Neighbourhoods - Garden Suites Monitoring Program - Final Report
summary: City Council has approved changes to Toronto's planning and zoning rules to expand options for building garden suites in residential neighbourhoods. These updates specify the required distances between a main house and a garden suite on the same property, aiming to clarify how residents can add these additional living spaces.

title: Expanding Housing Options in Neighbourhoods: Major Streets Study - Ontario Land Tribunal Hearing - Request for Directions
summary: Toronto City Council adopted its legal strategy for an upcoming hearing at the Ontario Land Tribunal regarding rules for new housing on major streets.
