The city will oppose certain proposed changes to these planning rules, aiming to ensure that new housing development across Toronto's neighbourhoods aligns with the city's vision.

title: 4610 Finch Avenue East - Zoning By-law Amendment Application - Decision Report - Approval
summary: City Council has approved changes to the zoning rules for the property at 4610 Finch Avenue East. This decision allows for a specific development project to move forward, which could change the types of buildings or how the land is used in this Scarborough neighbourhood.

title: 2025 Winter Maintenance Program Review
summary: To improve winter maintenance services, City Council approved changes that will allow residents to report snow removal issues immediately during a snowfall. The City will also develop a detailed plan to handle major snow events, including determining equipment needs and responding to specific neighbourhood deficiencies.

title: ConnectTO Program Update
summary: Toronto City Council is urging the federal government to enhance and expand programs that help low-income families afford home internet, and to update eligibility thresholds for these subsidies. Additionally, the city will explore setting up public kiosks that offer free Wi-Fi, 911 calling, and access to city services, aiming to improve digital access for all residents.

title: Toronto's Early Years and Child Care Service Plan, 2025-2030
summary: Toronto City Council has approved its Early Years and Child Care Service Plan for 2025-2030, outlining how the city will manage and grow its childcare system over the next five years. This plan prioritizes equitable access to services, including a commitment to co-develop an Indigenous-led plan, and urges provincial and federal governments for increased funding, expanded age coverage, and better wages for childcare workers.

title: Updated Coyote Coexistence and Response Strategy
summary: Toronto City Council has adopted an updated plan for managing coyotes in the city. This strategy aims to improve public education on how to coexist with coyotes, include wildlife considerations in new development projects, and seeks financial support from the Ontario government for urban wildlife management.

title: 220, 230 and 240 Lake Promenade and 21 and 31 Park Boulevard - Ontario Land Tribunal Hearing - Request for Directions
220, 230 and 240 Lake Promenade and 21 and 31 Park Boulevard - Ontario Land Tribunal Hearing - Request for Directions
summary: Council has decided to keep legal advice confidential regarding an ongoing land dispute at the Ontario Land Tribunal for properties on Lake Promenade and Park Boulevard. This protects the City's strategy as it addresses a potential development and a settlement offer for the Etobicoke-Lakeshore neighbourhood.

title: Mayor's Economic Action Plan in Response to United States Tariffs - Update to Council
Mayor's Economic Action Plan in Response to United States Tariffs - Update to Council
summary: To help local businesses counter US tariffs and protect jobs, Toronto City Council adopted a plan to boost the city's manufacturing and industrial sectors. This plan directs staff to develop a job growth strategy and to explore increasing financial incentives, potentially offering grants covering up to 100% of new property tax growth for businesses that create jobs, for a limited time until 2027.

title: Request to Review Supplier Suspensions and Reinstate IPAC Paving Limited and Pave-1 Construction Limited
summary: Toronto City Council has decided to allow IPAC Paving Inc. and Pave-1 Construction Limited to bid on city contracts again, effective immediately. These two paving companies were previously suspended and can now compete for city work on roads and other infrastructure projects.

title: Request to Review Supplier Suspensions and Reinstate IPAC Paving Limited and Pave-1 Construction Limited
Fossil Fuel Advocacy Advertising on City Assets
summary: Toronto City Council has approved new measures to combat "greenwashing" – misleading claims about environmental benefits – in advertising displayed on city property. This means advertisers will need to sign a declaration, and the city will provide details on its website about how residents can report potentially false environmental claims to federal regulators. The goal is to ensure ads are truthful and promote transparency on climate action.

title: Strengthening Protections for Toronto's Tree Canopy - by Councillor Rachel Chernos Lin, seconded by Councillor Shelley Carroll
summary: Toronto City Council has approved measures to better protect the city's valuable tree canopy. This involves exploring new tools to safeguard trees, clarifying how city tree rules interact with provincial building permits, and seeking to make tree by-law violation outcomes publicly available. These actions aim to preserve more of Toronto's green spaces and enhance environmental health for residents.

title: 250 Wincott Drive - Request for City Solicitor to Attend at the Ontario Land Tribunal - by Councillor Stephen Holyday, seconded by Councillor Parthi Kandavel
summary: City Council decided not to send the city's lawyers to a provincial hearing about a minor zoning change for the property at 250 Wincott Drive. This means the City of Toronto will not officially present its position on the requested building alterations at this hearing, which could affect local development.

title: Accepting In-Kind Donation from Scarborough Cricket Association for Facility Improvements at Milliken Park - by Councillor Jamaal Myers, seconded by Councillor Mike Colle
summary: Toronto City Council approved accepting a $150,000 donation from the Scarborough Cricket Association to build new cricket facilities at Milliken Park. This investment will create a cricket pitch and/or batting cages, enhancing recreational options for residents in Scarborough North at no direct cost to taxpayers, pending community consultation results.

title: 2674-2704 Yonge Street and 19 Alexandra Boulevard - Official Plan Amendment and Zoning By-law Amendment - Decision Report - Approval
2674-2704 Yonge Street and 19 Alexandra Boulevard - Official Plan Amendment and Zoning By-law Amendment - Decision Report - Approval
summary: City Council has approved changes to planning rules for properties along Yonge Street and Alexandra Boulevard, paving the way for a new development. This approval requires the developer to provide 205 square metres of public parkland on-site to expand Snider Parkette, and also pay funds for additional parkland not provided directly on the property.

title: Proposed Licensing Regulations for Animal-Related Businesses
summary: Toronto City Council has adopted new licensing regulations for animal-related businesses like pet shops, kennels, and groomers. These changes require separate licenses for each establishment and specific endorsements for services like breeding or boarding, aiming to improve animal welfare and safety standards across the city.

title: Review of the Order Paper
summary: Toronto City Council officially approved its agenda, known as the 'Order Paper,' for its meetings on July 23 and 24, 2025. This action finalized the specific items and key matters that councillors debated and made decisions on during these sessions.

title: City Foundation Drainage Policy
summary: Toronto City Council has decided to review its Foundation Drainage Policy, which impacts how new buildings manage water drainage. City staff will consult with housing developers and other experts to understand the policy's costs and risks, aiming to balance the need for new homes with the policy's environmental objectives. A report with potential amendments and criteria for exemptions is expected in early 2026, alongside a look into revising manganese limits for storm sewer discharge.

title: RapidTO: Transit Priority on Dufferin Street and Bathurst Street
RapidTO: Transit Priority on Dufferin Street and Bathurst Street
RapidTO: Transit Priority on Dufferin Street and Bathurst Street
summary: Toronto City Council has approved new dedicated transit lanes on major sections of Dufferin Street and Bathurst Street, with some lanes also open to bicycles. This decision aims to improve the speed and reliability of TTC services, while also altering traffic flow for drivers on these busy corridors.

title: FIFA World Cup 2026 - Toronto, Governance Lessons Learned for Bidding and Planning to Host Future Mega Events
summary: Toronto City Council has adopted a series of recommendations to improve how the city bids for, plans, and governs future large-scale events like the FIFA World Cup. These changes aim to ensure better oversight, transparency, and risk management, making sure councillors are well-informed about project changes and potential financial impacts.

title: Call to Order
summary: Toronto City Council formally convened its meeting, with the Speaker calling members to order. The session began with O Canada, an acknowledgement of Indigenous lands, and moments of silence in memory of several individuals. This procedural item marked the official start of the Council's deliberations.

title: Appointment of a Public Member to the Toronto Public Library Board
summary: Toronto City Council has appointed Ben Liu as a new public member to the Toronto Public Library Board. He brings extensive experience in higher education administration and community board leadership, which will help guide the city's library system until November 2026.

title: Grant and Program Recommendations
summary: The Toronto Atmospheric Fund approved over $260,000 in new grants for projects that will advance renewable energy and electric vehicle adoption in the city. This funding will support research into new energy models and advocacy for policies that make it easier for Toronto residents and businesses to embrace cleaner energy, helping the city meet its climate goals.

title: Report on the Unauthorized Release of Confidential Information from a 2023 Closed Toronto Transit Commission Board Meeting
summary: After an investigation into the unauthorized release of confidential information from a 2023 meeting, the TTC Board decided not to issue a penalty to Chair Myers. Instead, the Board is asking staff to develop new guidelines on when and with whom confidential information can be shared, aiming to prevent future leaks and ensure appropriate conduct.

title: Report on the Unauthorized Release of Confidential Information from a 2023 Closed Toronto Transit Commission Board Meeting
Publicly Honouring the Service and Contributions of Veterans - by Chair Jamaal Myers, seconded by Commissioner Dianne Saxe
summary: The TTC Board has asked its staff to explore new ways to honour and remember Canada's veterans, especially those who have worked for the transit system. This initiative aims to highlight their historical contributions in TTC vehicles and stations, ensuring the public is more aware of their service to Canada and Toronto.

title: Honouring Frederick Langdon Hubbard - by Chair Jamaal Myers, seconded by Commissioner Josh Matlow
summary: Toronto's transit commission has decided to honour Frederick Langdon Hubbard, the first Black Canadian to chair the TTC Board. Staff will identify TTC properties to be named after him by June 2026, marking the 100th anniversary of his historic 1930 appointment, and an annual leadership award will also be created in his name.

title: Parking Access Revenue Control Systems (PARCS) Contract Award Negotiation Selection
Parking Access Revenue Control Systems (PARCS) Contract Award Negotiation Selection
summary: The Toronto Parking Authority Board has approved moving forward with selecting a company to negotiate a contract for new parking access and revenue control systems. This decision will modernize the hardware and software used in city parking lots, affecting how residents pay for and access these facilities.

title: Feasibility of Entering into a Long-term Nominal Lease Agreement with Toronto Fire Fighters Charities Inc., - 641 Eglinton Avenue West
summary: The city is exploring the possibility of leasing a former fire hall at 641 Eglinton Avenue West to Toronto Fire Fighters Charities Inc. for a 'Home Away Program' assisting burn victims and their families. This update reports that the project is feasible but requires substantial renovation and funding, with some urgent repairs already planned.

title: Cycling Network Plan - 2025 Cycling Infrastructure and Missing Sidewalk Installation - Second Quarter Update
summary: City Council approved several updates to Toronto's cycling network, including changing bikeway types on streets like Blue Jays Way, Peter Street, and Davenport Road. These adjustments, along with the installation of a new traffic signal on Finch Avenue East, aim to improve safety and traffic flow for cyclists and drivers on these routes.

title: Collection Options for District 2 and District Comparative Analysis
summary: Toronto's Infrastructure and Environment Committee has asked senior city staff to conduct a thorough review of future waste collection services for District 2. Staff will explore the practicality of having city employees take over waste collection services after the current private contract ends in 2031, as well as alternative options for facilities and operations. This work aims to ensure future services maintain high standards for residents, improve waste diversion, and control costs.

title: Collection Options for District 2 and District Comparative Analysis
Renaming Rosebank Park in Memory of Councillor Cynthia Lai
summary: Scarborough Community Council approved the renaming of Rosebank Park to Cynthia Lai Park. This decision honours the late Councillor Cynthia Lai, who represented Scarborough North, recognizing her public service and lasting impact on the community.

title: Downtown Community Outreach Response and Engagement (CORE) Program
summary: Toronto City Council has approved the continued operation of the Downtown Community Outreach Response and Engagement (CORE) program. This initiative allows the Medical Officer of Health to work with the Toronto Police Service and other agencies to provide coordinated outreach and support services in the downtown area.

title: Downtown Community Outreach Response and Engagement (CORE) Program
1410 Eglinton Avenue West - Zoning By-law Amendment - Appeal Report
summary: Toronto City Council has decided to oppose the current development proposal for 1410 Eglinton Avenue West at an upcoming Ontario Land Tribunal hearing. This action allows the city to negotiate with the developer to resolve outstanding issues and ensure any approved project includes necessary infrastructure upgrades to protect local services.

title: Re-Opening King Street for Business: Keeping Toronto's Downtown Core and Canada's Financial District Moving - by Councillor Brad Bradford, seconded by Councillor Stephen Holyday
Temporary Street Closure for Eastbound F.G. Gardiner Expressway and Northbound Don Valley Parkway - 2026 Toronto Triathlon Festival
summary: Toronto City Council has approved the temporary closure of the eastbound Gardiner Expressway and northbound Don Valley Parkway on Sunday, July 26, 2026, from 2:00 a.m. to 12:00 p.m., for the 2026 Toronto Triathlon Festival. To manage expected traffic, the city will deploy extra traffic agents and prohibit other event-related road closures in a wide area that day.

title: F.G. Gardiner Expressway and Don Valley Parkway Closure - Bike for Brain Health 2026
summary: Toronto City Council has approved the full closure of the Gardiner Expressway and Don Valley Parkway on Sunday, May 31, 2026, from 2:00 a.m. to 4:00 p.m. for the annual Bike for Brain Health event. This decision means residents should expect significant traffic impacts, but to help alleviate congestion, other major road closures will be prohibited that day, and the TTC is requested to avoid subway disruptions.

title: A Vision and Strategy for a Universal School Food Program in Toronto
summary: City Council has approved funding to expand Toronto's student nutrition programs, creating a "Universal Morning Meal Program" to provide more students with nutritious food. This initiative aims to accelerate the rollout of meals to students in schools. The city also requested increased and inflation-adjusted funding from provincial and federal governments to support this vital program.

title: 36 Romeo Street - Request for the City Solicitor to Attend at the Toronto Local Appeal Body - by Councillor Frances Nunziata, seconded by Councillor Paula Fletcher
summary: The city has decided to authorize its City Solicitor to attend the Toronto Local Appeal Body to defend previous planning decisions regarding the property at 36 Romeo Street. This means the City will uphold the Committee of Adjustment's rulings for this address, and the solicitor has the discretion to negotiate a settlement or hire outside experts if needed.

title: Waiving of Toronto Parking Authority Fees on Bond Street - by Councillor Chris Moise, seconded by Deputy Mayor Ausma Malik
summary: City Council has approved waiving over $115,000 in Toronto Parking Authority fees associated with removing three paid parking spots on Bond Street. This decision supports the St. Michael's Hospital Redevelopment project by reducing costs for this critical infrastructure work.

title: New Etobicoke Centre Shelter Public Consultation - by Councillor Stephen Holyday, seconded by Councillor Vincent Crisanti
summary: Toronto City Council opted to send a proposal about public consultations for a new Etobicoke Centre shelter to the Economic and Community Development Committee for more review. This means the community's opportunity to provide input on the potential shelter will be delayed while the committee considers the matter.

title: Recognition of 50th Anniversary of the Cabbagetown Group Softball League - by Councillor Paula Fletcher, seconded by Councillor Chris Moise
summary: City Council has officially congratulated the Cabbagetown Group Softball League on its 50th anniversary. This decision celebrates a significant milestone for a local sports league that has fostered community spirit and recreation for half a century in Toronto.

title: Commencing Public Consultations for Communities Impacted by New Shelter Locations - by Councillor James Pasternak, seconded by Councillor Parthi Kandavel
summary: Toronto City Council has decided to improve how it consults with residents about new homeless shelters. The city will now hold individual public meetings for each planned shelter location, working closely with local councillors to determine the best format and timing. This also means restarting the community engagement process for a proposed shelter at 1220 Wilson Avenue, ensuring local input and language needs are met before any construction begins.

title: Commencing Public Consultations for Communities Impacted by New Shelter Locations - by Councillor James Pasternak, seconded by Councillor Parthi Kandavel
Leveraging City-Owned Real Estate to Support City Council Objectives - Long-Term Financial Plan Update
summary: City officials have approved a new strategy to build more housing by combining new homes with existing or planned Parks and Recreation facilities. This initiative aims to make better use of city-owned land to help address Toronto's housing needs and will be integrated into the five-year plan for parks and recreation spaces.

title: Automated Vehicle Pilot Deployment in Toronto Under the Highway Traffic Act
summary: City Council has decided to oversee a pilot program testing automated vehicles for delivering goods in Toronto, with a report on the findings and lessons learned due by late 2026. Council is also urgently seeking advice from federal and provincial privacy commissioners regarding a company's practice of recording people's faces on public streets without consent and sending those images to the United States. This action aims to address privacy concerns and ensure residents' rights are protected as new vehicle technologies are explored in the city.

title: Automated Vehicle Pilot Deployment in Toronto Under the Highway Traffic Act
Assessment of City Impacts from Bill 17, Protect Ontario by Building Faster and Smarter Act, 2025 and Provincial Budget
summary: Toronto City Council is requesting the provincial government to compensate the city for an estimated $1.9 billion cash flow reduction over the next decade, caused by Bill 17's changes to development charge payments. The city also decided to temporarily stop increasing local development charges in 2025 and 2026, as it seeks more control over funding new infrastructure and setting affordable housing requirements.

title: A Strategic Parking Framework for the City of Toronto
A Strategic Parking Framework for the City of Toronto
summary: Toronto City Council has approved a new city-wide plan for managing parking. A key decision prevents the city from introducing new fees for parking at currently free city-owned lots, such as those at parks, community centres, and libraries. The plan also aims to improve parking signage, make visitor permits more convenient, and add more accessible parking spaces.

title: Updates on Vision Zero Road Safety Initiatives: Improving Crossings for Pedestrians, Updated Road Classification Criteria and Other Matters
summary: Toronto City Council adopted a series of updates to its Vision Zero road safety plan, introducing new policies for deciding where to install pedestrian crossings and traffic lights. The changes also include adding traffic signals at 15 intersections across the city, updating road classifications, and lowering speed limits on specific streets like parts of Forest Manor Road and Avenue Road. These initiatives are designed to improve safety for pedestrians, cyclists, and drivers, helping to reduce collisions and serious injuries on Toronto's streets.

title: Updates on Vision Zero Road Safety Initiatives: Improving Crossings for Pedestrians, Updated Road Classification Criteria and Other Matters
2823, 2825, 2827 and 2829 Eglinton Avenue East - Zoning By-law Amendment - Decision Report - Approval
summary: Toronto City Council has approved changes to the zoning rules for properties located at 2823, 2825, 2827, and 2829 Eglinton Avenue East. This decision allows for new development at these specific addresses, impacting the type and scale of buildings that can be constructed in this Scarborough Southwest neighbourhood.

title: 672 to 676 Birchmount Road - Official Plan and Zoning By-law Amendment Applications - Decision Report - Approval
summary: City Council has updated the Official Plan and Zoning By-law for the properties located at 672-676 Birchmount Road in Scarborough Southwest. This decision permits new development at these sites, which could lead to changes in the type or density of buildings in the area.

title: Housing Accelerator Fund: Apartment Infill Study - Residential Infill Report
summary: Toronto City Council has approved new zoning rules aimed at increasing housing options across the city. This decision makes it easier to build small apartment buildings in residential neighbourhoods, previously limited to single-family homes, helping to address the housing shortage.

title: Housing Action Plan: Avenues Policy Review - Phase Two Work Plan
Housing Action Plan: Avenues Policy Review - Phase Two Work Plan
summary: Toronto City Council adopted the next phase of its plan to review policies for building more housing along the city's "avenues" – major streets designated for growth. This work plan includes public consultations and outlines how the city will study potential changes to encourage more housing options, including adding Ward 2 (Etobicoke Centre) to a later stage of the review.

title: 3406-3434 Weston Road - Official Plan and Zoning By-law Amendment Application - Decision Report - Approval
summary: Toronto City Council has approved changes to the planning rules for the property located at 3406-3434 Weston Road. This decision allows for new development on the site, potentially altering the neighbourhood's character by permitting different types of buildings or uses.

title: Addressing Gaps in the City's Heat Relief Strategy - by Mayor Olivia Chow, seconded by Councillor Alejandra Bravo
Addressing Gaps in the City's Heat Relief Strategy - by Mayor Olivia Chow, seconded by Councillor Alejandra Bravo
Addressing Gaps in the City's Heat Relief Strategy - by Mayor Olivia Chow, seconded by Councillor Alejandra Bravo
summary: Toronto City Council has approved a series of measures to improve the city's response to extreme heat. This involves allocating funds for water bottle distribution, ensuring outdoor pools are properly staffed, planning for more 24/7 cooling centres and water trucks, and developing a program with the Red Cross to check on vulnerable residents during heat waves. These steps aim to better protect residents, especially those most vulnerable, during extreme weather events.

title: Securing a Dedicated School Location to Support Toronto's Film Industry - by Councillor Rachel Chernos Lin, seconded by Councillor Paula Fletcher
summary: To boost Toronto's thriving film and television industry, City Council has asked staff to explore finding a closed Toronto District School Board school. This location would be dedicated to film and TV production, with staff reporting back by late 2025 with options for securing such a site.

title: 3755 Bloor Street West - Construction Staging Area
summary: To allow for a construction staging area at 3755 Bloor Street West, Toronto City Council has approved temporary closures of a sidewalk, a right-turn lane, and a bike lane on Kipling Avenue. These closures, which will last from June 30 to December 31, 2025, will impact pedestrians, cyclists, and drivers, but the City is requiring the developer to provide a safe, accessible pedestrian walkway and ensure traffic safety measures are in place.

title: Torbarrie Road and Judy Sgro Avenue - Pedestrian Crossing Protection
summary: Toronto City Council has approved the installation of new traffic lights at the intersection of Torbarrie Road and Judy Sgro Avenue to improve safety and traffic flow. This decision means residents in the Humber River - Black Creek area will get traffic signals, but not a separate pedestrian crossover.

title: Re-Opening King Street for Business: Keeping Toronto's Downtown Core and Canada's Financial District Moving - by Councillor Brad Bradford, seconded by Councillor Stephen Holyday
summary: To alleviate downtown traffic congestion, Toronto City Council approved temporarily reopening King Street between Spadina Avenue and Church Street to vehicular traffic while streetcars are not in service. Council also requested a report by April 2026 outlining better ways to engage the public and mitigate traffic impacts before future major construction projects begin, aiming to reduce disruptions for residents.

title: Speeding Up Streetcars: Traffic Amendments on Adelaide Street, King Street and York Street - by Mayor Olivia Chow, seconded by Deputy Mayor Ausma Malik
summary: To help manage traffic during ongoing streetcar track construction at King and Church Streets, City Council approved temporary changes to parking and traffic rules on nearby Adelaide, King, and York Streets. An additional $350,000 will also be spent to hire more traffic officers, aiming to keep traffic moving and speed up streetcar service in the area.

title: Expanding Housing Options in Neighbourhoods - Multiplex - Monitoring Program - Final Report
Expanding Housing Options in Neighbourhoods - Multiplex - Monitoring Program - Final Report
summary: To increase housing options across Toronto, City Council has approved changes to zoning and city rules that make it easier to build multiplexes like duplexes and triplexes in more neighbourhoods. The city will also closely monitor the impact and effectiveness of these new rules until the end of 2027.

title: Housing Accelerator Fund: Expanding Permissions in Neighbourhoods for Low-Rise Sixplexes - Final Report
Housing Accelerator Fund: Expanding Permissions in Neighbourhoods for Low-Rise Sixplexes - Final Report
Housing Accelerator Fund: Expanding Permissions in Neighbourhoods for Low-Rise Sixplexes - Final Report
Housing Accelerator Fund: Expanding Permissions in Neighbourhoods for Low-Rise Sixplexes - Final Report
Housing Accelerator Fund: Expanding Permissions in Neighbourhoods for Low-Rise Sixplexes - Final Report
Housing Accelerator Fund: Expanding Permissions in Neighbourhoods for Low-Rise Sixplexes - Final Report
summary: City Council has approved changes to city planning rules to allow for low-rise buildings with up to six units, known as sixplexes, in residential neighbourhoods. This decision aims to increase the supply of housing options, initially focusing on the Toronto and East York district and a specific area in Scarborough. The city will also ensure infrastructure and services are in place to support these new housing types.

title: Call to Order
summary: Toronto City Council officially began its meeting on June 25 and 26, 2025. Council observed a moment of silence for several community members and acknowledged the traditional territory of Indigenous Peoples before commencing their work on city matters.

title: Platform Edge Doors Study - Outcome of Feasibility and Business Case Studies
Platform Edge Doors Study - Outcome of Feasibility and Business Case Studies
Platform Edge Doors Study - Outcome of Feasibility and Business Case Studies
summary: To boost subway safety and prevent service disruptions from track intrusions, the Toronto Transit Commission has approved plans to integrate platform safety enhancements. This means future new stations will require Platform Edge Doors, and a pilot installation for an existing station is being considered for the 2026 budget, alongside ongoing evaluation of other protective technologies.

title: Platform Edge Doors Study - Outcome of Feasibility and Business Case Studies
49 Bellamy Road South - Application to Remove a Private Tree
summary: Scarborough Community Council approved a resident's request to remove a private black walnut tree at 49 Bellamy Road South, even though the city's forestry division found it to be healthy. This decision requires the resident to replace it with five new trees, either by planting them or making a payment to the city.

title: 62 Orchard Park Drive - Zoning By-law Amendment Application - Decision Report - Approval
summary: City Council has approved changes to the zoning rules for 62 Orchard Park Drive, which will allow for a new development on the property. To address community concerns, the city will also work with local residents to improve parking and traffic safety on nearby streets, exploring options like speed humps, stop signs, and traffic signals.

title: Amberjack Boulevard - Traffic Calming (Speed Humps)
summary: To improve road safety and slow traffic, the Scarborough Community Council has authorized installing eight new speed humps on Amberjack Boulevard, specifically between Bellamy Road North and Brimorton Drive. This action will make the street safer for local residents and pedestrians.

title: Appointment of Chief Executive Officer
summary: The TTC Board has approved the appointment of a new Chief Executive Officer to lead Toronto's public transit system. This decision ensures ongoing leadership for the TTC, impacting the future direction and management of transit services for residents.

title: 224 Hallam Street - Lemonade Stand
committee: Toronto and East York Community Council
summary: Toronto's East York Community Council approved a request to allow a lemonade stand on the public sidewalk next to 224 Hallam Street. The property owner will enter an agreement with the city, taking responsibility for the stand's maintenance and removal if needed, and will pay a fee for the use of public space. This decision sets conditions for small businesses to operate on public property while ensuring public safety and access.

title: 604-610 Bay Street and 130 Elizabeth Street - Official Plan and Zoning By-law Amendment Application - Decision Report - Approval
committee: Toronto and East York Community Council
summary: Toronto City Council has approved amendments to the city's Official Plan and Zoning By-law for properties located at 604-610 Bay Street and 130 Elizabeth Street. These changes will permit new development in the area, which could transform the urban landscape and land use at these downtown sites.

title: 499 Briar Hill Avenue - Request for a Fence Exemption to the Toronto Municipal Code, Chapter 447
summary: The North York Community Council has postponed its decision on a homeowner's request at 499 Briar Hill Avenue for an exemption from city fence rules. The property owner seeks to keep a gate and enclosure that do not meet city safety standards for fences and decks, having previously received a violation notice.

title: Sunnylea and The Kingsway Neighbourhoods - Traffic Calming (Speed Humps) and All-Way Stop Control
summary: The Etobicoke York Community Council has approved installing new speed humps on several streets within the Sunnylea and The Kingsway neighbourhoods. This decision aims to slow down traffic and enhance safety for residents and pedestrians on Ashton Manor, Edgemore Drive, Elsfield Road, Fieldway Road, Grenview Boulevard South, Humbervale Boulevard, and Meadowvale Drive.

title: 2025 Toronto Urban Health Fund Allocations and Review Process
summary: The Board of Health approved over $2.3 million in grants through the Toronto Urban Health Fund to support community-based health projects for 2025. This funding will go towards 22 initiatives focusing on critical public health areas like HIV prevention, harm reduction, and child and youth resiliency across Toronto.

title: 2025 Toronto Urban Health Fund Allocations and Review Process
Proposed By-law Amendment to Provide Access to Social Infrastructure
Proposed By-law Amendment to Provide Access to Social Infrastructure
Proposed By-law Amendment to Provide Access to Social Infrastructure
Proposed By-law Amendment to Provide Access to Social Infrastructure
Proposed By-law Amendment to Provide Access to Social Infrastructure
summary: City Council has updated rules about activities near "social infrastructure" like schools and childcare centres. The changes expand protected zones around these facilities from 20 to 50 metres, while explicitly ensuring that peaceful protests, demonstrations, and labour picketing are still allowed.

title: Ensuring Transparency and Accountability in the Mayor's Office - by Councillor Brad Bradford, seconded by Councillor Vincent Crisanti
summary: City Council has sent a motion proposing increased transparency and accountability within the Mayor's office to its Executive Committee for further review. This means the committee will discuss the proposed changes, which aim to make the Mayor's operations more open and responsible to residents, before potentially bringing them back to the full Council for a decision.

title: Securing a Dedicated School Location to Support Toronto's Film Industry - by Councillor Rachel Chernos Lin, seconded by Councillor Paula Fletcher
summary: Toronto City Council postponed its decision on a motion proposing to find a dedicated school location to support the city's film industry. This means the discussion about potentially creating new educational space for the film sector will continue at a later meeting in June.

title: Securing a Dedicated School Location to Support Toronto's Film Industry - by Councillor Rachel Chernos Lin, seconded by Councillor Paula Fletcher
Renaming Sumach-Shuter Parkette to Louis March Park - by Councillor Chris Moise, seconded by Councillor Paula Fletcher
summary: City Council has approved renaming the Sumach-Shuter Parkette to Louis March Park, recognizing a significant individual in the community. This decision, made despite standard property naming policies, means the local park will now carry a new name and honour.

title: 521 Davisville Avenue - Request for City Solicitor to Attend at the Toronto Local Appeal Body - by Councillor Rachel Chernos Lin, seconded by Councillor Josh Matlow
summary: In a recent decision, City Council has instructed the city's legal team to attend an appeal hearing for the property at 521 Davisville Avenue. The legal team will actively oppose an appeal made against a local planning decision, which may include hiring outside consultants, to defend the city's approach to development in the area.

title: Development Application Fee Review
summary: Toronto's City Council has updated the fees developers pay for submitting new building applications, with the changes taking effect on July 1, 2025. This includes a substantial decrease in the fee for a specific type of by-law amendment application, aimed at refining development costs and processes. The city will also continue reviewing other development-related fees on a regular cycle.

title: 114 Restever Gate - Request for City Solicitor to Attend at the Toronto Local Appeal Body - by Councillor Vincent Crisanti, seconded by Councillor Frances Nunziata
summary: Toronto City Council has directed its legal team to challenge a proposed development at 14 Restever Gate before the Toronto Local Appeal Body. This move aims to stop a project in Etobicoke North that the city believes is inappropriate, giving local residents a say in protecting their neighbourhood's character.

title: 11 Oswald Crescent - Request for City Solicitor to Attend at the Toronto Local Appeal Body - by Councillor Rachel Chernos Lin, seconded by Councillor Mike Colle
summary: Toronto City Council has directed the City Solicitor to defend a local planning committee's decisions about property changes at 11 Oswald Crescent before an independent appeal body. This action means the city will use legal resources, and potentially hire outside consultants, to uphold rulings that affect local development and how planning rules are applied in the neighbourhood.

title: Ontario-Toronto New Deal - Advancing the Delivery of Transit Oriented Communities along the Eglinton Crosstown West Extension
Ontario-Toronto New Deal - Advancing the Delivery of Transit Oriented Communities along the Eglinton Crosstown West Extension
Ontario-Toronto New Deal - Advancing the Delivery of Transit Oriented Communities along the Eglinton Crosstown West Extension
Ontario-Toronto New Deal - Advancing the Delivery of Transit Oriented Communities along the Eglinton Crosstown West Extension
summary: Toronto City Council has approved transferring ownership of City-owned lands along the Eglinton Crosstown West Extension to Metrolinx or another provincial agency. This supports the creation of Transit-Oriented Communities, which are new developments built near transit to encourage ridership and urban growth. Council has also requested the Province hold public consultations to get community input on their plans for these lands.

title: Declaring the Office of Councillor, Ward 25 - Scarborough-Rouge Park Vacant and Filling the Vacancy
summary: City Council has declared the Ward 25 (Scarborough-Rouge Park) Councillor position vacant and decided to fill it by holding a by-election. This means residents in Ward 25 will soon vote for their new representative, with the election costing a significant amount from a city reserve fund.

title: Call to Order
summary: City Council officially opened its May 21 and 22 meeting, observing a moment of silence for recent tragedies and reaffirming its commitment to Indigenous Peoples with a land acknowledgment. This procedural step marked the beginning of their important work on city matters.

title: Award of Doc4755698985 to Carla Construction and Maintenance Ltd., for Operation and Maintenance of a Passenger Transportation Shuttle Service for High Park and other Potential Parks
Award of Doc4755698985 to Carla Construction and Maintenance Ltd., for Operation and Maintenance of a Passenger Transportation Shuttle Service for High Park and other Potential Parks
Award of Doc4755698985 to Carla Construction and Maintenance Ltd., for Operation and Maintenance of a Passenger Transportation Shuttle Service for High Park and other Potential Parks
summary: City Council has approved a five-year contract for Carla Construction and Maintenance Ltd. to operate a new passenger shuttle service in High Park, starting December 2025. This service will help residents and visitors get around the park, with an aim to offer family fare discounts and potentially expand to other parks. Council also requested a review of expanding the TTC's High Park service and community input on stop locations.

title: Air Conditioner Pilot Program for Low-Income Seniors
summary: City Council has approved a new pilot program to help low-income seniors living in apartments get access to air conditioning. This initiative aims to protect vulnerable residents from extreme heat, with the program expected to launch before summer 2025.

title: Toronto Builds - A Policy Framework to Build More Affordable Rental Homes on Public Land
Toronto Builds - A Policy Framework to Build More Affordable Rental Homes on Public Land
summary: City Council has adopted a new strategy called 'Toronto Builds' to create more affordable rental homes and mixed-income communities on city-owned land. This framework will standardize how the City and its housing agencies develop these properties, aiming to increase housing options for residents across Toronto.

title: Commencing Public Consultations for Communities Impacted by New Shelter Locations - by Councillor James Pasternak, seconded by Councillor Parthi Kandavel
summary: City Council postponed its decision on whether to begin public consultations for communities affected by new shelter locations. This means that residents will have to wait longer for the opportunity to provide input on new shelters in their neighbourhoods.

title: Transitioning Towards Zero-Emission Outdoor Power Equipment
summary: Toronto City Council has approved a plan to transition its own gas-powered outdoor equipment, like leaf blowers and lawnmowers, to zero-emission alternatives. The city will also promote these cleaner, quieter options to residents and businesses, aiming to reduce noise and air pollution across Toronto.

title: Ombudsman Toronto Report: An Investigation into the City's Response to a Vital Services Outage in a Multi-Tenant Home
Ombudsman Toronto Report: An Investigation into the City's Response to a Vital Services Outage in a Multi-Tenant Home
summary: Toronto City Council has approved recommendations from the Ombudsman's report, which investigated how the city handled a vital services outage in a shared home. These changes will improve how city staff respond to urgent tenant complaints, communicate with residents, and ensure housing standards enforcement considers the right to adequate housing.

title: 2025 Update on SafeTO Implementation
summary: Toronto City Council has adopted an update to SafeTO, the city's 10-year plan for community safety and well-being. The plan will continue to advance its goals, including creating a toolkit for resident-led Neighbourhood Watch programs and expanding access to crisis services on public transit. The City also asked the federal government for ongoing funding to support SafeTO's violence prevention programs.

title: 2025 Update on SafeTO Implementation
Improved Active Transportation and Water Access to Toronto Island Park
summary: City Council has decided to create a task force to explore ways to improve transportation and access to Toronto Island Park, including potential long-term options like a fixed link. Additionally, residents will not see an increase in Toronto Island ferry fees for 2025, as current prices will be maintained while new fees are reviewed for the 2026 budget.

title: TTC/TMU Partnership Proposal and Renaming Dundas Station
summary: Toronto's transit commission has approved renaming Dundas Station to TMU Station, reflecting the area's strong connection to Toronto Metropolitan University. This change is part of a new partnership with the university and will be implemented with minimal cost and disruption by aligning it with other upcoming transit updates.

title: Award of Doc4929553192 to Stantec Consulting Ltd., for Professional Consulting Services for the Bridge Program Management Assignment Number 3 for Engineering and Construction Services
summary: City officials have approved a contract for professional consulting services to help manage Toronto's Bridge Rehabilitation Program. This funding will ensure the design and supervision of necessary repairs for 28 bridges and culverts across the city, helping to keep vital infrastructure safe and in good condition for residents.

title: Amendment to Blanket Contract Number 47025662 to Avron Foods Limited for the Supply and Delivery of Snack Foods for the Afterschool Recreation Care Programs and CampTO Child Nutrition Program for Parks and Recreation
summary: Toronto approved increasing a contract with Avron Foods Limited to provide snack foods for its Afterschool Recreation Care programs and CampTO summer camps. This additional funding helps feed more children participating in expanded afterschool care and a new snack program for 56,000 kids at 90 CampTO locations, ensuring they receive nutritious food.

title: Nominal Sublicense Agreement with Malvern Family Resource Centre for Use of a Portion of the Finch Hydro Corridor as a Community Garden
summary: Toronto City Council has approved an agreement allowing the Malvern Family Resource Centre to use 2.6 acres of land in the Finch Hydro Corridor. This decision enables the centre to operate a community garden, providing local residents with green space for growing food and fostering community connections.

title: Official Plan Amendments to Align with Provincial Legislative and Policy Changes Related to Employment Areas - Decision Report
summary: Toronto City Council updated the city's Official Plan to align with provincial policy changes affecting designated 'Employment Areas.' These changes refine the types of businesses and developments, like offices and retail, that are permitted or encouraged in these job-focused zones. This decision helps shape future economic development and the character of these areas across the city.

title: Noise Action Plan Update
summary: Toronto's Board of Health has adopted an updated Noise Action Plan to address noise pollution and its health impacts on residents. City departments will collaborate on assessing noise by-law changes and create guidelines to reduce noise from waste collection. The Board is also pressing provincial and federal governments, along with airport authorities, to fund health studies on airplane noise and engage with affected communities.

title: Introduction of By-laws
summary: Scarborough Community Council formally adopted new local by-laws, which include updates to fire route regulations for specific properties and changes to traffic and parking rules on Brockley Drive. These decisions help manage local safety and traffic flow in these Scarborough neighbourhoods.

title: 390 Glencairn Avenue - Application to Remove a Private Tree
summary: North York Community Council has approved a homeowner's request to remove a private tree at 390 Glencairn Avenue, despite initial recommendations to deny the permit. To offset the loss, the homeowner must provide five replacement trees, either by planting them on their property or by paying the city a cash equivalent. This decision reflects how the city balances individual property owner requests with maintaining Toronto's urban tree canopy.

title: Naming Recommendation for a Parcel of Land within Lawrence Park Ravine to "Jaye Robinson Park"
summary: Toronto City Council has approved naming a new park in the Lawrence Park Ravine as "Jaye Robinson Park." This decision honours Jaye Robinson, a former city councillor, and provides a designated green space for the community, even though it deviates from standard naming policy.

title: Build More Homes: Identifying Opportunities for More Purpose-Built Rental Housing
summary: To boost Toronto's rental housing supply, the City Council has directed staff to review projects that could have their development fees deferred indefinitely. This incentive, contingent on provincial funding, aims to encourage the construction of more purpose-built rental homes, potentially increasing housing options for residents.

title: Freezing Development Charges - by Mayor Olivia Chow, seconded by Councillor Gord Perks
summary: City Council has decided to freeze development charges for new housing developments starting May 1, 2025, using special agreements. This move aims to incentivize new housing construction by keeping costs predictable for builders while a comprehensive review of these charges is underway. The Council also reiterated its requests to the Ontario government for more flexibility in managing development charge revenue and compensation for lost funds due to previous provincial legislation.

title: 2025 Education Property Tax Levy and Clawback Rate By-Law
summary: Toronto City Council has approved the education property tax rates for 2025, which will fund local schools through a total levy of over $2.1 billion. Council also decided on 'clawback' percentages that reduce tax decreases for commercial, industrial, and multi-residential properties, helping the city recover lost revenue from property tax caps.

title: Transit Priority Measures to Support Streetcar Service Diversions during King Street East and Church Street Intersection Closures
summary: Toronto City Council has approved temporary changes to traffic and transit routes to help streetcars move efficiently during upcoming construction at King Street East and Church Street. These measures include a new transit-only lane on Queen Street East, adjusted parking and traffic rules, and restrictions on some street-side patios along diversion routes. This aims to keep transit flowing and manage traffic for residents during the intersection closure.

title: Review of Bid Award Panel
summary: City Council approved changes to how the city awards contracts by eliminating the Bid Award Panel. This decision delegates authority to the Chief Procurement Officer and City Manager to approve contracts up to $30 million, aiming to streamline the purchasing process for large projects. New policies will also require mandatory performance evaluations for contracts over $500,000 and regular public reporting to maintain transparency.

title: 158 Sterling Road (Blocks 5B, 5C and 5D) and 190 Sterling Road (Block 3B) - Official Plan and Zoning By-law Amendment Applications - Decision Report - Approval
summary: City Council has approved changes to the Official Plan and Zoning By-laws for the properties at 158 and 190 Sterling Road in the Davenport area. This decision allows for new development or different land use on these sites, with the property owner required to ensure necessary infrastructure upgrades are planned and secured.

title: 164 Homewood Avenue - Request for the City Solicitor to Appeal the Committee of Adjustment Decision and Attend at the Toronto Local Appeal Body - by Councillor Lily Cheng, seconded by Councillor James Pasternak
summary: Toronto City Council has decided to challenge a planning decision made by the Committee of Adjustment for a property at 164 Homewood Avenue. This means the City's legal team will appeal the initial approval to ensure local development aligns with community planning guidelines. The City Solicitor is authorized to hire outside experts and negotiate a settlement to resolve the issue.

title: Taking a Stand to Protect Ontario Place and Our City's Public Waterfront - by Councillor Josh Matlow, seconded by Deputy Mayor Ausma Malik
Taking a Stand to Protect Ontario Place and Our City's Public Waterfront - by Councillor Josh Matlow, seconded by Deputy Mayor Ausma Malik
summary: City Council has requested a comprehensive report on the provincial Therme spa project planned for Ontario Place. This report will investigate how 16 acres of City-owned land were transferred to the Province, assess the project's alignment with City planning principles for public benefit and waterfront access, and detail any remaining required approvals. This decision aims to protect Toronto's public waterfront and address local concerns like parking and construction impacts.

title: Results of Collective Bargaining Negotiations between the City of Toronto and The Carpenters' District Council of Ontario/United Brotherhood of Carpenters and Joiners of America
summary: Toronto City Council has approved a new labour agreement with the Carpenters' District Council of Ontario. This decision updates the city's fair wage rules for carpenters working on municipal construction projects, ensuring fair compensation and consistent standards across the city.

title: Mayor's Economic Action Plan in Response to United States Tariffs - Update to Council
summary: Toronto City Council has approved continued work on the Mayor's Economic Action Plan, which aims to protect local businesses and jobs from the economic impacts of United States tariffs. City staff will keep advancing these efforts and report back on progress later this year.

title: Enhancing Capital Construction Delivery
summary: Toronto City Council is improving how it manages and delivers construction projects across the city. This initiative aims to make projects faster and more efficient by enhancing project management, strengthening contractor oversight, and improving communication with residents.

title: Enhancing Capital Construction Delivery
Safety Barriers Along the Leaside Bridge - Feasibility Study Update
Safety Barriers Along the Leaside Bridge - Feasibility Study Update
summary: Toronto City Council is fast-tracking the installation of permanent safety barriers on the Leaside Bridge and implementing immediate safety measures. This decision aims to improve public safety on the bridge, and the city will also seek provincial funding to support these suicide prevention efforts.

title: Toronto's Academic Housing Strategy - Improving Housing Outcomes for Post-Secondary Students
Toronto's Academic Housing Strategy - Improving Housing Outcomes for Post-Secondary Students
summary: Toronto City Council has approved a new strategy to improve housing options for post-secondary students. This plan aims to create more affordable student housing, including a pilot project on public land, and will involve expanding financial incentives to help students secure housing. The goal is to address the significant housing challenges faced by students across the city.

title: Scarborough Busway - On-Street Measures to Support Busway Operations
summary: To improve bus service and traffic flow, City Council approved a series of road changes on Eglinton Avenue East in Scarborough. These changes include installing new traffic signals, creating dedicated bus lanes, and adjusting turn restrictions to better support the Scarborough Busway.

title: Scarborough Bluffs West Project: Environmental Assessment Agreement - by Councillor Paul Ainslie, seconded by Councillor Paula Fletcher
summary: City Council has postponed a decision on an environmental assessment agreement for the Scarborough Bluffs West Project until a later meeting in May. This agreement is crucial for the project to move forward and for the required Terms of Reference to be submitted to the provincial government, meaning the project's progress will be delayed.

title: Introduction of General Bills and Confirming Bills
summary: City Council formally approved a series of by-laws, confirming all decisions made during its recent meeting. These by-laws also authorize special charges on specific properties, allowing the city to bill owners for local improvements they benefit from.

title: Implementation Guidelines for Rental Replacement
summary: Toronto City Council has adopted new guidelines to better protect tenants when old rental buildings are demolished for redevelopment. The new Rental Replacement Handbook will guide how the city handles these development applications, and Council also requested further reports on additional support for tenants facing displacement, including improving access to legal help.

title: 1 Dundas Street East: Amendments to the Sign By-law
summary: City Council has updated the sign by-law for the area around 1 Dundas Street East, officially changing all references from "Dundas Square Special Sign District" to "Sankofa Square Special Sign District." This also means signs in the square will now follow the city's standard permitting rules, ensuring consistency with other areas.

title: 1 Broadlands Boulevard - Zoning By-law Amendment Application- Decision Report - Approval
summary: City Council has approved changes to the zoning rules for 1 Broadlands Boulevard. This decision allows for new development or different types of buildings on the property, which will impact the surrounding Don Valley East neighbourhood.

title: 111 Peter Street - Zoning By-law Amendment Application - Decision Report - Approval
summary: Toronto City Council has approved a zoning change for 111 Peter Street, allowing a new development to proceed. This decision includes a requirement for the developer to provide 24 affordable rental housing units within the building, offering more housing options for residents.

title: Review of the Order Paper
summary: City Council formally adopted its agenda for April 23rd and 24th, setting the list of items they would debate and decide on those days. This procedural step determines the order of business and highlights key priorities, including the Mayor's focus on congestion and building more rental homes.

title: Advancing the George Street Revitalization Project
summary: Toronto City Council has approved moving forward with the design and construction of the first phase of the George Street Revitalization project. This initiative aims to improve social services, housing, and public spaces for residents in the downtown area.

title: Congestion Management Plan - 2025 Update
summary: Toronto City Council is updating its plan to reduce traffic congestion, including funding Smart Commute programs with a $400,000 contract. The city also plans to introduce new escalating fees for companies that disrupt roads, aiming to better manage construction-related traffic and its impact on residents.

title: Congestion Management Plan - 2025 Update
Amendments to Municipal Code Chapter 190, Polling and Notification including Lowering the Polling Age
summary: Toronto's City Council decided to lower the minimum voting age from 18 to 16 for many local community polls, which could include decisions on neighbourhood issues or facility usage. This change, taking effect November 1, 2025, will give younger residents a greater voice in local matters that directly affect them.

title: 7-9 Wardlaw Crescent - City-Initiated Zoning By-law Amendment - Decision Report - Approval
summary: Toronto City Council has approved a zoning change for 7-9 Wardlaw Crescent, allowing for the development of new homes in the area. To support the community, the city will work with local groups to establish a liaison committee, conduct a community safety assessment, and explore improvements to parking and road safety.

title: 7-9 Wardlaw Crescent - City-Initiated Zoning By-law Amendment - Decision Report - Approval
Access to Outdoor Ovens in Toronto Parks
summary: The City's Economic and Community Development Committee has postponed its decision on making Toronto's public outdoor ovens more accessible. They are looking at ways to encourage more community use, potentially by removing booking fees and simplifying administrative processes for residents who want to use these amenities.

title: 150 The Donway West and 4 Overland Drive - Zoning Amendment - Decision Report - Approval
summary: Toronto City Council approved changes to the city's zoning rules for properties at 150 The Donway West and 4 Overland Drive, which will allow for a new development. As part of this approval, the developer must provide 770 square metres of land for a new public park and make a payment to the city for the remaining parkland requirement.

title: Indigenous Wellness Committee: Update
summary: The Board of Health received an update on the Indigenous Wellness Committee, which helps shape Toronto's health strategies in partnership with Indigenous communities. This committee ensures that city health initiatives, especially for mental health and addictions, address the specific needs of urban Indigenous residents and align with Toronto's Reconciliation Action Plan.

title: Identifying and Addressing Pressures in the Refugee and Emergency Shelter System
summary: City Council has approved new measures to ensure that any future changes to who qualifies for Toronto's emergency shelters are made fairly and uphold human rights. This means creating clear procedures, consulting with human rights experts, training staff, and making sure policies align with the city's commitment to housing as a human right.

title: Addressing the Financial Industry's Carbon Footprint  - by Councillor Paula Fletcher, seconded by Councillor Dianne Saxe
summary: Toronto City Council has adopted a plan to encourage the financial industry to reduce its carbon footprint. This includes exploring a "Sustainable Finance Hub," studying best practices from other cities, and urging local financial institutions to invest more in clean energy and less in fossil fuels. The aim is to build a greener economy and help future-proof against climate risks.

title: Increasing Toronto Community Housing Corporation Board Remuneration - by Councillor Paula Fletcher, seconded by Councillor Frances Nunziata
summary: New compensation limits have been approved for public members on the Toronto Community Housing Corporation (TCHC) Board of Directors. This change sets a maximum annual payment of $70,000 for the Board Chair and $20,000 for other public members, affecting the city's spending on governance for its largest social housing provider.

title: 55 Lonborough Avenue - Request for City Solicitor to Attend at the Toronto Local Appeal Body - by Councillor Frances Nunziata, seconded by Councillor Paula Fletcher
summary: Toronto City Council has instructed its legal team to challenge an appeal concerning a property at 55 Lonborough Avenue before the Toronto Local Appeal Body. This action aims to defend a previous planning decision about the site, which could affect local development and the character of the neighborhood for residents.

title: Authorization to Release Section 37 Funds from the Development at 5840, 5868, and 5870 Yonge Street to Toronto Community Housing Corporation for 175 Cummer Avenue - by Councillor Lily Cheng, seconded by Deputy Mayor Ausma Malik
summary: Toronto City Council has approved allocating $140,000 in developer contributions to improve security for residents at a Toronto Community Housing Corporation building. These funds will be used to design and install a new secure exterior fence at 175 Cummer Avenue.

title: Replacement Units for Demovicted Dwelling Room Tenants
summary: Toronto City Council is exploring new rules to protect tenants displaced when buildings are demolished. City staff will study changes to planning policies that could require developers to replace affordable dwelling rooms, like those in rooming houses, if they demolish six or more such rooms, or if the combined total of units and rooms is ten or more. This initiative aims to prevent the loss of affordable housing and support vulnerable tenants in the city.

title: Supporting Ownership Housing Developments
Supporting Ownership Housing Developments
summary: Toronto City Council has adopted a new plan to speed up the construction of new condominium developments, especially those that include affordable housing units. This initiative allows developers of qualifying projects to defer significant fees to the city, waive interest on these deferrals, and benefit from potentially lower fee rates, aiming to make more housing available sooner. The city will prioritize projects with a higher percentage of affordable homes, supporting up to 3,000 units.

title: Update on Electric Ferries Shoreside Infrastructure and Ferry Terminal Improvement Projects
Update on Electric Ferries Shoreside Infrastructure and Ferry Terminal Improvement Projects
summary: City Council has approved an increase in funding for the infrastructure needed to support Toronto's new electric ferry fleet at the Jack Layton Ferry Terminal. This will cover charging and docking facilities, necessary dock wall repairs, and interim upgrades to the ferry terminal itself, aiming to improve service for those travelling to the Toronto Islands.

title: Toronto Ferry Service Operational Review
summary: Toronto City Council has completed an operational review of its ferry services. The Council is now awaiting an update from city staff by late 2025 on the progress of implementing any changes recommended by this review, aiming to improve how ferries are run.

title: Extending Vehicle Age Limits for Vehicles-for-Hire
Extending Vehicle Age Limits for Vehicles-for-Hire
summary: Toronto City Council has approved new rules allowing taxis, limousines, and ride-share vehicles to operate for a longer period. This change permits most licensed vehicles to be up to ten years old, providing financial relief for drivers and potentially impacting the cost and availability of these services for residents.

title: Addressing Vacant Storefronts - by Councillor Josh Matlow, seconded by Councillor Paula Fletcher
summary: To address the problem of empty storefronts across Toronto, City Council has asked staff to investigate several solutions. This includes looking into ways to make landlords rent out vacant spaces, improving property standards for these sites, and making empty storefronts more lively with temporary art or pop-up shops. The city will also explore the possibility of giving tax breaks to property owners who participate in these beautification efforts.

title: Review of Toronto's Winter Maintenance Program
summary: Toronto City Council has approved a comprehensive review of the city's winter maintenance operations and contracts, including a forensic audit of recent snow removal agreements. This initiative aims to improve how snow is cleared from streets and sidewalks, ensure contractors are held accountable, and better prepare for future winter storms, directly impacting residents' daily commutes and safety.

title: Evans Avenue - Heavy Trucks
summary: City Council has approved a change to traffic rules on Evans Avenue between Islington Avenue and Royal York Road. Heavy trucks will now be banned from this section of the road 24 hours a day, instead of just overnight. This aims to improve quality of life for residents by reducing noise and traffic from large vehicles at all times.

title: Review of Councillor Remuneration
summary: Toronto City Council has approved a salary increase for all Ward Councillors, setting their new annual pay at $170,588.60. The city will transfer $957,000 from its corporate budget to cover these increases, impacting city spending for services. The decision also established a process for future salary reviews at the start of each council term.

title: Review of Councillor Remuneration
Review of Councillor Remuneration
Review of Councillor Remuneration
Advancing the Tamil Community Centre
summary: To support the creation of a new community hub, City Council approved waiving planning application fees for the Tamil Community Centre project. This decision helps the Canadian Tamil community move forward with building a valuable centre for residents in northeast Scarborough.

title: 104 - 114 Finch Avenue East - Official Plan Amendment, Zoning By-law Amendment, Applications - Decision Report - Approval
summary: Toronto City Council has approved updates to the planning and zoning rules for 104-114 Finch Avenue East, paving the way for a new development. As part of this approval, the developer must provide parkland, either by dedicating land at another location or by paying a sum to the city for future parkland acquisitions.

title: Road Alteration - King Street East at George Street
summary: Toronto City Council has approved closing the southerly eastbound lane on King Street East, between George Street East and 38 metres west, to regular car traffic. This change will alter traffic flow in the area, potentially benefiting transit or creating more space for other uses on this busy street.

title: Proposed naming of the Public Lane North of Gerrard Street East and East of Greenwood Avenue
summary: City Council decided to name a public laneway in the Toronto-Danforth area 'Luella Price Lane.' To do this, they bent the rules slightly by accepting signatures gathered before the pandemic, ensuring this local space gets a permanent identity.

title: Use of Nathan Phillips Square for Various Events - May 1 - September 30, 2025
Use of Nathan Phillips Square for Various Events - May 1 - September 30, 2025
summary: Toronto City Council has approved permits for many community and cultural organizations to host events in Nathan Phillips Square from May to September 2025. This decision allows for activities like beer gardens and donation drives, ensuring residents have access to a variety of public events downtown.

title: Review of the Order Paper
summary: Toronto City Council approved its meeting agendas for March 26 and 27, 2025. This decision finalized the order in which councillors would discuss various important items, including collective bargaining negotiations and pressures in the refugee and emergency shelter system.

title: Transit Priority on Queens Quay East - Interim Priority Bus Lanes
summary: City Council has approved new interim priority bus lanes on sections of Queens Quay East, Parliament Street, and Front Street West. These changes aim to make transit service more efficient and reduce travel times for riders in the downtown core. The plan also includes specific intersection changes and turn restrictions to support the new bus lanes.

title: Mayor's Economic Action Plan in Response to United States Tariffs
Mayor's Economic Action Plan in Response to United States Tariffs
Mayor's Economic Action Plan in Response to United States Tariffs
summary: City Council has adopted the Mayor’s Economic Action Plan to help Toronto respond to ongoing trade disputes and tariffs from the United States. This plan outlines strategies to protect the city’s economy and jobs, including support for businesses and improving the movement of goods and services. The City Manager will regularly update the plan to ensure it remains effective in addressing economic uncertainties.

title: Results of Collective Bargaining Negotiations Between the City of Toronto and CUPE Local 79
summary: City Council has approved new collective agreements with CUPE Local 79, which represents many Toronto city workers. These agreements set new terms for pay, benefits, and working conditions, requiring adjustments to the city's operating budgets.

title: Recruitment for the Deputy City Manager, Community and Social Services
summary: Toronto City Council has appointed Kate Bassil and Denise Andrea Campbell to new Deputy City Manager roles. These senior leaders will oversee crucial city departments, including Community and Emergency Services, and Community Development and Social Services, shaping how the city delivers vital services to residents.

title: Building a Universal Student Food Program in Toronto
Building a Universal Student Food Program in Toronto
summary: Toronto City Council has approved a $5 million expansion for the city's school food program in 2025, aiming to provide more students with access to healthy meals. This increase will be covered by existing savings from last year, meaning no new net costs for taxpayers. Council is also requesting the federal and provincial governments contribute more funding to help build a universal student food program.

title: Proposed Pilot for Non-Motorized Mobile Food Vendors in University-Rosedale
summary: City Council has launched a pilot program allowing non-motorized food vendors, such as food trailers, to operate in designated zones within the University-Rosedale area. This trial runs from April 1 to December 31, 2025, offering residents more diverse food options on city streets during this period.

title: Cost-Benefit Analysis of a Pilot for Free Wi-Fi Service on Bus and Streetcar Platforms
summary: The Toronto Transit Commission is exploring options to provide public Wi-Fi on buses, streetcars, and in subway tunnels, working to close the city's digital divide. They will also survey customers to understand the actual demand, given that many riders now prefer using widely available cellular services. This decision will shape future internet access for commuters across the transit network.

title: Suspension of Duron Ontario Ltd.
summary: To ensure responsible contracting and protect public funds, City Council has banned Duron Ontario Ltd. and its affiliates from bidding on any City of Toronto contracts for five years. This decision also includes a directive to review potential impacts on unionized construction workers involved in city projects.

title: An Urgent Need to Improve Bus and Streetcar Travel Time and Reliability - by Commissioner Julie Osborne, seconded by Commissioner Fenton Jagdeo
summary: Transit officials have approved an urgent request to City Council to prioritize improving bus and streetcar travel times and reliability across Toronto. This decision aims to make public transit faster and more dependable by influencing urban planning, roadway design, and re-evaluating street parking, ensuring recent investments in increased service are truly effective. This matters to residents by encouraging transit use and helping reduce overall traffic congestion.

title: 513 Huntingwood Drive - Application to Remove a Private Tree
summary: Scarborough Community Council rejected an application to remove a privately owned Colorado blue spruce tree at 513 Huntingwood Drive. While the homeowner wanted the tree removed due to concerns about its condition, the City's Urban Forestry staff determined the tree is healthy and maintainable. This decision means the tree will remain on the property, upholding the City's tree protection by-laws.

title: 2759 and 2761 to 2763 Kingston Road and 52 St Quentin Avenue - Official Plan and Zoning By-law Amendment Application - Decision Report - Approval
summary: City Council approved changes to planning rules for properties on Kingston Road and St Quentin Avenue in Scarborough, allowing for a new development. These changes require a minimum number of larger family-sized units, specifically 2- and 3-bedroom apartments, to be included in the project. The owner must also provide a comprehensive relocation and financial assistance plan for existing tenants displaced by the redevelopment.

title: Ensuring Safety for the Ontario Line Construction -  Ward 14
committee: Toronto and East York Community Council
summary: Toronto's City Council recently addressed safety concerns for the Ontario Line construction, asking Metrolinx to prioritize safety when using city roads for their work. They also requested Metrolinx to clearly post all heavy truck routes on their website, ensuring residents have access to crucial information about construction impacts in their neighbourhoods.

title: Ontario Honda Dealers Indy Toronto Race 2025 - Temporary Street Closures
committee: Toronto and East York Community Council
summary: Toronto City Council has approved the return of the Ontario Honda Dealers Indy Toronto Race for 2025, allowing a new agreement with the event organizer. This means residents in the downtown area, especially around Liberty Village, should expect temporary street closures and traffic disruptions for the annual event.

title: 2810 - 2816 Bayview Avenue - Official Plan Amendment and Zoning By-law Amendment - Decision Report - Approval
summary: Toronto City Council has approved changes to planning and zoning rules for a new development at 2810-2816 Bayview Avenue. This decision allows for a new building project, which now must include at least 16 visitor parking spaces and either provide land for a park or contribute funds towards creating park space in the city.

title: Evans Avenue - Speed Limit Amendment
summary: The Etobicoke York Community Council has decided to reduce the speed limit on a section of Evans Avenue from 50 km/h to 40 km/h. This change, applying to the stretch between Islington Avenue and Royal York Road, aims to improve safety for residents in the area.

title: 2025 Operating and Capital Budget
summary: Toronto City Council has approved the city's 2025 annual spending plan, which outlines how funds will be allocated for various services and programs. Council made several adjustments, reallocating money to support initiatives such as the Little Jamaica Cultural District, expand pickleball and seniors programs, and enhance tree care and ravine stewardship.

title: Budget Implementation Including Property Tax Rates, User Fees and Related Matters
Budget Implementation Including Property Tax Rates, User Fees and Related Matters
Budget Implementation Including Property Tax Rates, User Fees and Related Matters
Budget Implementation Including Property Tax Rates, User Fees and Related Matters
summary: Toronto City Council has set the property tax rates for 2025, which includes a 5.4% increase for residential properties. These changes will help fund essential city services and specific capital projects for transit and housing.

title: Re-Opening and Amending Item 2024.EX15.3 - Advancing the Homelessness Services Capital Infrastructure Strategy (HSCIS) and the 2025 Shelter Infrastructure Plan - by Councillor James Pasternak, seconded by Councillor Jon Burnside
summary: City Council rejected a request to re-open and amend a previous decision regarding the homelessness services capital infrastructure strategy, specifically for a site at 1220 Wilson Avenue. This means the original plans for that shelter location remain in effect, continuing the city's approach to housing and support for people experiencing homelessness.

title: Let's Amplify the Need for a New Fiscal Framework - New Deal for Cities - by Councillor Lily Cheng, seconded by Councillor Jamaal Myers
Let's Amplify the Need for a New Fiscal Framework - New Deal for Cities - by Councillor Lily Cheng, seconded by Councillor Jamaal Myers
summary: City Council is advocating for a "New Deal for Cities" to establish a more sustainable financial framework for Toronto. This means the city will collaborate with other municipal organizations to highlight the need for better funding from provincial and federal governments, helping Toronto fund services without relying heavily on property taxes.

title: Creation of a City of Toronto "Buy Local, Buy Canadian" Campaign - by Councillor Mike Colle, seconded by Councillor Jennifer McKelvie
Creation of a City of Toronto "Buy Local, Buy Canadian" Campaign - by Councillor Mike Colle, seconded by Councillor Jennifer McKelvie
summary: Toronto City Council recently approved a 'Buy Local, Buy Canadian' campaign to protect local jobs and businesses. In response to potential tariffs from the US, the city will encourage residents, businesses, and its own departments to prioritize Canadian-made goods and services.
