They also directed staff to enhance local procurement practices and called on the federal government to create a clear 'Canadian content' label for products.

title: Affirming Our Canadian Independence - by Councillor Stephen Holyday, seconded by Councillor Vincent Crisanti
summary: Toronto City Council has formally declared its opposition to any proposal for Canada to join the United States. This decision serves as a statement of national identity from the country's largest city and will be communicated to all Toronto Members of Parliament.

title: Housing Action Plan: Avenues Policy Review - Decision Report
summary: To help speed up housing construction, City Council removed some review requirements for development along "Avenues" (major streets identified for growth) as part of its Housing Accelerator Fund commitment. However, for new areas being considered as Avenues, the city will undertake detailed community consultations and studies on local impacts like traffic, parking, and infrastructure before any changes are made.

title: Housing Action Plan: Avenues Policy Review - Decision Report
Housing Action Plan: Avenues Policy Review - Decision Report
Expanding Housing Options in Neighbourhoods - Ward 23 Multiplex Study - Official Plan and Zoning By-law Amendments - Decision Report - Approval
summary: Toronto City Council has approved changes to planning and zoning rules to allow for more housing options, specifically fiveplex and sixplex buildings, in Ward 23 (Scarborough North). These updates aim to increase housing choices in established neighbourhoods. The city will also protect by-law protected trees during new construction and monitor the impact of these changes over the next few years.

title: Moss Park Arena - Governance and Operational Transition Plan
summary: Toronto City Council has updated the governance of Moss Park Arena, changing how its managing board is appointed. The Board is now tasked with developing a multi-year plan to boost the arena's finances through new revenue and fundraising, explore potential full reconstruction, and enhance community access with free skating opportunities and improved facilities. This decision aims to revitalize the arena and better serve local residents.

title: ReNew Golden Mile Environmental Assessment:   Final Report
ReNew Golden Mile Environmental Assessment:   Final Report
summary: The City Council has adopted the final plans for new and reconfigured streets in the Golden Mile area of Scarborough, moving forward with a major infrastructure project. This decision approves specific street designs and additional road improvements, ensuring that transportation in the area is ready for future growth and development while addressing safety and utility needs.

title: Toward Municipal Autonomy and Effective Local Governance
Toward Municipal Autonomy and Effective Local Governance
summary: City Council has decided to establish a new advisory group focused on enhancing Toronto's independence and improving how the city is governed. This group will explore ways for the city to gain more control over local decisions, with representatives from all four city districts to ensure broad input.

title: Progress on Building More Supportive Homes
Progress on Building More Supportive Homes
summary: Toronto City Council has approved applying for and receiving federal funding from a rapid housing program. This funding will be used to build more affordable and supportive homes across the city, often in partnership with non-profit groups and the University Health Network, to help address the urgent need for housing.

title: 3100 Weston Road - Notice of Intention to Designate a Property under Part IV, Section 29 of the Ontario Heritage Act
summary: Toronto City Council has decided to officially designate the property at 3100 Weston Road as a heritage site, protecting it under the Ontario Heritage Act. This decision ensures the preservation of its historical and cultural significance, which includes Indigenous heritage and its past use by a Ukrainian Greek Catholic monastic order.

title: Feedback from Toronto Public Health's Health and Homelessness Working Table
summary: Toronto's health board recently approved recommendations to enhance support for residents experiencing homelessness. This means public health officials will assess city services for better accessibility and collaborate with other city divisions and health partners to ensure comprehensive care. The city also urged provincial and federal governments to provide more funding and policy support for critical health, mental health, and harm reduction services.

title: 425 Manor Road East - Application to Remove a Private Tree
summary: Local city councillors denied a homeowner's request to remove a healthy red oak tree on their private property. This decision upholds city bylaws designed to protect maintainable trees, ensuring the preservation of local green spaces and the urban forest canopy.

title: Results of Collective Bargaining Negotiations between the City of Toronto and Toronto Civic Employees Union Local 416
summary: Toronto City Council has approved a new labour agreement with the Toronto Civic Employees Union Local 416, which represents many city workers. This settlement sets the terms for their pay and benefits, directly impacting the city's 2025 operating budget and how public funds are spent on delivering city services.

title: Analysis of Driver Wages in the Vehicle-for-Hire Industry
Analysis of Driver Wages in the Vehicle-for-Hire Industry
summary: City Council has decided to launch a public campaign advocating for fair wages for gig workers in Toronto, including ride-share and taxi drivers. The city will also conduct a data-driven study to analyze taxi driver wages and explore how to improve accessible vehicle-for-hire services for residents.

title: Establishing a Framework to Address Excessive Indoor Temperatures in Leased Residential Premises
Establishing a Framework to Address Excessive Indoor Temperatures in Leased Residential Premises
summary: Toronto City Council has adjusted the dates when landlords must provide heating and air conditioning in rental properties. The new rules, effective April 30, 2025, will require air conditioning systems (if provided by the owner) to operate from June 1 to September 30, while the heating season will run from October 1 to May 15. This aims to improve tenant comfort and safety, with City staff also tasked to explore establishing a maximum indoor temperature standard of 26°C for rental units in the future.

title: Relaunch of the Home Ownership Assistance Program to Support New Non-Profit Affordable and Attainable Home Ownership Housing
summary: Toronto City Council approved the relaunch and updated terms of its Home Ownership Assistance Program, aiming to make homeownership more accessible for residents. The program offers financial incentives like waived development fees and charge exemptions to non-profit and private developers building affordable ownership housing, with $5 million allocated to support these projects. A pilot of up to 50 units will also test a new 'turn-key' approach.

title: 1245 and 1285 Dupont Street, 1260 Dufferin Street, and 213 Emerson Avenue - Ontario Land Tribunal Hearing - Request for Directions
summary: Toronto City Council has approved a settlement for a development application concerning properties on Dupont, Dufferin, and Emerson Streets. This decision means the City will support the project at the Ontario Land Tribunal, conditional on the developer addressing requirements for infrastructure upgrades and municipal servicing.

title: Ombudsman Toronto Report - An Investigation into the City's Decision to Stop Allowing Refugee Claimants into Base Shelter System Beds
summary: City Council acknowledged and reviewed a report from Ombudsman Toronto regarding the city's decision to stop allowing refugee claimants into its main shelter system. This report investigated how the city handled this significant policy change, which impacts vulnerable newcomers seeking shelter.

title: Investing In Youth, Empowering Our City's Potential: Creation of a Toronto Youth Employment Program - by Councillor Parthi Kandavel, seconded by Councillor Jamaal Myers
summary: City Council decided to send a proposal for a new Toronto Youth Employment Program to a committee for further review. This means the idea for a program aimed at helping young people find jobs will be studied in more detail before Council makes a final decision.

title: Turn Off the Lights: Requesting a Review of the Nuisance Lighting By-law - by Councillor Brad Bradford, seconded by Councillor Dianne Saxe
Turn Off the Lights: Requesting a Review of the Nuisance Lighting By-law - by Councillor Brad Bradford, seconded by Councillor Dianne Saxe
summary: Toronto City Council has approved a request to review the city's nuisance lighting rules. This means city staff will look into strengthening the bylaw to address issues caused by excessive or poorly directed outdoor lighting, including modern LED and soffit lights. This decision aims to help residents dealing with light pollution and improve neighbourhood comfort by potentially updating current regulations.

title: Toward Municipal Autonomy and Effective Local Governance - by Councillor Josh Matlow, seconded by Councillor Jamaal Myers
summary: Toronto City Council has forwarded a motion to the Executive Committee for consideration, which proposes giving the city more power and control over its local governance. This initiative could significantly impact how the city delivers services and manages its finances, affecting all residents in the long run.

title: Supporting a Fire Fighters Burn Victim Care Centre - by Councillor Josh Matlow, seconded by Councillor Jennifer McKelvie
summary: Toronto City Council is exploring ways to support a "Home Away Program" for burn victims and their families. They have asked for a report on the possibility of leasing a City-owned property at 641 Eglinton Avenue West at a very low cost to the Toronto Fire Fighters Charities, to provide a temporary home for those receiving treatment at Sunnybrook or SickKids. The City will also collaborate with the provincial government to support this vital program.

title: 187 Oakridge Drive - Request for City Solicitor to Attend at the Toronto Local Appeal Body - by Councillor Parthi Kandavel, seconded by Councillor Lily Cheng
summary: Toronto City Council has decided to challenge a recent planning decision for the property at 187 Oakridge Drive. This means the city's legal team will appeal the ruling by a city planning committee at an independent appeals board, aiming to ensure local development aligns with city guidelines and community interests.

title: Stating in the Strongest Terms Possible, that Proposed Arbitrary Tariffs on Canadian Exports Entering the United States Will Severely Damage Our Respective Economies and Should Be Reconsidered - by Councillor James Pasternak, seconded by Councillor Chris Moise
summary: Toronto City Council has officially expressed its strong objection to proposed new tariffs on Canadian goods entering the United States. Council believes these tariffs would severely harm both Canadian and American economies, impacting local businesses and residents. They will forward their objection to the U.S. Consul General in Toronto.

title: 30 Charles Street East- Request for City Solicitor to Attend at the Toronto Local Appeal Body - by Councillor Dianne Saxe, seconded by Councillor Jamaal Myers
summary: Toronto City Council has decided to have its legal team officially oppose an appeal at a local tribunal regarding a property at 30 Charles Street East. The appeal challenges the city's initial refusal of a property owner's request for minor changes, and the city's lawyers will defend this decision, potentially hiring outside experts. This move ensures the city defends its planning decisions, which can impact local development.

title: Guns, not Fentanyl and Aliens, is our threat - by Councillor Michael Thompson, seconded by Councillor Mike Colle
summary: Addressing concerns about cross-border firearm smuggling, City Council has urged the Canadian government to implement retaliatory tariffs on specific U.S. imports, such as delivery trucks and auto parts. This action is proposed as a response to potential U.S. tariffs on Canadian goods, aiming to pressure the U.S. to halt the flow of illegal guns into Canada and enhance public safety.

title: Authority to Enter into Agreement with Scarborough Health Network for the Alteration of City's Watermain and Sewer - by Councillor Jennifer McKelvie, seconded by Councillor Paul Ainsle
summary: Toronto has finalized an agreement with Scarborough Health Network to allow the relocation and alteration of city water and sewer pipes on hospital property. This supports the urgent expansion of the Centenary Hospital Emergency Department, with the hospital network fully responsible for all related costs.

title: Shelter Safety Action Plan and Contract Amendments related to Lodging for Asylum Seekers and Refugees, and Community Safety Team Services
Shelter Safety Action Plan and Contract Amendments related to Lodging for Asylum Seekers and Refugees, and Community Safety Team Services
summary: Toronto City Council adopted a new action plan to improve safety for both staff and clients within the city's shelter system and will implement tools to address anti-Black racism. The city also approved reimbursing community organizations up to $3.4 million for providing temporary shelter to refugee claimants and committed to expanding specialized housing options for refugees.

title: 321 Laird Drive - Request for City Solicitor to Attend at the Toronto Local Appeal Body - by Councillor Rachel Chernos Lin, seconded by Councillor Mike Colle
summary: Toronto City Council has directed its legal team to participate in an appeal concerning a property at 321 Laird Drive. The City Solicitor will attend the local appeals board to oppose a challenge to a previous planning decision, aiming to uphold the city's planning rules for this specific development. This action shows the city's commitment to defending its position on local planning matters.

title: Call to Order
summary: Toronto City Council convened its meeting, beginning with a formal land acknowledgement recognizing Indigenous peoples and territories. Council members also observed moments of silence to reflect on significant events and to remember individuals who have passed away.

title: SmartTrack Stations Program - Update
SmartTrack Stations Program - Update
summary: Toronto City Council has decided to prioritize the construction of three new SmartTrack transit stations: East Harbour, Bloor-Lansdowne, and St. Clair-Old Weston, as part of a multi-billion dollar program. Council is also pressing the Province of Ontario to secure funding for the two remaining stations, Finch-Kennedy and King-Liberty, ensuring no additional financial burden on city taxpayers. This decision shapes future transit development and local community access across Toronto.

title: Policy Framework - City Response to Demonstrations
Policy Framework - City Response to Demonstrations
Policy Framework - City Response to Demonstrations
Policy Framework - City Response to Demonstrations
summary: Toronto City Council has approved a new policy framework outlining how the city will respond to public demonstrations, aiming to balance public safety with protecting residents' rights. The city will also develop a by-law to protect places of worship, schools, and cultural institutions from hate-motivated protests, and is establishing a grant program to help community groups fund security improvements.

title: Expanding Housing Options in Neighbourhoods - Neighbourhood Retail and Services Study Phase Two Final Report
summary: Toronto City Council has delayed a decision on proposals to allow more housing and small local businesses in residential neighbourhoods. Instead of approving new rules, they sent the plan back for further public consultation and a campaign to explain the changes, ensuring residents can provide feedback before any updates to zoning bylaws are considered.

title: 4001 Steeles Avenue West, 5000 Jane Street and 360 Hullmar Drive - Ontario Land Tribunal Hearing - Request for Directions
summary: City Council has approved a settlement with a developer regarding new zoning rules for properties at 4001 Steeles Avenue West, 5000 Jane Street, and 360 Hullmar Drive. This decision means the city avoids a long dispute over development plans at a provincial tribunal, allowing for progress on the project once the developer meets specific conditions for things like traffic and utilities.

title: 33-51 Walsh Avenue and 2717-2745 Weston Road - Ontario Land Tribunal Hearing - Request for Directions
summary: The city has agreed to a settlement with a developer regarding proposed building projects on Walsh Avenue and Weston Road, which were previously under review by a provincial planning appeals board. This decision allows for development to proceed, provided the developer meets specific conditions related to zoning, infrastructure, and traffic management to ensure the project fits within community standards and local services.

title: 417-419 Burnhamthorpe Road - Ontario Land Tribunal Hearing - Request for Directions
417-419 Burnhamthorpe Road - Ontario Land Tribunal Hearing - Request for Directions
summary: City Council has agreed to settle a development application for a property at 417-419 Burnhamthorpe Road with the Ontario Land Tribunal. This decision allows for a new development to proceed, provided the builder meets specific conditions, including requirements for residential and visitor parking, addressing drainage issues, and ensuring proper tree preservation.

title: Declaring Food Insecurity an Emergency - by Mayor Olivia Chow, seconded by Councillor Rachel Chernos Lin
summary: Toronto City Council has declared food insecurity an emergency, recognizing the urgent need to address the issue across the city. This decision directs the City Manager to incorporate the emergency declaration into ongoing strategies like the Poverty Reduction Strategy and Food Charter. Council also called on the provincial government to immediately boost funding for school food programs and enable their expansion.

title: 2025 Interim Solid Waste Management Services Rates and Fees
summary: Beginning January 1, 2025, Toronto residents will see new interim rates for solid waste management services, as approved by City Council. The city will also launch a campaign to help residents, especially seniors, find ways to save on their garbage bills, for example by switching to smaller bins.

title: Exempting Dwelling Rooms in a Multi-tenant House from Parkland Dedication and Clarifications to Exemptions in Article III of Chapter 415 - Development of Land
summary: Toronto City Council has updated its development rules to remove parkland dedication requirements for certain types of new housing. This change exempts homeowners creating additional residential units, like secondary suites or laneway homes (up to five units per property), from having to set aside land for parks or pay a fee. The goal is to encourage more diverse housing options, including multi-tenant homes, across the city.

title: 123 Bellamy Road North - Zoning Amendment Application - Appeal Report
summary: Toronto City Council has decided to oppose a proposed development at 123 Bellamy Road North at the Ontario Land Tribunal. This means the city is advocating for stricter conditions and revisions to the project to ensure it meets city standards for issues like traffic, servicing, and green spaces. If the development is eventually approved, the developer will be responsible for paying for any necessary infrastructure upgrades.

title: Updates on Vision Zero Road Safety Plan
summary: Aiming to enhance road safety, City Council approved new rules requiring vehicles to stop further away from pedestrian crossovers, specifically 15 metres on the approach side and 10 metres beyond. These changes, part of the Vision Zero plan, also include designating new Community Safety Zones across the city and establishing updated penalties for violations, effective February 10, 2025.

title: Feasibility of Requiring Side Guards on All Commercial Trucks Operating in the City of Toronto, Including Retrofitting the City's Fleet
summary: Toronto City Council has taken a step to improve road safety for pedestrians and cyclists by approving a plan to install side guards on its suitable commercial trucks. This measure, intended to prevent serious collisions, is estimated to cost $5.68 million and will be funded through the 2025 budget process. The City is also asking the federal government to implement national safety regulations for commercial vehicle side guards.

title: Introduction of General Bills and Confirming Bills
summary: City Council passed a series of by-laws, including one that appoints Dr. Na-Koshie Lamptey as the new Acting Medical Officer of Health for Toronto. This change in leadership at the Toronto Public Health unit ensures continued oversight of public health initiatives vital to resident well-being.

title: Advancing Phase One of the Purpose-built Rental Housing Incentives Stream
summary: Toronto City Council has approved new financial incentives to encourage the construction of more rental housing, including affordable units. This decision allows developers to defer fees for building over 6,100 new rental homes and grants property tax exemptions for the affordable units within these projects. These measures aim to boost the supply of much-needed rental housing across the city.

title: Strengthening the City of Toronto's Housing Development Capacity to Deliver Housing Faster
Strengthening the City of Toronto's Housing Development Capacity to Deliver Housing Faster
summary: City Council has approved a new approach to speed up housing construction across Toronto. This will involve Toronto Community Housing Corporation and CreateTO working as "public developers" to build more homes on City-owned land, aiming to deliver housing faster for residents.

title: Proposed Land Lease with the Community Music Schools of Toronto at 1785 Finch Avenue West
summary: City Council has approved a plan to lease a portion of 1785 Finch Avenue West to the Community Music Schools of Toronto. This decision allows the non-profit music school to establish or expand its facilities on city property, providing valuable music education and arts programs to residents at a very low cost.

title: Auditor General's Office 2025 Work Plan and Budget Highlights
summary: Toronto City Council recently reviewed the Auditor General's office 2025 work plan, which outlines upcoming audit projects for the city. This plan guides how the Auditor General will investigate city operations to find efficiencies and ensure public funds are managed responsibly, benefiting all residents.

title: Ombudsman Toronto Housing Unit Status Report 2024
summary: A report from Ombudsman Toronto regarding its Housing Unit has been approved by City Council. This decision ensures regular updates on how the city supports housing rights and will prompt a review of a potential "Housing Commissioner" role, aiming to improve how Toronto addresses systemic housing challenges and advocates for residents.

title: Guild Park and Gardens Revenue New Source
summary: City officials reviewed a proposal recommending that revenue from the Guild Inn Estates sublease be reinvested into Guild Park and Gardens. This suggestion aims to fund future repairs, improvements like public washrooms and trail upgrades, and the restoration of public art, ensuring the park's long-term vitality for residents.

title: Assessment of Scarborough Rapid Transit Adaptive Reuse and Next Steps
summary: City Council has decided to remove the old Scarborough RT infrastructure that is no longer needed for transit. This decision paves the way for public consultations in 2026 to explore new uses for the corridor, such as parks, trails, and other community spaces, with future capital costs to be determined.

title: Metrolinx Scarborough Subway Extension - Long-term Temporary Road Closures and Public Highway Dedication - Kennedy Station Connection, Emergency Exit Building-1, and Lawrence East Station
summary: To allow for continued construction of the Scarborough Subway Extension, City Council has approved several temporary road and sidewalk closures in Scarborough. Drivers and pedestrians in areas like McCowan Road and Eglinton Avenue East will experience disruptions and detours for periods lasting up to six years.

title: Renaming Rosebank Park in Memory of Councillor Cynthia Lai
summary: Scarborough Community Council is reviewing a proposal to rename Rosebank Park after the late Councillor Cynthia Lai, who represented Ward 23 - Scarborough North. This change would honor her significant contributions as a dedicated public servant and community builder, particularly as the first immigrant woman of Asian descent elected to City Council in Scarborough. City staff will report back in 2025 on the renaming and the inclusion of a biographical plaque.

title: Lawrence Avenue East and Valia Road/Private Driveway - Traffic Control Signals
summary: Toronto City Council has approved the installation of new traffic lights at Lawrence Avenue East and Valia Road/Private Driveway. This decision aims to improve safety for drivers, pedestrians, and transit riders in the area.

title: 20 Shirley Crescent - Application to Remove a Private Tree
summary: Scarborough Community Council rejected a homeowner's request to remove a large silver maple tree at 20 Shirley Crescent. The Council decided to uphold the city's tree bylaws, which deemed the 153-centimeter diameter tree healthy and maintainable. This means the tree will remain, contributing to the local environment and urban tree canopy.

title: Euclid Avenue - Traffic Calming (Speed Humps)
summary: To make streets safer, Scarborough Community Council has approved the installation of 13 speed humps on Euclid Avenue between Morrish Road and Meadowvale Road. They also reduced the speed limit on that stretch of road from 40 km/h to 30 km/h, aiming to slow down traffic and improve neighborhood safety for residents.

title: Megan Avenue - Traffic Calming (Speed Humps)
summary: Scarborough Community Council has approved measures to make Megan Avenue safer for residents. Speed humps will be installed along the street, and the speed limit will be reduced from 40 or 50 km/h to 30 km/h in different sections. These changes aim to calm traffic and reduce speeding.

title: Rouge Hills Drive - Traffic Calming (Speed Humps)
summary: Scarborough Community Council has approved the installation of 13 speed humps on Rouge Hills Drive, between Island Road and Lawrence Avenue East. This decision aims to improve road safety by reducing speeding, and also includes lowering the speed limit in this area from 40 km/h to 30 km/h.

title: Housing Accelerator Fund: Expanding Permissions in Neighbourhoods for Six Units and Four Storeys - Preliminary Report
summary: Toronto's Planning and Housing Committee is moving forward with a plan to explore allowing more multi-unit homes, specifically buildings with up to six units and four storeys, across the city. This initial report outlines a workplan for staff to study the idea, learn from other projects, and gather community input throughout 2025. The goal is to increase housing options in residential areas, a initiative supported by the federal Housing Accelerator Fund, with final recommendations expected by late next year.

title: 124 Sandringham Drive - Application to Remove a Tree in a Ravine - Protected Area
124 Sandringham Drive - Application to Remove a Tree in a Ravine - Protected Area
summary: North York Community Council has rejected a property owner's appeal to remove a 63-centimetre honey locust tree at 124 Sandringham Drive, which is located in a protected ravine area. This means the permit to remove the tree, initially denied by Urban Forestry, will not be issued, upholding the city's bylaws that protect trees in ravine and natural feature areas.

title: 220, 230 and 240 Lake Promenade and 21 and 31 Park Boulevard - Official Plan Amendment, Zoning By-law Amendment, and Draft Plan of Subdivision Applications - Appeal Report
summary: Toronto City Council will oppose a developer's request at a provincial tribunal to change planning rules for a proposed development near Lake Promenade and Park Boulevard. If the development is approved, the City will require the developer to cover costs for any necessary upgrades to local services like water, sewers, and roads, ensuring existing infrastructure can handle the new buildings.

title: 4875 Dundas Street West - Official Plan Amendment and Zoning By-law Amendment Application - Decision Report - Refusal
summary: Toronto City Council has refused a developer's application to change the building and land use rules for the property at 4875 Dundas Street West. This decision means the proposed project cannot move forward as requested, though the applicant has the option to appeal the refusal to the Ontario Land Tribunal.

title: 50 Richmond Street East - Nominal Lease Agreement with Unity Health Toronto
summary: Toronto City Council has approved a long-term lease agreement with Unity Health Toronto for the property at 50 Richmond Street East. This decision enables Unity Health Toronto to use the space, with the City contributing up to $10.025 million to cover significant renovation and accessibility upgrade costs. This investment supports healthcare or community services provided by Unity Health Toronto in the downtown core.

title: 3374 Keele Street - Zoning Amendment Application - Request for Direction
summary: Toronto City Council has approved a settlement for a new development at 3374 Keele Street, agreeing to a specific zoning plan rather than continuing a dispute at the planning appeals tribunal. This means the developer must meet conditions, including ensuring local infrastructure like stormwater and transportation can handle the new buildings, before the project can fully proceed.

title: Implementing a Rental Renovation Licence By-law to Address Renovictions
Implementing a Rental Renovation Licence By-law to Address Renovictions
Implementing a Rental Renovation Licence By-law to Address Renovictions
summary: Toronto City Council has approved a new by-law requiring landlords to get a special license before renovating rental units. This new rule aims to protect tenants from "renovictions," where landlords evict residents under the guise of renovations. The by-law comes into effect on July 31, 2025.

title: Building More Missing Middle - Addressing Gaps in Multiplex Permissions
Building More Missing Middle - Addressing Gaps in Multiplex Permissions
summary: Toronto City Council has directed staff to clarify rules for building multiple homes in semi-detached properties, which could lead to more diverse housing options in residential areas. They also asked for a review of development fees for multiplexes with more than four units, potentially impacting the cost and feasibility of building larger multi-unit homes.

title: Expanding Housing Options in Neighbourhoods - Beaches-East York Pilot Project - Official Plan and Zoning By-law Amendments - Decision Report - Approval
summary: City Council has updated the planning rules and zoning for 72 Amroth Avenue, a key step in the Beaches-East York Pilot Project. This decision aims to create more diverse housing options within the neighbourhood, potentially leading to more types of homes being built in existing residential areas.

title: Parkside Drive Study Final Report
summary: City Council has approved a road safety project in principle for Parkside Drive in the Parkdale-High Park area. This plan includes adding a new bikeway, upgrading TTC bus stops, and improving major intersections at Lake Shore Boulevard West and Bloor Street West with designated turning lanes, aiming to make the street safer for all users.

title: Declaring Toronto a Paid-Plasma-Free Zone - by Councillor Chris Moise, seconded by Councillor Alejandra Bravo
Declaring Toronto a Paid-Plasma-Free Zone - by Councillor Chris Moise, seconded by Councillor Alejandra Bravo
summary: Toronto City Council has formally declared its opposition to private companies that pay people for blood and plasma donations within the city. This decision means the city will advocate to other levels of government and health organizations for a system of voluntary donations only, aiming to uphold ethical blood collection practices.

title: Analyzing the Compensatory Aspects of a Councillor's Office - Councillor James Pasternak, seconded by Councillor Chris Moise
Analyzing the Compensatory Aspects of a Councillor's Office - Councillor James Pasternak, seconded by Councillor Chris Moise
summary: Toronto City Council has decided to review how councillors are paid and how their office staff's salaries and job roles are structured. The City Manager will report by March 2025 with recommendations to adjust councillor pay to be competitive, while the City Clerk will suggest modernizing staff compensation by late 2025. This process aims to ensure fair and appropriate pay for those serving the public.

title: 16 Martindale Road - Request for City Solicitor to Attend at the Toronto Local Appeal Body - by Councillor Parthi Kandavel, seconded by Councillor Dianne Saxe
summary: Toronto City Council has decided to send the city's legal team to an independent appeals tribunal to defend a local planning committee's refusal of a development application at 16 Martindale Road. This means the city will actively oppose an appeal by the property owner, impacting the future development of the site and the surrounding neighbourhood.

title: Providing Safety and Convenience for Midtown Residents: Moving Forward with a Pedestrian Scramble at Yonge and Eglinton - by Councillor Josh Matlow, seconded by Councillor Mike Colle
summary: Toronto City Council has asked for a study on the possibility of adding a pedestrian scramble at the busy Yonge and Eglinton intersection. This aims to improve safety and convenience for people walking in the area once the Eglinton Crosstown LRT is fully operational.

title: Provincial Overreach and Toronto's Infrastructure - by Councillor Dianne Saxe, seconded by Councillor Amber Morley
summary: Councillors Dianne Saxe and Amber Morley proposed a motion concerning provincial interference in Toronto's infrastructure funding and local decision-making. However, City Council did not consider the motion as it was deemed redundant, meaning a similar discussion was already taking place under a different agenda item.

title: 171 Pendrith Street - Request for City Solicitor to Attend at the Toronto Local Appeal Body - by Councillor Dianne Saxe, seconded by Councilor Parthi Kandavel
summary: Toronto City Council has directed its legal team to challenge a proposed development at 171 Pendrith Street. City lawyers will oppose the project before the independent appeals body, with the option to hire outside experts or negotiate a settlement. This decision means the city is actively working to prevent a development in the University-Rosedale neighbourhood that may not align with local planning expectations.

title: Protecting Tenants from Price-Fixing on Rental Apartments - by Councillor Josh Matlow, seconded by Councillor Alejandra Bravo
summary: To protect tenants from potential rent price-fixing, Toronto City Council has asked the City Manager to investigate software used by landlords for this purpose. The report, due by mid-2025, will explore how the city can restrict such software and also includes a request to the provincial government to ban it outright.

title: Renewal of Below Market Rent Lease Agreement with Toronto Azzurri Soccer Club - by Councillor James Pasternak, seconded by Councillor Anthony Perruzza
summary: City Council has approved a 10-year low-cost lease renewal for the Toronto Azzurri Soccer Club, allowing them to continue operating their field house and soccer fields at 4995 Keele Street until 2035. This decision ensures ongoing community access to recreational sports facilities in the York Centre area.

title: 80 Guestville Avenue - Revoking a Building Permit - by Councillor Frances Nunziata, seconded by Councillor Paula Fletcher
summary: To protect tenants at 80 Guestville Avenue from eviction, City Council asked the Chief Building Official to investigate the building permits issued for that address. This inquiry will determine if construction has not begun within six months, potentially leading to the revocation of permits to stop current residents from losing their homes.

title: Respecting Local Democracy and Cities
summary: Toronto City Council has formally opposed a provincial bill (Bill 212) that would change the Highway Traffic Act, arguing it interferes with the city's ability to manage its own roads and address traffic congestion. Council believes this provincial action undermines local democracy and has stated it will not pay for any costs resulting from these proposed changes to city infrastructure.

title: Respecting Local Democracy and Cities

Toronto Transit Commission Environmental Report for the Conversion of the Scarborough Rapid Transit Right-of-Way, Busway Project - Tara Avenue and Mooregate Avenue Bus Stop - by Councillor Michael Thompson, seconded by Councillor Nick Mantas
summary: City Council rejected a proposal related to the environmental report for converting the Scarborough Rapid Transit line into a busway, specifically concerning a bus stop near Tara Avenue and Mooregate Avenue. This decision means that the city will not proceed with the associated recommendation, which could have allowed funds to be reallocated to other priority Toronto Transit Commission capital projects.

title: Appointment of Fire Chief and General Manager, Toronto Fire Services - by Councillor Jennifer McKelvie, seconded by Councillor Chris Moise
summary: Toronto City Council has decided to appoint a new Fire Chief and General Manager for Toronto Fire Services, following reference checks and contract negotiations. This individual will lead the city's fire safety and emergency response efforts, with their official start date set for January 2, 2025, or when their employment contract is finalized.

title: Culture Connects: An Action Plan for Culture in Toronto (2025-2035)
Culture Connects: An Action Plan for Culture in Toronto (2025-2035)
summary: Toronto City Council has approved "Culture Connects," a new 10-year action plan to support and grow the city's arts and culture sector from 2025 to 2035. This plan aims to improve funding, stability, and affordability for cultural organizations and artists, with dedicated staff and resources to be requested in upcoming city budgets. The City will also seek provincial support to help achieve these goals.

title: Sidewalks to Skylines: An Action Plan for Toronto's Economy (2025-2035)
summary: Toronto City Council has approved a new long-term economic plan called 'Sidewalks to Skylines,' which will guide the city's growth and development from 2025 to 2035. This strategy aims to boost the city's economy by outlining future initiatives, including developing a major event hosting strategy and enhancing support for local business districts. City departments are now tasked with putting this plan into action and will include necessary resources in upcoming city budget discussions.

title: 49-59 Lawrence Avenue East - Ontario Land Tribunal Hearing - Request for Directions
summary: Toronto City Council has reached an agreement with a developer regarding a project at 49-59 Lawrence Avenue East, avoiding a full hearing at the Ontario Land Tribunal. This decision means a revised development plan for the site will be approved, but only after the developer meets specific conditions related to planning, engineering, and wind studies.

title: Temporary Street Closure for Eastbound F.G. Gardiner Expressway and Northbound Don Valley Parkway - 2025 Toronto Triathlon Festival
summary: Toronto City Council has approved the temporary closure of the eastbound Gardiner Expressway and northbound Don Valley Parkway for the 2025 Toronto Triathlon Festival. These major routes will be shut down on Sunday, July 27, 2025, from 2 AM to 12 PM, impacting residents' travel; to mitigate congestion, the city will prohibit other major road closures on that day and require the event organizer to cover related expenses.

title: Housing Action Plan: As-of-Right Zoning for Mid-rise Buildings on Avenues and Updated Rear Transition Performance Standards - Final Report
Housing Action Plan: As-of-Right Zoning for Mid-rise Buildings on Avenues and Updated Rear Transition Performance Standards - Final Report
Housing Action Plan: As-of-Right Zoning for Mid-rise Buildings on Avenues and Updated Rear Transition Performance Standards - Final Report
summary: Toronto City Council has approved changes to make it easier to build mid-rise apartment buildings along major streets (Avenues). These updates simplify zoning rules and adjust how new buildings fit with existing neighbourhoods, aiming to boost housing supply and offer more housing options across the city.

title: 710 The West Mall - Ontario Land Tribunal Hearing - Request for Directions
710 The West Mall - Ontario Land Tribunal Hearing - Request for Directions
summary: Toronto City Council has agreed to settle a development appeal for a property at 710 The West Mall with the developer. This means the city will support a specific development plan at the provincial Ontario Land Tribunal, provided the developer meets various conditions related to traffic management, local infrastructure, and stormwater plans.

title: Proclaiming December as Christian Heritage Month - by Councillor Nick Mantas, seconded by Councillor Frances Nunziata
summary: City Council has formally declared December as Christian Heritage Month across Toronto. This decision aims to recognize and celebrate the cultural contributions and heritage of the Christian community within the city.

title: Kingston Road and Columbine Avenue - Traffic Control Signals
summary: Toronto City Council has approved new traffic lights at the intersection of Kingston Road and Columbine Avenue. This decision aims to improve pedestrian safety and make crossings safer and more accessible for residents following community concerns about the busy street.

title: Ookwemin Minising and Biidaasige Park: Naming the New Island and Park in the Port Lands
summary: City Council has officially named the new island in the Port Lands "Ookwemin Minising" and the new park "Biidaasige Park." These names, chosen through engagement with Indigenous communities, establish the identity of significant new public spaces created as part of the Port Lands flood protection project.

title: Low-Rise Residential Lands in the Toronto and East York District of the City of Toronto, to Zoning By-law 569-2013
summary: City Council has approved changes to the city's zoning by-law for low-rise residential areas in the Toronto and East York district. This decision updates the rules for what can be built on these lands, aiming to increase housing options and supply as part of the city's Housing Action Plan.

title: Review of the Order Paper
summary: City Council approved its official agenda for the November 13 and 14 meetings. This decision sets the schedule for debating important city issues, including key matters such as housing incentives and a rental renovation by-law, guiding what topics councillors will prioritize for Toronto residents.

title: Build More Homes: Expanding Incentives for Purpose Built Rental Housing
summary: Toronto City Council has approved a new program aimed at creating 20,000 new rental homes across the city, including at least 4,000 affordable units. The city will provide financial incentives like property tax reductions to developers, while also requesting substantial grant funding from the federal and provincial governments to help achieve this housing goal.

title: Extending Voting Opportunities in City Polls to 16- and 17-Year-Olds - by Councillor Dianne Saxe, seconded by Councillor Lily Cheng
Extending Voting Opportunities in City Polls to 16- and 17-Year-Olds - by Councillor Dianne Saxe, seconded by Councillor Lily Cheng
summary: City Council has decided to explore allowing 16 and 17-year-olds to vote in local neighbourhood polls. They have asked the City Clerk to develop a plan by early 2025 on how to reduce the minimum voting age for these specific local elections. This move aims to increase youth engagement and participation in important community decisions.

title: FIFA World Cup 2026 Toronto: Revenue Opportunities to Support Hosting Obligations, Procurement Plan Revisions, and Social Procurement and Community Workforce Development Updates
summary: To help fund the 2026 FIFA World Cup, City Council approved a temporary increase in the Municipal Accommodation Tax, also known as the hotel tax. This tax will rise from 6% to 8.5% for stays between June 2025 and July 2026, meaning visitors staying in Toronto hotels during that period will pay more to help cover hosting costs.

title: Audit of Parks Branch Operations - Phase 1: Improving Oversight of Day-to-Day Maintenance Helps to Ensure City Parks are Beautiful, Clean and Safe
summary: To improve the cleanliness and safety of Toronto's parks, City Council has adopted recommendations from an audit of the Parks Branch. The Council is directing staff to use GPS tracking and better oversight of work records to ensure park maintenance crews are more efficient and accountable for their time. This aims to improve how parks are maintained across the city.

title: Reinforcing the Importance of Openness, Fairness and Transparency in City Procurement: An Audit of the Procurement and Implementation of the PayIt Unsolicited Proposal
summary: City Council has adopted a series of recommendations to improve how Toronto handles unsolicited proposals from companies, sparked by an audit of a previous 'PayIt' proposal. This decision ensures greater fairness, openness, and transparency in city procurement processes. It aims to protect public interests by updating policies, providing staff training, and strengthening project planning for large city-wide initiatives.

title: CaféTO Business Improvement Area (BIA) Pilot Programs Update and Toronto Municipal Code Amendments for 2025
CaféTO Business Improvement Area (BIA) Pilot Programs Update and Toronto Municipal Code Amendments for 2025
summary: Toronto City Council has updated the regulations for sidewalk cafés, including the popular CaféTO program, for 2025. These changes amend city bylaws to clarify who must be notified when a new sidewalk café applies for a permit, particularly when it is located next to residential properties. This impacts how residents and local businesses are informed about proposed cafés in their communities.

title: FIFA World Cup 2026 Toronto: Revenue Opportunities to Support Hosting Obligations, Procurement Plan Revisions, and Social Procurement and Community Workforce Development Updates
summary: City Council is considering increasing the tax on hotel stays from 6% to 8.5% between June 2025 and July 2026, a move recommended by a city subcommittee. This proposed change aims to raise more money to cover Toronto's hosting costs for the 2026 FIFA World Cup and includes updates to how the city plans to procure goods and services for the event.

title: Establishing a Permanent Memorial in Scarborough Honouring Grant Faulkner
summary: The Scarborough Community Council has decided to name a new park located at 320 Markham Road as "Grant Faulkner Park." This action establishes a permanent memorial in Scarborough to honour the memory and lasting impact of Grant Faulkner on the community.

title: 230 and 240 The Donway West - Official Plan Amendment and Zoning Amendment Application - Decision Report - Approval
summary: New development is coming to 230 and 240 The Donway West after Toronto City Council approved changes to the area's Official Plan and zoning. This decision requires the developer to create a new public park spanning over 513 square metres, adding green space for local residents.

title: 85 Helendale Avenue - Application to Remove a Private Tree
summary: North York Community Council has denied a resident's request for a permit to remove a private black walnut tree at 85 Helendale Avenue. This decision means the healthy tree, protected by City by-laws, will remain on the property, supporting the city's urban forest canopy.

title: 85 Helendale Avenue - Application to Remove a Private Tree
110 Caines Avenue  - Application to Remove a City Tree
summary: North York Community Council has decided to deny a resident's request to remove a City-owned red oak tree at 110 Caines Avenue. This decision means the healthy tree, which the resident wanted to remove for a driveway construction, will be protected and remain part of the city's urban forest.

title: 110 Caines Avenue  - Application to Remove a City Tree
985 Woodbine Avenue and 2078, 2086, 2100, 2102 and 2106 Danforth Avenue - Zoning By-law Amendment and Rental Housing Demolition Applications - Decision Report - Approval
committee: Toronto and East York Community Council
summary: Toronto City Council approved zoning changes for properties on Woodbine and Danforth Avenues. This decision allows for a new development to proceed, which will include 12 affordable rental housing units as part of the project.

title: 985 Woodbine Avenue and 2078, 2086, 2100, 2102 and 2106 Danforth Avenue - Zoning By-law Amendment and Rental Housing Demolition Applications - Decision Report - Approval
committee: Toronto and East York Community Council

title: Summary of Legal Disputes - 2024 Third Quarter Update
summary: The Toronto Parking Authority Board received its regular update on ongoing legal issues, which include disputes with suppliers, personal injury claims, and trademark protection. Details about these specific cases remain confidential to protect solicitor-client privilege. This ensures the TPA continues to manage potential liabilities and safeguard its operations, such as parking facilities and Bike Share Toronto.

title: Litigation Update Regarding Option to Purchase Retail Component of Development at 935 Eglinton Avenue West (Car Park 655)
summary: Toronto Parking Authority's Board received an update on a lawsuit they filed to claim $7 million from a developer. This lawsuit is about the Authority's option to buy a retail unit at 935 Eglinton Avenue West, and its outcome could affect how this property serves residents.

title: Update on the Status of Public Spaces around Nathan Phillips Square and Toronto City Hall
summary: Toronto's General Government Committee is exploring new ways to fund improvements for Nathan Phillips Square. They've asked staff to review current event booking fees and consider dedicating that revenue to a special fund for capital upgrades. This aims to ensure the iconic public space remains well-maintained for residents and visitors.

title: Building a Universal Student Food Program in Toronto
Building a Universal Student Food Program in Toronto
summary: City Council is moving forward with plans for a universal student food program in Toronto schools. They've requested a report by November 2024 detailing funding, including contributions from other governments, and operational needs to start programs by January 2025 for eligible schools previously denied due to budget limits. Additionally, a comprehensive strategy will be developed by mid-2025 to achieve universal mid-morning meals by the 2026/2027 school year and universal lunch programs by 2030, ensuring all students have access to nutritious food.

title: 35 Bellevue Avenue - Zoning By-law Amendment - Decision Report - Approval
summary: City Council has approved changes to the zoning rules for 35 Bellevue Avenue, which will allow a new development to proceed at this location. This decision also includes requests for a new city-serviced garbage collection system that won't delay construction and a lighting plan for the building's south side.

title: Ensuring a Complete Community in Don Mills by Having Places to Play, Visit, Work and Study - by Councillor Jon Burnside, seconded by Councillor Parthi Kandavel
summary: Toronto City Council has supported reserving a portion of the city land at 770 Don Mills Road, not designated for new housing, exclusively for recreation and education. This decision ensures that this specific area in Don Mills will provide dedicated space for community activities and learning.

title: Re-Opening and Amending Item 2024.EY15.16 Bloor Street West - St. Clement Catholic School - Speed Limit Amendment - by Councillor Stephen Holyday, seconded by Councillor Vincent Crisanti
Re-Opening and Amending Item 2024.EY15.16 Bloor Street West - St. Clement Catholic School - Speed Limit Amendment - by Councillor Stephen Holyday, seconded by Councillor Vincent Crisanti
summary: Toronto City Council has adjusted the boundaries for a 40 km/h speed limit zone on Bloor Street West. This updated rule sets the slower speed limit for a slightly different stretch of the road, aiming to improve safety for drivers and pedestrians, especially near St. Clement Catholic School.

title: Extending Voting Opportunities in City Polls to 16- and 17-Year-Olds - by Councillor Dianne Saxe, seconded by Councillor Lily Cheng
summary: City Council has decided to postpone its discussion on a proposal that would allow 16 and 17-year-olds to vote in Toronto's municipal elections. This means the important question of extending voting opportunities to younger residents will be revisited at an upcoming meeting in November.

title: Trains Not Tunnels - Alternatives to the 401 Tunnel - by Councillor Mike Colle, seconded by Councillor Jennifer McKelvie
Trains Not Tunnels - Alternatives to the 401 Tunnel - by Councillor Mike Colle, seconded by Councillor Jennifer McKelvie
Trains Not Tunnels - Alternatives to the 401 Tunnel - by Councillor Mike Colle, seconded by Councillor Jennifer McKelvie
summary: Toronto City Council has asked the Province of Ontario to consider alternatives to a potentially disruptive and costly tunnel under Highway 401. The Council suggests exploring a high-speed rail system connecting cities like Hamilton and Montreal, and buying back the 407 Express Toll Route, aiming to reduce congestion and improve travel options for residents.

title: Requesting Metrolinx to Provide Transparency and Accountability on the Eglinton Crosstown - by Councillor Josh Matlow, seconded by Councillor Mike Colle
Requesting Metrolinx to Provide Transparency and Accountability on the Eglinton Crosstown - by Councillor Josh Matlow, seconded by Councillor Mike Colle
summary: Toronto City Council is demanding more transparency and accountability from Metrolinx regarding the severely delayed Eglinton Crosstown LRT project. Council has formally asked the Province of Ontario to launch a public inquiry into the project's extensive delays and rising costs. They also want top Metrolinx and provincial transportation officials to come before a city committee to explain the situation, including the expected completion date and additional costs, and outline support for affected residents and businesses.

title: Request for Federal and Provincial Support in Bail Reform - by Councillor Frances Nunziata, seconded by Councillor Mike Colle
Request for Federal and Provincial Support in Bail Reform - by Councillor Frances Nunziata, seconded by Councillor Mike Colle
summary: Toronto City Council has called on the federal and provincial governments for increased support and funding for bail reform initiatives, including resources for the Toronto Police Service to improve monitoring of individuals on bail and fund programs addressing the root causes of crime. This action aims to enhance public safety by ensuring bail decisions are effective and supported by adequate services and data collection.

title: 186 Thirtieth Street - Request for City Solicitor to Attend at the Toronto Local Appeal Body - by Councillor Amber Morley, seconded by Councillor Gord Perks
summary: City Council has decided to send its lawyers to an appeal hearing to defend a local planning committee's decision regarding a property at 186 Thirtieth Street. This means the City is upholding the refusal of a development application, which could impact the future of the neighbourhood.

title: Municipal Code Amendment - Exemptions for Dwelling Rooms - Councillor Chris Moise, seconded by Councillor Gord Perks
summary: To help speed up the creation of affordable housing, City Council has decided that a new multi-tenant home at 268 Seaton Street does not need to provide land for a park or pay money in its place. This exemption aims to reduce costs and expedite the development of much-needed housing options.

title: Representation at a Toronto Local Appeal Body hearing for 228 Guildwood Parkway - by Councillor Paul Ainslie, seconded by Councillor Jennifer McKelvie
summary: Toronto's legal team has been directed to attend an appeal hearing to oppose a development application for 228 Guildwood Parkway. This decision helps uphold local planning rules and protect the character of the Guildwood neighbourhood, with the city solicitor able to negotiate a resolution if possible.

title: Actions on MyToronto Pay Platform (PayIt) Pending Consideration of Auditor General Report - by Mayor Olivia Chow, seconded by Councillor Gord Perks
summary: Toronto City Council has temporarily paused the expansion of the MyToronto Pay platform, which residents use to pay for city services online. No new services or payment options will be added until Council reviews a report from the Auditor General that investigated how the platform was acquired, aiming to ensure transparency and public confidence in city contracts.

title: Enhancing Capital Infrastructure Program Coordination
summary: City Council has approved the first phase of a new Strategic Capital Coordination Office to improve how the city manages major infrastructure projects. This office aims to make large construction projects, like roads and water systems, more efficient and accountable, which could reduce delays and disruptions for residents.

title: Congestion Management Plan 2023 - 2026 - Fall Update
summary: Toronto City Council has approved measures to reduce traffic congestion and travel times across the city. This plan includes exploring how new developments can minimize lane closures during construction and establishing a new fee for temporary street occupation permits.

title: Congestion Management Plan 2023 - 2026 - Fall Update
Redesigning the Vacant Home Tax Program and Supporting Housing Supply
summary: Toronto City Council approved several adjustments to the Vacant Home Tax program, making it easier for residents to understand and comply with the rules. These changes include extending the declaration and payment deadlines, adding new exemptions for medical reasons, and clarifying definitions for self-contained units and employment-related occupancy. The goal is to improve fairness and efficiency in a program designed to encourage housing occupancy in the city.

title: Redesigning the Vacant Home Tax Program and Supporting Housing Supply
Approach to Public Electric Vehicle (EV) Charging to 2030
Approach to Public Electric Vehicle (EV) Charging to 2030
summary: Toronto City Council adopted a plan to expand public electric vehicle (EV) charging across the city until 2030. This initiative will coordinate city efforts to install more charging stations on public properties, develop funding plans, and ensure consistent operations for these new facilities.

title: Fossil Fuel Advocacy Advertising on City Assets
Fossil Fuel Advocacy Advertising on City Assets
summary: City Council has directed staff to develop a new policy by mid-2025 that will restrict fossil fuel advocacy advertising on city-owned property, like transit shelters and community centres. This decision aims to align city assets with Toronto's climate action plan, TransformTO, by only allowing ads that meet specific environmental goals and have independently verified claims.

title: Official Plan Policy Amendments for Infrastructure Projects in Parks and Open Space Areas - Decision Report
Official Plan Policy Amendments for Infrastructure Projects in Parks and Open Space Areas - Decision Report
summary: Toronto City Council has approved new rules that will change how infrastructure projects, such as transit lines or utilities, can be built within the city's parks and green spaces. This decision affects the future use and protection of Toronto's public parkland by establishing new policies for these developments.

title: Protecting Community Health by Advocating for Supervised Consumption Services and Consumption and Treatment Services Sites - by Councillor Chris Moise, seconded by Councillor Alejandra Bravo
Protecting Community Health by Advocating for Supervised Consumption Services and Consumption and Treatment Services Sites - by Councillor Chris Moise, seconded by Councillor Alejandra Bravo
summary: Toronto City Council has urged the provincial government to continue funding all supervised consumption and treatment services beyond March 31, 2025. Council also requested that the province include these vital services in new homelessness and addiction recovery programs, emphasizing safety and improved access to treatment for community health and well-being.

title: Solve the Crisis: Support of the Ontario Big City Mayors Call for Action to Address the Growing Mental Health, Addictions and Homelessness Crisis - by Councillor Jennifer McKelvie, seconded by Mayor Olivia Chow
summary: Toronto City Council has formally endorsed the Ontario Big City Mayors' "Solve the Crisis" campaign, urging the provincial government to take immediate action on the growing mental health, addictions, and homelessness crisis. Council is asking the province to provide sustainable funding to municipalities, appoint a dedicated Minister, and invest in 24/7 community hubs and crisis centres. This move aims to push the province for a coordinated, funded strategy to address these critical issues affecting Toronto communities.

title: 2023-2024 Annual Progress Update Report - HousingTO 2020-2030 Action Plan and Housing Action Plan (2022-2026)
summary: City Council adopted a progress report on Toronto's housing plans, formally requesting billions in funding and partnerships from the provincial and federal governments. This effort aims to build 65,000 new rent-controlled homes by 2030 and provide stable housing and support for residents experiencing homelessness.

title: Council Direction on PH14.13 - Allowing Time for a Thorough Review
Council Direction on PH14.13 - Allowing Time for a Thorough Review
Council Direction on PH14.13 - Allowing Time for a Thorough Review
summary: Toronto City Council has decided to thoroughly review the rules for garden suites on properties along Parkmount Road that back onto Craven Road in the Danforth area. This detailed study will examine existing regulations, potential changes, and the impact of garden suites on the community, ensuring any future zoning amendments are well-considered before a final decision.

title: Billy Bishop Toronto City Airport - Runway End Safety Areas
Billy Bishop Toronto City Airport - Runway End Safety Areas
Billy Bishop Toronto City Airport - Runway End Safety Areas
summary: City Council approved extending its agreement with PortsToronto for Billy Bishop Airport, allowing for the construction of new runway safety areas that are critical for aviation safety. This project involves extending land into the lake and requires PortsToronto to meet environmental, community impact, and financing conditions. The agreement term can be extended until 2045 to secure the necessary funding, and the City will discuss updating the airport's master plan to align with broader waterfront goals.

title: Award of Doc4553928310 to GFL Environmental Inc., for Curbside Collection Services in District 2 for Solid Waste Management Services
summary: Toronto City Council has postponed making a decision on a contract to GFL Environmental Inc. for curbside waste collection services in District 2. This means that residents across several central and western wards will await a final choice on who will handle their garbage and recycling pickup, with the item sent back to a committee for further review.

title: Electric Ferries Shoreside Infrastructure Work Plan
summary: Toronto City Council has approved a work plan to upgrade the shoreside infrastructure at the Jack Layton Ferry Terminal, preparing it for the future electric ferry fleet. This involves an agreement with the Toronto Port Lands Company to manage the design and construction of these essential improvements, aiming to enhance the ferry service and facilities for residents.

title: 214-230 Sherbourne Street - Official Plan and Zoning By-law Amendment Application - Decision Report - Approval
committee: Toronto and East York Community Council
summary: City Council has approved zoning and official plan changes for the properties at 214-230 Sherbourne Street. This decision permits a new development project in the area, and Council also requested efforts to include affordable housing and allocate community funds from the project towards local neighbourhood revitalization.

title: Denrock Drive - Parking Amendments
summary: North York Community Council has approved new parking rules for Denrock Drive. The previous blanket 'no parking' restriction has been replaced with 'no standing' zones on most of the street, alongside a specific 17-meter section on the east side where vehicles can park for up to one hour at any time. These changes aim to improve traffic flow and manage vehicle stopping in the area.

title: Toronto Public Health 2025 Operating Budget Submission
summary: The Board of Health has adopted its proposed 2025 operating budget for Toronto Public Health, outlining how the city will fund essential public health services. This budget includes increased funding from the provincial government for health programs and the Healthy Babies Healthy Children Program, and calls for more federal and provincial investment in school food programs.

title: 70-86 Lynn Williams Street - Zoning By-law Amendment Application- Ontario Land Tribunal Hearing - Request for Directions
70-86 Lynn Williams Street - Zoning By-law Amendment Application- Ontario Land Tribunal Hearing - Request for Directions
summary: This item concerns a development proposal at 70-86 Lynn Williams Street. City Council approved a settlement with the developer regarding their zoning application, which was being heard at the Ontario Land Tribunal. As part of this agreement, the developer will provide a minimum 325 square metre park space on the property for public use.

title: 4195 Dundas Street West and 556, 558, 560, 562 and 564 Prince Edward Drive North - Official Plan Amendment and Zoning By-law Amendment Applications - Ontario Land Tribunal Hearing - Request for Directions
summary: Toronto City Council has approved a settlement for a development at 4195 Dundas Street West and nearby properties, resolving a dispute at the Ontario Land Tribunal. As part of this agreement, the developer will create and provide a new public park of at least 1,134 square metres to the city at no cost, offering more green space for local residents.

title: Advancing the Transformation of Toronto's Housing Delivery
summary: City Council recently received a detailed report outlining strategies to transform how housing is delivered across Toronto. By formally acknowledging this report for information, the city is signaling its ongoing commitment to improve housing construction and availability, potentially influencing future development policies.

title: Subway Agreement in Principle and Update on Metrolinx Subway Program - Third Quarter 2024
summary: Toronto City Council has approved a foundational agreement with the Province of Ontario to guide the ongoing subway program. This agreement sets the terms for how the city and province will work together on future subway expansion projects, ensuring continued progress on Toronto's transit network.

title: Call to Order
summary: City Council opened its July 24 and 25 meeting with traditional ceremonies. Members observed moments of silence for the discovery of unmarked graves near a former residential school and to remember recently deceased individuals, followed by a land acknowledgement.

title: Single Exit Stair: Ontario Building Code Feasibility Study
summary: Toronto City Council is asking provincial and national authorities to consider allowing single exit stairs in residential buildings up to six storeys to make mid-rise housing easier to build. The city will also create public guidelines for developers, aiming to increase housing options while ensuring safety for residents and first responders.

title: Welcome to Toronto, Taylor Swift! - by Councillor Jennifer McKelvie, seconded by Deputy Mayor Ausma Malik
summary: To celebrate Taylor Swift's upcoming concerts in November, City Council has approved temporarily renaming streets near the Rogers Centre as "Taylor Swift Way" and lighting the Toronto sign red. The signs will be donated to the Daily Bread Food Bank for auction after the event, with the costs covered by event organizers to avoid using taxpayer funds.

title: Jurisdictional Research on Municipal Fiscal Frameworks - by Councillor Lily Cheng, seconded by Mayor Olivia Chow
Jurisdictional Research on Municipal Fiscal Frameworks - by Councillor Lily Cheng, seconded by Mayor Olivia Chow
summary: City Council has approved a request to research how major cities worldwide are funded by their provincial or national governments. This study will help Toronto explore new ways to manage its finances and inform discussions about how the city gets funding, particularly as it prepares for the 2025 budget.

title: Declaring April 11 to 17, 2025 as Black Maternal Health Week in the City of Toronto - by Councillor Michael Thompson, seconded by Councillor Amber Morley
summary: Toronto City Council has officially declared April 11 to 17, 2025, as Black Maternal Health Week. This declaration aims to raise awareness about the health and well-being of Black mothers and birthing people, allowing organizers to proceed with planning community events and initiatives.

title: Authorization to Release Section 37 Funds towards Public Realm and Streetscape Improvements related to "Manor Road Square" as prescribed in Official Plan Amendment 405 as a City-initiated Public Realm Move - by Councillor Josh Matlow, seconded by Councillor Mike Colle
summary: City Council has approved funding to upgrade the public space around Manor Road Square, located west of Yonge Street. This investment of $318,600 will pay for streetscape improvements, making the area more enjoyable for residents. The funds come from community benefits collected from a nearby development, ensuring growth helps improve local neighbourhoods.

title: Process for Award of Negotiable Request for Proposal Document Number 4053424337 for the Construction of Two New Fully Electric Ferry Vessels
Process for Award of Negotiable Request for Proposal Document Number 4053424337 for the Construction of Two New Fully Electric Ferry Vessels
summary: Toronto City Council has approved moving forward with a contract to build two new fully electric ferries for service to the Toronto Islands. This decision also starts the planning process for necessary upgrades to the Jack Layton Ferry Terminal and the islands to support these new eco-friendly vessels, aiming for more reliable and sustainable island transportation.

title: Reducing Runoff and Mitigating Flooding - by Mayor Olivia Chow, seconded by Councillor Jennifer McKelvie
Reducing Runoff and Mitigating Flooding - by Mayor Olivia Chow, seconded by Councillor Jennifer McKelvie
summary: Toronto City Council has approved a plan to explore new ways to reduce flooding and better manage heavy rainfall across the city. This involves studying current and potential programs for homeowners to reduce runoff from their properties, increasing natural solutions like rain gardens in public spaces, and assessing how to reduce stormwater from commercial properties. The goal is to protect residents from flood damage and improve water quality.

title: Multi-Tenant Housing Framework: Parks Levy Exemption and Framework Application - by Councillor Alejandra Bravo, seconded by Councillor Chris Moise
summary: City Council recently approved changes to Toronto's Multi-Tenant Housing Framework. These updates will exempt certain types of multi-tenant housing (dwelling rooms) from needing to dedicate parkland and will clarify how the framework applies to temporary housing for refugees. This aims to remove hurdles for housing providers and help create more diverse and accessible housing options across the city, including better support for refugee housing initiatives.

title: Medical - Office Facilities/Affordable-Seniors Residential Project in Continuum of Care Setting - Request for Minister's Zoning Order at 4646 Dufferin Street
summary: City Council decided not to support a request for a provincial order that would fast-track a proposed medical facility and affordable seniors' housing project at 4646 Dufferin Street. This means the development will not bypass standard city planning processes and will need to follow the usual approval procedures.

title: Medical - Office Facilities/Affordable-Seniors Residential Project in Continuum of Care Setting - Request for Minister's Zoning Order at 4646 Dufferin Street
Delegation of Development Review Related Authorities
summary: Toronto City Council has shifted the authority for reviewing and approving development applications, including new subdivisions, condominiums, and site plans. These responsibilities were moved from the Chief Planner to the Executive Director of Development Review to help streamline how new construction projects are processed across the city.

title: Canada-wide Early Learning and Child Care Implementation Roundtable: Update
summary: Toronto City Council is advocating for the long-term stability and expansion of childcare services across the city. Council is urging the provincial government to reverse the cancellation of 48 previously approved childcare projects in Toronto schools, which would have created 3,083 new spaces, and to implement a sustainable funding formula for the Canada-wide Early Learning and Child Care system.

title: Toronto Island Park Master Plan
summary: City Council has approved a new Master Plan for Toronto Island Park, setting the vision for its improvements and development over the next 25 years. This plan will guide future capital and operating budgets to enhance visitor experience, infrastructure, and ensure continued engagement with Indigenous communities.

title: Building a Permanent Safety Barrier Along the Leaside Bridge to Prevent Suicide Attempts
Building a Permanent Safety Barrier Along the Leaside Bridge to Prevent Suicide Attempts
summary: To enhance public safety, City Council has asked city staff to study the possibility of installing a permanent barrier on the Leaside Bridge. This initiative aims to help prevent suicide attempts from the bridge, with a report on its feasibility expected by early 2025.

title: Advancing the Construction of Affordable Rental Homes at 777 Victoria Park Avenue
summary: Toronto City Council has approved plans to advance the construction of new affordable rental homes at 777 Victoria Park Avenue. To address concerns about lost commuter parking at the site, the City is exploring options for new paid parking nearby and at the Dentonia Park Golf Course.

title: 5051-5061 Yonge Street - Official Plan and Zoning Amendment Applications - Ontario Land Tribunal Hearing - Request for Directions
summary: City Council approved a settlement for a new building proposal at 5051-5061 Yonge Street, directing staff to support it at the Ontario Land Tribunal. A key part of this settlement removes the requirement for 333.71 square metres of affordable housing from the project.

title: Expanding Permitted Uses in Laneway and Garden Suites
summary: Toronto City Council is looking into expanding the types of housing allowed in laneway and garden suites, like group homes and multi-tenant homes, by requesting reviews of current zoning rules. This move could create more diverse housing options in residential areas across the city. Additionally, the Council decided to remove the option for new garden suites on properties along a specific section of Craven Road.

title: 39 Dundalk Drive Modular Housing
summary: Toronto City Council has reviewed an inquiry and response concerning the proposed modular housing project at 39 Dundalk Drive. This means Council considered information about the project without making any new decisions on its approval or funding.

title: 1053 Don Mills Road, 2, 4, 4R, and 6 The Donway East Zoning By-law Amendment - Request for Direction Report
summary: City Council has approved a settlement offer for a revised development proposal at 1053 Don Mills Road and surrounding properties. This decision allows a new plan for the area to move forward, subject to final approvals on zoning details and engineering requirements, which will impact local density and design.

title: Review of the Order Paper
summary: City Council formally approved the agenda for its July 24th and 25th meetings, setting the order for debating important issues like the Gardiner Expressway and accelerating housing development. This procedural decision determines which topics councillors will discuss and vote on, impacting various city services and projects.

title: Review of the Order Paper

Acceleration of the State of Good Repair Design-Build Contract for the F.G. Gardiner Expressway Rehabilitation Section 2 - Dufferin Street to Strachan Avenue and Enhanced Congestion Management Measures

Acceleration of the State of Good Repair Design-Build Contract for the F.G. Gardiner Expressway Rehabilitation Section 2 - Dufferin Street to Strachan Avenue and Enhanced Congestion Management Measures
summary: City Council has approved a plan to accelerate rehabilitation work on the Gardiner Expressway, specifically the section between Dufferin Street and Strachan Avenue. This decision aims to complete the major repairs faster, but will involve specific traffic changes and temporary closures of the eastbound Spadina off-ramp and an on-ramp from Lake Shore Boulevard West to manage congestion during construction.

title: Update on the Reallocation of Funding from the Provincial Upload of the F.G. Gardiner Expressway and Don Valley Parkway
summary: Toronto City Council approved a plan to reallocate funds that became available when the Province took over responsibility for the Gardiner Expressway and Don Valley Parkway. This means the money previously spent on the expressways will now be prioritized for investment in other city infrastructure projects starting with the 2025 budget.

title: 155 Antibes Drive - Zoning By-law Amendment Application - Decision Report - Approval
summary: City Council has approved changes to the building rules for 155 Antibes Drive, allowing for a new development.
