As part of this approval, the developer will provide a 1,050 square meter strip of land along the northern boundary for a new multi-use pathway for the community, adding public space and improving local connectivity.

title: Report on Councillor Brad Bradford's Use of Constituent Contact Information
summary: City Council adopted findings that Councillor Brad Bradford violated the Code of Conduct regarding his use of constituent contact information. As a result, Council formally reprimanded him, reinforcing the importance of ethical conduct for elected officials and maintaining public trust.

title: Report on Councillor Frances Nunziata's Use of Constituent Contact Information
summary: City Council agreed that Councillor Frances Nunziata violated the City's Code of Conduct regarding the use of constituent contact information. As a result, Council formally reprimanded the Councillor, upholding accountability for elected officials.

title: Governance, Community Benefits Plan, Legacy and Program Advisory Framework, FIFA Fan Festival
summary: Toronto City Council has approved a plan for the city to accept funds from corporations and donors to help cover the costs of hosting the 2026 FIFA World Cup. Additionally, the city will accelerate the planning and design for new dedicated transit priority lanes on Dufferin Street and Bathurst Street to improve public transportation.

title: Ontario Science Centre Update
Ontario Science Centre Update
Ontario Science Centre Update
summary: Toronto City Council has urged the Province of Ontario to reverse its decision to close the Ontario Science Centre and to make necessary urgent repairs to its building. Council also called for the Province to accept donations for immediate fixes and to establish a working group to review the original lease agreement and provincial maintenance obligations. This action aims to keep the Science Centre open and preserve a vital cultural and educational institution for residents.

title: 100 Thorncliffe Park Drive - Development Charges Complaint
summary: Toronto City Council granted a complaint concerning development charges for 100 Thorncliffe Park Drive, instructing city staff to refund $400,147.13. This decision means the city will return funds to a developer that were initially collected to help pay for infrastructure and services supporting new growth in the city.

title: Introduction of By-law
summary: Toronto's General Government Committee recently adopted By-law 885-2024 to officially confirm all the decisions it made at its July 2, 2024 meeting. This by-law is a procedural step that ensures previously approved actions and policies are legally binding.

title: Proposed Land Lease for the Somali Centre for Culture and Recreation
summary: The Executive Committee has deferred a decision on leasing city land at Buttonwood Park for a new Somali Centre for Culture and Recreation. Instead, city staff will continue negotiating the land lease, explore other potential sites, and conduct public consultations with residents before bringing the proposal back for further consideration. This means residents will have an opportunity to provide feedback on the future use of Buttonwood Park for a community centre.

title: 3291 Kingston Road and 2 and 4 Windy Ridge Drive - Rental Housing Demolition Application - Decision Report - Approval
summary: City Council has approved the demolition of eight rental homes at 3291 Kingston Road, but with strict conditions. The property owner must replace these units with eight new rental homes on site, maintaining them as rentals for 20 years. At least these new units must be offered at affordable rents for ten years, and current tenants must receive a relocation and assistance plan including the right to return.

title: 400 Broadview Avenue - Official Plan and Zoning By-law Amendment - Decision Report - Approval
committee: Toronto and East York Community Council
summary: Toronto City Council has approved the necessary planning changes to allow for a new long-term care facility at 400 Broadview Avenue. This decision paves the way for a new care home in the area and includes requests for the developer to work closely with local residents on construction management, like setting up a liaison committee and maintaining a clean site, to minimize neighborhood disruption.

title: 1875 Steeles Avenue West - Zoning By-law Amendment and Rental Housing Demolition and Conversion Applications - Decision Report - Approval
1875 Steeles Avenue West - Zoning By-law Amendment and Rental Housing Demolition and Conversion Applications - Decision Report - Approval
summary: City Council refused a development application at 1875 Steeles Avenue West that proposed changing zoning rules and demolishing or converting existing rental housing. This decision means the proposed project will not move forward, maintaining the current status of the property.

title: North York Civic Centre 2025 - Strategic Plan
summary: City Council has decided to create a detailed plan for how community spaces are used at the North York Civic Centre. This plan will focus on addressing local needs, ensuring affordability, and will involve input from residents and local school boards. This means the Civic Centre's community spaces could become more accessible and better meet the needs of North York residents.

title: Annual Winter Maintenance Report
summary: Toronto City Council has approved updated plans for how the city manages winter snow, including new service levels for clearing cycling paths. These changes also allow the General Manager of Transportation Services to temporarily close roads for up to 24 hours during major snow events to quickly clear them, aiming for more efficient snow removal across the city.

title: Community Housing Sector Modernization and Growth Strategy
summary: City Council adopted a strategy to modernize and grow Toronto's community housing sector. This includes new funding and service agreements to help providers with expiring mortgages repair and revitalize their homes, alongside a new policy for leasing City-owned land to non-profit and Indigenous housing groups. These changes aim to support long-term affordable housing for residents.

title: Jane Finch Community Development Plan 2024-2034
summary: Toronto City Council has approved a 10-year development plan for the Jane Finch community. This plan aims to guide long-term improvements in the area by fostering collaboration between City divisions, residents, and local businesses. It includes establishing a Community Partnership Circle and a Resident Leadership Circle to ensure ongoing local engagement and secure funding for various initiatives.

title: Jane Finch Secondary Plan and Urban Design Guidelines - Final Report
summary: City Council has adopted the new Jane Finch Secondary Plan and Urban Design Guidelines. This plan will guide future development in the Jane Finch area, outlining policies for new housing, public spaces, community facilities, and building heights to shape the neighbourhood's growth.

title: Impact of Bill 165 and Gas Utility Use of Public Property in Toronto
summary: Toronto City Council has asked the province for permission to charge gas utilities for their use of public property, a practice already common in other major Canadian cities. The goal is to collect fair payments that could help fund city projects to make infrastructure more resilient against climate change. The City also asked the Ontario Auditor General to investigate Bill 165 and its impact on Toronto residents.

title: Villiers Island Precinct - City-Initiated Official Plan and Zoning By-law Amendment - Decision Report - Approval
Villiers Island Precinct - City-Initiated Official Plan and Zoning By-law Amendment - Decision Report - Approval
summary: Toronto City Council approved a comprehensive set of plans and rules for developing Villiers Island, a major revitalization project in the Port Lands. This includes updating zoning and land-use guidelines, adopting a detailed development plan, and establishing a master plan for public art, all designed to transform the island into a vibrant new community.

title: De Havilland 'Mossie' Park Underground Storage Tank
summary: To help prevent basement flooding in the Winston Park Neighbourhood, City Council has decided to advance the upgrade of the underground storage tank at De Havilland 'Mossie' Park. Council also asked Toronto Water for an update on the detailed design and construction timeline for a related project that addresses local flooding.

title: 14 Lacewood Crescent - Request for City Solicitor to Attend at the Toronto Local Appeal Body - by Councillor Jon Burnside, seconded by Councillor James Pasternak
summary: The City has decided to defend its refusal of a development application for a property at 14 Lacewood Crescent. Council authorized its legal team to represent the City at an appeal hearing and to negotiate a resolution, aiming to uphold local planning rules and the character of the neighbourhood.

title: Honouring the Late Councillor Jaye Robinson by Naming a Landmark or Site in Don Valley West - by Councillor James Pasternak, seconded by Councillor Mike Colle
Honouring the Late Councillor Jaye Robinson by Naming a Landmark or Site in Don Valley West - by Councillor James Pasternak, seconded by Councillor Mike Colle
summary: City Council approved a plan to honour the late Councillor Jaye Robinson by asking staff to identify suitable parks, streets, or other landmarks in Don Valley West to name after her. This decision recognizes her extensive public service and contributions to the city, with a report on potential locations expected by the end of 2024.

title: Requesting Alignment of the Independent Electricity System Operator Integrated Regional Resource Plan with the City's 2040 Target of Net Zero Greenhouse Gas Emissions - by Councillor Dianne Saxe, seconded by Councillor Paula Fletcher

Requesting Alignment of the Independent Electricity System Operator Integrated Regional Resource Plan with the City's 2040 Target of Net Zero Greenhouse Gas Emissions - by Councillor Dianne Saxe, seconded by Councillor Paula Fletcher
summary: Toronto City Council has urged Ontario's electricity system operator to align its energy planning for the city with Toronto's goal of achieving net-zero greenhouse gas emissions by 2040. This means phasing out gas-fired electricity generation at the Portlands Energy Centre by 2035 and boosting local renewable energy sources to ensure a reliable and affordable clean power supply for residents.

title: Request to Implement Measures for Mandatory Lead Disclosure and Mitigation in Multi-Residential Housing - by Councillor Dianne Saxe, seconded by Deputy Mayor Ausma Malik
summary: A proposal calling for mandatory lead disclosure and mitigation in multi-residential housing was sent to the Planning and Housing Committee for further review. This initiative aims to protect residents from lead exposure, particularly in older apartment buildings, by requiring landlords to address potential health risks.

title: Fostering Belonging, Community and Inclusion, and Combating Hate in Toronto - by Councillor James Pasternak, seconded by Mayor Olivia Chow, Deputy Mayor Ausma Malik, Councillor Mike Colle, Councillor Jennifer McKelvie, and Councillor Amber Morley
summary: Toronto City Council has adopted measures to combat hate and foster a more inclusive city. This includes re-launching a public education campaign, reviewing urban design for safety, improving rapid response to hate graffiti, and exploring future funding for community gathering spaces like gardens and benches.

title: An Update on Work to Protect the Science Centre and Support Thorncliffe and Flemingdon Park - by Councillor Josh Matlow, seconded by Councillor Jon Burnside
An Update on Work to Protect the Science Centre and Support Thorncliffe and Flemingdon Park - by Councillor Josh Matlow, seconded by Councillor Jon Burnside
An Update on Work to Protect the Science Centre and Support Thorncliffe and Flemingdon Park - by Councillor Josh Matlow, seconded by Councillor Jon Burnside
summary: Toronto City Council is exploring options to keep the Ontario Science Centre at its current location and support nearby communities. They've asked city staff to assess if the city could operate the Science Centre and to connect with partners offering to fund urgent roof repairs. This move aims to preserve a valuable public institution and ensure community input on its future.

title: Towards a Beautiful City - by Councillor Josh Matlow, seconded by Councillor Parthi Kandavel
Towards a Beautiful City - by Councillor Josh Matlow, seconded by Councillor Parthi Kandavel
summary: Toronto City Council has requested a detailed plan to enhance the beauty and design quality of public spaces across the city. This initiative aims to create consistent design standards for streets, parks, and new private developments, and will explore establishing a City Designer's office to oversee these improvements, impacting how residents experience their neighbourhoods.

title: Toronto International Pizza Festival - by Councillor Mike Colle, seconded by Councillor Frances Nunziata
summary: City Council recently discussed a proposal to establish a Toronto International Pizza Festival. Rather than approving it directly, they sent the idea to the Economic and Community Development Committee for further review, meaning residents will need to wait for the committee's decision before knowing if the city will support a new festival.

title: Toronto International Pizza Festival - by Councillor Mike Colle, seconded by Councillor Frances Nunziata
222 Rosedale Heights Drive - Request for City Solicitor to Attend at the Toronto Local Appeal Body - by Councillor Dianne Saxe, seconded by Councillor Alejandra Bravo
summary: Toronto City Council has decided to challenge a proposed development at 222 Rosedale Heights Drive. They are directing the City Solicitor to appeal a previous decision and represent the City's opposition at the Toronto Local Appeal Body. This action ensures the city's legal team will work to prevent a project that may not align with community interests.

title: A Governance and Operational Review of Sankofa Square (formerly Yonge-Dundas Square)
A Governance and Operational Review of Sankofa Square (formerly Yonge-Dundas Square)
A Governance and Operational Review of Sankofa Square (formerly Yonge-Dundas Square)
summary: City Council formally adopted the name change of Yonge-Dundas Square to Sankofa Square. This decision also directs the new Sankofa Square Board to create a multi-year plan for its revitalization, focusing on enhancing programming, safety, and exploring new funding to make the space more vibrant and accessible for residents.

title: The City's Encampment Approach and Strategy
The City's Encampment Approach and Strategy
summary: Toronto City Council has adopted an updated protocol for managing encampments in city parks and public spaces. This strategy aims to prioritize broad public access to parks while directing staff to connect people experiencing homelessness to housing and support services. It also urges provincial and federal governments to provide more funding and create a coordinated regional approach to homelessness and asylum seeker arrivals, specifically requesting a $54 million housing benefit allocation.

title: Review of the Order Paper
summary: City Council formalized the agenda for its June 26 and 27 meetings, approving the list of items that would be debated. This decision determined the key issues, such as housing policy, that councillors would discuss and vote on during these sessions.

title: Supporting the City of Toronto in Joining the Strong Cities Network and Hosting a Future Strong Cities Summit in October 2025 - by Councillor James Pasternak, seconded by Councillor Brad Bradford
Supporting the City of Toronto in Joining the Strong Cities Network and Hosting a Future Strong Cities Summit in October 2025 - by Councillor James Pasternak, seconded by Councillor Brad Bradford
summary: Toronto will apply to join the Strong Cities Network, an international group focused on making communities safer and more resilient against extremism. City staff will also study the possibility of Toronto hosting a global summit for this network in October 2025, and City Council is asking the provincial and federal governments for financial help to do so.

title: Strengthening and Improving the City-Wide Approach to Youth Violence Interventions - by Councillor Vincent Crisanti, seconded by Councillor Nick Mantas
summary: Toronto City Council has approved steps to strengthen its city-wide approach to preventing youth violence. The plan involves requesting updates on existing community programs, improving youth outreach methods, and engaging community organizations to share best practices for gun violence reduction efforts.

title: Request to Rename an Existing or Planned Community Garden After the Late Estella Wheeler - by Councillor James Pasternak, seconded by Councillor Chris Moise
summary: City Council has approved a request to explore naming a community garden after the late Estella Wheeler. City staff will now review options for renaming an existing garden or dedicating a new one in her honour, and report back with recommendations by the end of 2024.

title: Modular Housing Initiative Phase 2: Update Report, and Amendments to Purchase Order Numbers 6052027 and 6052881 issued to NRB Inc. for Design-Build Services for Supportive Housing
summary: Toronto officials have authorized amendments to contracts with NRB Inc. for the construction of supportive housing at 175 Cummer Avenue and 39 Dundalk Drive. This decision helps advance the city's Modular Housing Initiative, which provides essential homes and supports for people experiencing homelessness.

title: Cycling Network Plan Update (2025 - 2027)
Cycling Network Plan Update (2025 - 2027)
Cycling Network Plan Update (2025 - 2027)
summary: Toronto City Council has approved an update to the city's Cycling Network Plan for 2025-2027. This decision will guide the design and implementation of new bikeway projects across the city, including studies for major city-wide routes, to improve cycling infrastructure for residents.

title: Cycling Network Plan -  2024 Cycling Infrastructure and Missing Sidewalk Installation - Third Quarter Update
Cycling Network Plan -  2024 Cycling Infrastructure and Missing Sidewalk Installation - Third Quarter Update
summary: Toronto City Council has approved the latest plans to expand the city's cycling network and install missing sidewalks in several areas. This decision will create more dedicated bike lanes and improve pedestrian safety, making it easier and safer for residents to get around actively.

title: 135 Roxborough Drive - Alterations to a Designated Heritage Property in the North Rosedale Heritage Conservation District
summary: A local heritage property at 135 Roxborough Drive cannot be altered as proposed, specifically regarding painting its masonry. City Council rejected the changes to protect the house's heritage features, and the city's legal team will defend this decision if the property owner appeals.

title: Declaring the Office of Councillor, Ward 15 - Don Valley West Vacant and Filling the Vacancy
summary: City Council has declared the Councillor position for Ward 15 - Don Valley West vacant and decided to hold a by-election to fill it. This means residents in Ward 15 will soon get to vote for their new representative. The by-election is expected to cost $550,000, which will be covered by a city reserve fund.

title: Renovictions Policy Implementation: Review of Hamilton Renovation Licence and Tenant Relocation By-law and developing a Toronto Renovictions By-law
Renovictions Policy Implementation: Review of Hamilton Renovation Licence and Tenant Relocation By-law and developing a Toronto Renovictions By-law
summary: Toronto City Council has taken a step towards protecting tenants from "renovictions" by directing staff to develop a new by-law. This proposed policy would require landlords to get a renovation licence and provide a tenant accommodation and compensation plan before evicting residents for major renovations. The goal is to prevent landlords from unfairly removing tenants and then re-renting at much higher rates.

title: 2444 Eglinton Avenue East - Zoning Amendment - Decision Report - Approval
summary: City Council approved a zoning change for the property at 2444 Eglinton Avenue East, allowing for new housing development. They also requested an analysis to find ways to replace the commuter parking lot that will be lost due to this project.

title: On-Street Logistics Mini-Hub on St. George Street -  Pilot Update and Next Steps
summary: Toronto City Council has approved a permanent program for "On-Street Logistics Mini-Hubs," allowing courier companies to apply for designated street parking spaces to sort packages and make deliveries. This aims to reduce traffic congestion and improve delivery efficiency across the city, with companies paying an annual fee for each parking spot utilized. The first permanent hub will be on St. George Street, operated by Purolator.

title: 300 Borough Drive, 1755 Brimley Road, 400 to 580 Progress Avenue and 350 Town Centre Court - Ontario Land Tribunal Hearing - Request for Directions
summary: City Council accepted a settlement proposal for significant development projects in Scarborough Centre, specifically for properties like 300 Borough Drive. This agreement determines the city's stance on future growth in these areas, and will be presented to the Ontario Land Tribunal for final approval.

title: Launching the Rental Housing Supply Program
Launching the Rental Housing Supply Program
summary: Toronto City Council has approved a new program designed to increase the supply of affordable rental homes across the city. This initiative will offer financial incentives, like waiving certain development fees, to eligible rental housing projects and provide funding to community groups to help plan new housing developments, ultimately creating more rent-controlled and affordable options for residents.

title: Advancing the Homelessness Services Capital Infrastructure Strategy (HSCIS) and the 2025 Shelter Infrastructure Plan
Advancing the Homelessness Services Capital Infrastructure Strategy (HSCIS) and the 2025 Shelter Infrastructure Plan
Advancing the Homelessness Services Capital Infrastructure Strategy (HSCIS) and the 2025 Shelter Infrastructure Plan
summary: Toronto City Council has approved an initial $89.5 million to start a new strategy for homelessness services infrastructure, focusing on acquiring properties for shelters and support. The city is also asking the federal government for an additional $674.5 million over ten years to help fund this long-term plan, which aims to improve and expand services for people experiencing homelessness.

title: Moss Park Arena - Governance and Operational Review
Moss Park Arena - Governance and Operational Review
summary: City Council has decided to take over the management of Moss Park Arena, transitioning it from an independent Board of Management to being directly operated by the City. This change, expected by the end of 2025, aims to improve community access to arena programs and services, with city staff tasked to develop a detailed transition plan that includes financial impacts.

title: Spinal Cord Injury Ontario Presentation - Accessible Taxi and Vehicle for Hire Services
summary: The Toronto Accessibility Advisory Committee recently received a presentation from Spinal Cord Injury Ontario. This presentation highlighted the challenges people with disabilities face using accessible taxi and vehicle-for-hire services, and provided recommendations to improve these services city-wide. The committee received this information to consider, but no specific decisions or actions were taken.

title: Housing Action Plan: As-of-Right Zoning for Mid-rise Buildings on Avenues and Updated Rear Transition Performance Standards - Proposals Report
summary: Toronto's Planning and Housing Committee is moving ahead with plans to make it easier to build mid-rise buildings along major streets, known as Avenues. This involves drafting new zoning rules to allow for taller and denser buildings, as well as updating urban design standards, all aimed at creating more housing across the city. The committee also asked for a review of specific planning rules for garden suites on Craven Road.

title: Brimley Road and McNicoll Avenue - Intersection Safety Review
summary: City Council has approved a new traffic rule prohibiting eastbound right turns on a red light at the intersection of Brimley Road and McNicoll Avenue. This change aims to improve safety for all road users, including pedestrians, cyclists, and drivers, especially given that TTC transit operates through this busy Scarborough intersection.

title: 4201 to 4203 Kingston Road and 120 Galloway Road -  Zoning Amendment Application - Decision Report - Approval
summary: City Council approved a zoning change for properties on Kingston Road and Galloway Road, allowing for new development. This decision ensures that 80 existing rental units will be preserved for at least 20 years and requires the owner to make significant improvements to these buildings at no cost to tenants. A plan to reduce construction impacts on current residents is also mandated.

title: 1911 Finch Avenue West (Jane Finch Mall) - Official Plan Amendment and Zoning Amendment Applications - Decision Report - Approval
summary: Toronto City Council approved changes to the Official Plan and zoning rules for the Jane Finch Mall site at 1911 Finch Avenue West. This decision paves the way for a major redevelopment of the property, which will include new housing and aims to bring affordable rental housing and community services to the Jane Finch neighbourhood.

title: 2023 Write-Off of Uncollectible Property Taxes and Accounts Receivable
summary: Toronto City Council has decided to remove nearly $9 million in uncollectible funds from its financial records. This includes outstanding property taxes from 182 accounts and over $6 million in Provincial Offences Act fines. This action helps the city clean up its books by acknowledging amounts it is no longer able to collect.

title: Litigation Update Regarding Option to Purchase Retail Component of Development at 935 Eglinton Avenue West (Car Park 655)
summary: The Toronto Parking Authority's Board has decided to keep confidential the legal advice regarding its ongoing lawsuit. The lawsuit seeks $7 million in damages from a developer for a broken contract concerning TPA's option to buy a retail unit at 935 Eglinton Avenue West.

title: Lease Arrears at 50 Cumberland Street, Units 11 and 12
summary: The Toronto Parking Authority Board approved confidential instructions to staff regarding a legal dispute. This dispute concerns unpaid rent for units at 50 Cumberland Street, with the details being kept private due to ongoing or potential litigation affecting the authority's property leases.

title: Summary of Legal Disputes - 2024 First Quarter Update
summary: Toronto's Parking Authority Board recently received an update on various ongoing legal disputes related to its operations, including commercial parking, property management, and Bike Share Toronto. These matters involve issues like supplier disagreements and personal injuries, which the Authority manages with insurance and legal counsel. The specific details of these legal cases remain confidential to protect the city's interests.

title: Ridgewood Road Sanitary Sewer Update
summary: Toronto's Infrastructure Committee approved an update on plans for a new sanitary sewer on Ridgewood Road, directing staff to minimize impacts on properties and trees. Residents will be actively involved throughout 2025 with mailed notices, walkabouts, and public meetings to discuss the design and timelines for the sewer, which will be paid for by property owners through a special charge.

title: 2024 Toronto Urban Health Fund Allocations and Review Process
summary: The Board of Health approved grants totaling over $2.5 million from the Toronto Urban Health Fund to 24 community projects focusing on HIV prevention, substance use, and harm reduction. This funding supports essential health services for residents, especially those facing linguistic, cultural, or geographic barriers. The city will also work with Indigenous groups to redesign how health funds are allocated to Indigenous-led initiatives.

title: 2024 Toronto Urban Health Fund Allocations and Review Process
824 Sheppard Avenue West, 177, 179 and 181 Cocksfield Avenue - Zoning Amendment  and Rental Housing Demolition and Conversion Application - Decision Report - Approval
summary: New zoning was approved for properties at 824 Sheppard Avenue West and Cocksfield Avenue to permit a new development. To support housing, the developer must provide fifteen replacement rental units on the site, all of which must be maintained at affordable rents for at least ten years.

title: A Micromobility Strategy for Toronto
A Micromobility Strategy for Toronto
A Micromobility Strategy for Toronto
summary: Toronto City Council has decided to allow low-speed vehicles (LSVs) on city streets as part of a provincial pilot project, but will not permit them to be used as taxis or ride-share vehicles. This means residents might see new types of small vehicles like golf carts used for local trips or deliveries, while electric kick-scooters remain prohibited in the city.

title: 1000 Mural Graffiti Removal Blitz
summary: Toronto City Council is looking into creating 1,000 new murals across the city and exploring new graffiti removal blitzes. They asked city staff to report on expanding the StreetARToronto program with community partners and to update on city-wide efforts to prevent vandalism and beautify public spaces.

title: The Future of Visitor Parking - by Councillor Lily Cheng, seconded by Councillor Jennifer McKelvie
summary: City Council has sent a proposal about the future of visitor parking to the Planning and Housing Committee for further review. This means the committee will study potential changes to how visitor parking operates across the city, which could affect residents and their guests.

title: Equitable Funding and Distribution for Canada Day Events in All Community Council Areas for 2025 - by Councillor Lily Cheng, seconded by Councillor Jennifer McKelvie
summary: To ensure Canada Day celebrations are accessible city-wide, Council asked city staff to propose a plan for fairly funding and distributing these events across all Toronto neighborhoods for 2025. This decision aims to make sure every part of the city has equitable access to community celebrations.

title: Strengthening Our Cultural Event Investment Throughout the City - by Councillor Lily Cheng, seconded by Councillor Jennifer McKelvie
summary: City Council has asked city staff to prepare a report on how cultural events are currently funded and distributed across Toronto. This report will also include a plan to boost funding for cultural events in growing neighbourhoods outside the downtown core, aiming to ensure more residents have access to local cultural programming.

title: Request for Review of Governance and Operations for Moss Park Arena - by Councillor Chris Moise, seconded by Councillor Paula Fletcher
Request for Review of Governance and Operations for Moss Park Arena - by Councillor Chris Moise, seconded by Councillor Paula Fletcher
summary: Toronto City Council has decided to study whether Moss Park Arena should be run directly by the City instead of its current Board of Management. This review will look into the financial costs, operational changes, and staffing needs, which could affect how the arena serves the community and its users.

title: Requesting the Province to Protect Small Businesses and Support Vibrant, Affordable, and Successful Mainstreets - by Councillor Josh Matlow, seconded by Councillor Mike Colle
Requesting the Province to Protect Small Businesses and Support Vibrant, Affordable, and Successful Mainstreets - by Councillor Josh Matlow, seconded by Councillor Mike Colle
summary: Toronto City Council has formally asked the Ontario government to implement commercial rent control for small, locally-owned businesses. This move aims to protect main street shops, making them more affordable and stable by also requesting a dispute resolution tribunal and standardized leases for commercial tenants and landlords.

title: Expanding Housing Options in Neighbourhoods: Major Streets Study - Final Report
Expanding Housing Options in Neighbourhoods: Major Streets Study - Final Report
Expanding Housing Options in Neighbourhoods: Major Streets Study - Final Report
Expanding Housing Options in Neighbourhoods: Major Streets Study - Final Report
summary: City Council approved major changes to the city's Official Plan and zoning rules to allow more housing options along many main streets across Toronto. This decision aims to increase housing supply by permitting a wider range of building types, though some specific areas were excluded from these new rules due to unique local conditions.

title: 1400 Gerrard Street East - Zoning By-law Amendment Application - Decision Report - Approval
summary: Toronto City Council has approved changes to the local building rules for 1400 Gerrard Street East. This decision allows for a new development at this site, but the owner must first address city requirements for engineering, waste, and stormwater management. Council also requested a review of how the new development could impact local street parking for residents.

title: Advancement of Professional Women's Soccer in Toronto, and Seeking Proposals for Lamport Stadium - by Deputy Mayor Ausma Malik, seconded by Councillor Jaye Robinson
summary: To boost professional women's soccer and improve local sports facilities, Toronto City Council has decided to negotiate agreements for two key sites. The city will work with Association Football Club Toronto for the use of the future Centennial Park soccer field, and will also seek proposals for a new operator for Lamport Stadium. These steps aim to attract capital investment for stadium upgrades, support professional women's sports, and ensure ongoing community access to recreation.

title: eglintonTOday Phase 1 Complete Street Project: Bicknell Avenue to Mount Pleasant Road
eglintonTOday Phase 1 Complete Street Project: Bicknell Avenue to Mount Pleasant Road
summary: Toronto City Council has approved major upgrades for Eglinton Avenue, creating a "complete street" from Bicknell Avenue to Mount Pleasant Road. This project aims to improve travel for drivers, transit riders, and pedestrians, and also includes new bike lanes on nearby streets to enhance cycling options.

title: Taking Congestion Seriously - A Plan to Accelerate Construction of the Gardiner Expressway - by Councillor Brad Bradford, seconded by Councillor Jon Burnside
summary: Toronto's City Council has requested a detailed plan to speed up ongoing rehabilitation work on the Gardiner Expressway. This plan, due by July, will explore options like 24/7 construction and off-site pre-fabrication to reduce traffic congestion and complete necessary repairs faster, while also considering impacts on nearby communities and other road projects.

title: 1095 and 1111 Danforth Avenue - Zoning By-law Amendment Application - Decision Report - Approval
summary: City Council has approved updated zoning rules for new construction at 1095 and 1111 Danforth Avenue. This decision includes a request to explore excluding the new development from permit parking, and directs any developer fees from the project towards creating affordable housing in Ward 14.

title: 1117 Danforth Avenue - Zoning By-law Amendment and Rental Housing Demolition Applications - Decision Report - Approval
summary: New building rules for 1117 Danforth Avenue have been approved by City Council, permitting the demolition of 27 social housing units. This approval mandates that the developer build an equal number of replacement social housing units on the site, which must be kept affordable for decades, while also assisting current tenants.

title: Official Plan Amendment 573 - Danforth Avenue - Planning Study (Segment 2 - Don Valley to Coxwell Avenue)
summary: Toronto City Council has directed city staff to advocate for changes to planning rules for a segment of Danforth Avenue, specifically from the Don Valley to Coxwell Avenue. These changes would allow for taller buildings, reduced setbacks, and potentially more shadows, but only if new developments include affordable housing, exceed green building standards, and don't negatively impact public spaces or surrounding neighbourhoods. This approach will be proposed during a legal appeal related to the area's official plan, potentially shaping future development along this stretch of Danforth.

title: Publicly Providing Receipts Before City Council Consideration of Expenses under the Members of Council Operations Policy - by Councillor Paula Fletcher, seconded by Councillor Jon Burnside
summary: City Council has approved changes to its operations policy, enhancing transparency for residents. For any expenses that require Council approval, the City Clerk will now publicly provide all supporting documentation, including receipts, as part of the agenda item.

title: Requesting the Province to Create Safety Zones Around Places of Worship, Faith-Based Schools and Vulnerable Community Social Infrastructure - by Councillor Brad Bradford, seconded by Councillor James Pasternak
summary: Toronto City Council has directed city staff to create a plan aimed at protecting residents' rights to practice their faith and gather freely around places of worship and community centres. This initiative seeks to ensure Torontonians can access these spaces without experiencing intimidation or harassment, and will involve relevant city departments and the police.

title: Yonge-Dundas Square Renaming Initiative
summary: Toronto City Council has decided to send the discussion about renaming Yonge-Dundas Square to its Executive Committee. This means the proposal will be reviewed by that committee first, postponing a final decision by the full Council on whether the square's name will change.

title: Report Back Regarding Item GG8.20 and Revisions to the Fair Wage Policy and Updating the Fair Wage Schedule to Include 2022-2024 Wage Rates
summary: Toronto City Council recently updated its Fair Wage Policy and the official schedule of wage rates for various trades working on city contracts. This update, effective July 1, 2024, ensures that workers performing services or construction for the City are paid fair and competitive wages. It aims to support local employment and the consistent delivery of quality city services.

title: Request a Review of Zoning Regulations to Restrict Outdoor Parking of Commercially Licensed Vehicles on Private Property in Residential Areas
summary: Toronto City Council has requested a review of rules for parking work vehicles outdoors on private property in residential areas. This review will consider the needs of vehicle operators to park near their homes and the impacts of COVID-19 on work arrangements, with a report expected by early 2025.

title: City Comments on Proposed Bill 185 - Cutting Red Tape to Build More Homes Act, 2024, Provincial Planning Statement 2024, and New Minister Zoning Order Framework
City Comments on Proposed Bill 185 - Cutting Red Tape to Build More Homes Act, 2024, Provincial Planning Statement 2024, and New Minister Zoning Order Framework
City Comments on Proposed Bill 185 - Cutting Red Tape to Build More Homes Act, 2024, Provincial Planning Statement 2024, and New Minister Zoning Order Framework
summary: Toronto City Council has provided feedback to the provincial government regarding a new bill and planning rules designed to speed up home construction. The City specifically urged the Province not to broadly limit the public's ability to appeal development decisions, unless the project is for social or affordable housing. This aims to protect community input on new buildings while still supporting housing initiatives.

title: City of Toronto's 2024 Corporate Asset Management Plan
summary: Toronto City Council has adopted its 2024 Corporate Asset Management Plan. This plan outlines how the city will strategically manage, maintain, and invest in its vast network of public infrastructure, ensuring city assets like roads, buildings, and parks continue to serve residents effectively.

title: Update Downsview: Secondary Plan, Zoning By-law, Urban Design Guidelines and Master Environmental Servicing Plan - Final Report
summary: Toronto City Council has approved the Downsview Secondary Plan, guiding the future development of the Downsview area. This comprehensive plan includes requirements for new housing, with targets for affordable rental housing increasing by 1.5% each year, as well as a minimum of 3 hectares of new public parkland and new childcare centres, shaping a vibrant new community.

title: Downsview Community Development Plan
summary: Toronto City Council has approved the Downsview Community Development Plan and a related 5-year action plan. This means future growth and services in the Downsview area will be guided by this framework, with a focus on partnering with diverse community groups and residents for implementation.

title: 2024 Local Arts Service Organization (LASO) Grant Allocation
summary: Toronto City Council has approved nearly $1.9 million in grants for six local arts organizations across the city. This funding will support arts programs that reach residents in various communities, with a new focus on engaging low-income Torontonians, and future plans to ensure fair funding distribution for these groups.

title: 2 Hawksdale Road - Application to Remove a Private Tree
summary: North York Community Council has denied a request to remove a healthy Colorado blue spruce tree at 2 Hawksdale Road. This decision upholds Toronto's Tree By-laws, which protect healthy trees even when they conflict with a planned driveway or alleged allergies, meaning the tree will remain.

title: 123 Wynford Drive - Refusal of Application for Alterations to the Attributes and Demolition of a Building and Heritage Attributes on a Designated Heritage Property Under Part IV, Sections 33 and 34 of the Ontario Heritage Act
summary: Toronto City Council has decided to reject a developer's plan to demolish and alter parts of the designated heritage property at 123 Wynford Drive. This decision protects the culturally and architecturally significant former Japanese Canadian Cultural Centre, designed by Raymond Moriyama, from a proposed residential development. The City will also explore nominating the site as a UNESCO World Heritage Site.

title: Immediate Actions to Address the 2023 Taxation Year of the Vacant Home Tax
Immediate Actions to Address the 2023 Taxation Year of the Vacant Home Tax
summary: Toronto City Council has waived the penalty fee for residents who missed the 2023 Vacant Home Tax declaration deadline, and those who paid it will receive a refund. For properties that didn't declare their status, the city will use their 2022 occupancy status for 2023, sending new notices that still allow homeowners to correct the information. The Council also directed staff to report back on a completely redesigned Vacant Home Tax program for 2024, focusing on better communication and user experience to improve the system.

title: Portlands Energy Centre: Request for Environmental Assessment - by Councillor Paula Fletcher, seconded by Councillor Dianne Saxe
Portlands Energy Centre: Request for Environmental Assessment - by Councillor Paula Fletcher, seconded by Councillor Dianne Saxe
summary: Toronto City Council has asked the provincial Minister of Environment, Conservation and Parks to require a comprehensive environmental assessment for Atura Power's proposal. This request is for a thorough review of plans to expand the Portlands Energy Centre's capacity by 50 megawatts, ensuring potential impacts on the environment and local residents are carefully studied.

title: 1111 Danforth Avenue - Notice of Intention to Designate a Property under Part IV, Section 29 of the Ontario Heritage Act
summary: City Council recently reviewed a proposal to protect 1111 Danforth Avenue as a heritage property, which would safeguard its historical or architectural features from significant changes. However, Council decided to take no further action on this proposal, meaning the property will not be designated for heritage protection by the city at this time.

title: Cycling Network Plan: 2024 Cycling Infrastructure Installation - Second Quarter Update and Missing Sidewalk Program - 2024 Local Road Sidewalk Installations
summary: Toronto City Council has approved the installation of new bike lanes and cycling infrastructure on several streets across the city. This expansion of the cycling network aims to provide safer and more convenient travel options for residents in multiple neighbourhoods. The General Manager of Transportation Services was also given authority to manage these projects as they are implemented.

title: RentSafeTO Program Update Report
summary: Toronto City Council has updated its RentSafeTO program to improve living conditions in apartment buildings and clarify responsibilities for landlords. The changes include new requirements for pest control logs, ensuring tenant notice boards are clear, and allowing the city to charge landlords for targeted visits and remedial inspections. These updates aim to better protect tenants and ensure landlords maintain their buildings.

title: Alcohol in Parks Pilot: Evaluation Findings
Alcohol in Parks Pilot: Evaluation Findings
summary: Following a pilot program, Toronto City Council has permanently approved allowing personal alcohol consumption in 45 specific parks across the city. This means residents can now legally drink alcohol in these public spaces, with certain health and safety restrictions in place.

title: The Somali Centre for Culture and Recreation
The Somali Centre for Culture and Recreation
summary: Toronto City Council has approved moving forward with plans for a Somali Centre for Culture and Recreation, requesting financial support from the Provincial and Federal governments. City staff will negotiate an agreement and identify potential sites, also exploring opportunities to include affordable housing in the city's northwest.

title: 2024 Education Property Tax Levy and Clawback Rate By-Law
2024 Education Property Tax Levy and Clawback Rate By-Law
summary: City Council has set the property tax rates that fund education in Toronto for 2024, as required by provincial regulations. These rates will generate over $2.15 billion for school purposes, with the City retaining a small portion of these funds. This decision directly impacts the education portion of property tax bills for Toronto homeowners and businesses.

title: Re-opening and Amending Item 2023.TE7.91- Altering Turning Restrictions at Old Forest Hill Road and Eglinton Avenue West - by Councillor Mike Colle, seconded by Councillor Frances Nunziata
summary: City Council has reinstated the northbound left-turn restrictions at the intersection of Old Forest Hill Road and Eglinton Avenue West during weekday morning and afternoon rush hours. This decision reverses an earlier one and aims to better manage traffic flow for residents in the area. The city will also study local neighbourhood traffic and install advance warning signs for drivers.

title: Our Plan Toronto: Official Plan Chapter One - Decision Report - Approval
summary: Toronto City Council has approved the first chapter of "Our Plan Toronto," the city's updated master plan that guides future development and land use. This foundational chapter sets the vision and principles for how the city will grow, including new directives to prioritize reducing fossil fuel use and address anti-Black racism, gentrification, and displacement in future planning decisions.

title: Large Multi-Passenger Pedalled Vehicles (Quadricycles) Pilot
summary: City Council has extended its pilot program for large multi-passenger pedalled vehicles, or 'pedicabs,' until 2032, allowing more of these vehicles to operate with updated rules. The city will now issue an unlimited number of annual licenses and will create a public list of streets where these pedicabs are not allowed to help manage traffic and ensure safety.

title: Response to Including New Approval Conditions for Rental Demolition Applications
Response to Including New Approval Conditions for Rental Demolition Applications
Response to Including New Approval Conditions for Rental Demolition Applications
summary: Toronto City Council has adopted new rules to better protect tenants when rental buildings are demolished for redevelopment. The city will now use more up-to-date and indexed market rent data to calculate financial assistance for displaced tenants, ensuring they receive fair compensation. Council also reiterated its request for the Province of Ontario to reinstate rent control for all rental units and uphold the city's authority to mandate replacement rental housing.

title: 30 Lakeside Avenue - Request for City Solicitor to Attend at the Toronto Local Appeal Body - by Councillor Councillor Parthi Kandavel, seconded by Councillor Mike Colle
summary: City Council has approved directing the city's legal team to represent its position before an independent tribunal regarding a property at 30 Lakeside Avenue. The lawyers will argue to uphold a local planning decision that rejected a development proposal for the site, and are also authorized to negotiate a resolution. This ensures the city actively defends local planning choices that can affect neighborhood development.

title: 171-175 Lowther Avenue - Official Plan and Zoning By-law Amendment Application - Decision Report - Approval
summary: City Council approved changes to planning rules for 171-175 Lowther Avenue, paving the way for a new development. This approval includes requirements for the owner to provide a tenant relocation plan with financial compensation for three existing rental units and to dedicate off-site parkland to the city.

title: Longbourne Drive - Introduction of Overnight On-Street Permit Parking Area 11
summary: Toronto City Council has approved new parking rules for Longbourne Drive. Residents living on the south side of the street between Willowridge Road and Redgrave Drive will now need a permit to park overnight from 12:01 a.m. to 7:00 a.m., with some exceptions. Parking is now banned at all times on the north side of the street, changing how residents can park near their homes.

title: Burnhamthorpe Road - Speed Limit Amendment
summary: Drivers on a section of Burnhamthorpe Road will need to slow down as the city has reduced the speed limit from 50 km/h to 40 km/h. This change, applying between Kipling Avenue and Dundas Street West, aims to make the road safer for all residents, including pedestrians and cyclists.

title: An Overview of Student Nutrition Program
summary: City Council has decided to advocate for more funding for student nutrition programs across Toronto. This includes urging the provincial government to access the new $1 billion federal National School Food Program, increase their financial support to match the City's contribution, and cover rising food costs. City staff will also develop plans to expand nutritious meal programs in schools and summer camps if additional funding becomes available.

title: Post-Transition of the Blue Box Program to Extended Producer Responsibility and Results of District 2 Service Delivery Options Review
Post-Transition of the Blue Box Program to Extended Producer Responsibility and Results of District 2 Service Delivery Options Review
summary: City Council has approved changes to how Toronto handles blue box recycling and general waste collection as the Blue Box program transitions to a new province-wide model. This means producers are now responsible for recycling their packaging, and the City will continue to manage blue box materials at its facilities while also seeking new companies for future waste and blue box pickup services and selling its blue bins.

title: 4888 Dundas Street West - Alteration of a Property Designated under Part IV of the Ontario Heritage Act and Authority to Enter into a Heritage Easement Agreement - Request for Directions
summary: Toronto City Council has approved changes to the heritage-designated property at 4888 Dundas Street West. This allows a new mixed-use building to be constructed on the site, provided that the owner agrees to protect the historical elements through a conservation plan and a special agreement with the city.

title: 4884-4896 Dundas Street West - Ontario Land Tribunal Hearing - Request for Directions
summary: Toronto City Council has agreed to a settlement regarding a development proposal at 4884-4896 Dundas Street West, which was under appeal at the Ontario Land Tribunal. This means the city will support a compromise, allowing the project to move forward once the developer meets specific conditions related to infrastructure, stormwater management, and traffic plans.

title: Call to Order
summary: City Council officially opened its meeting on April 17th. The session began with O Canada, a moment of silence to honour several individuals who passed away, and a land acknowledgement recognizing Indigenous Peoples and treaties.

title: Increase in Penalty Amounts for Parking Offences and Establishment of New Electric Vehicle Parking Offences for Off-Street Parking Facilities
summary: Toronto City Council has approved an increase to the fines for various parking offences across the city, with these changes taking effect on August 1, 2024. Additionally, new rules are now in place for off-street electric vehicle parking spaces, requiring vehicles to be actively charging to park there and preventing non-EVs from using these spots.

title: Increase in Penalty Amounts for Parking Offences and Establishment of New Electric Vehicle Parking Offences for Off-Street Parking Facilities
Appointment of a Public Member to the Toronto Transit Commission Board
summary: City Council has appointed Liane Kim as a public member to the Toronto Transit Commission (TTC) Board, serving until June 16, 2027. This appointment ensures ongoing oversight and decision-making for Toronto's public transit system.

title: 12 Hillside Road - Official Plan Amendment and Zoning By-law Amendment Application - Decision Report - Refusal
summary: City Council has rejected an application to amend planning rules for a new home at 12 Hillside Road. This decision protects a designated natural area, ensuring that development aligns with the city's environmental and planning guidelines. If the developer appeals, the city will defend its refusal at the Ontario Land Tribunal.

title: Short-Term Rental By-law Implementation Update
summary: To protect long-term housing, Toronto City Council has strengthened rules for short-term rentals like Airbnb. These changes require operators to submit extra documents to prove their rental is their principal residence, clarify that secondary suites on the same property cannot be rented out short-term, and allow the city to request in-person interviews or inspections for compliance.

title: Short-Term Rental By-law Implementation Update
Re-opening NY10.22 - 31 Norden Crescent - Application for Fence Exemption
summary: North York Community Council approved an exemption for the property owner at 31 Norden Crescent, allowing their existing pool fence and gate to remain as constructed. This means the fence can stay, despite not fully meeting current city safety rules regarding how close the gate is to climbable surfaces, but any future replacement must comply with those rules.

title: 1837-1845 Bayview Avenue - Residential Demolition Applications
1837-1845 Bayview Avenue - Residential Demolition Applications
summary: North York Community Council approved the demolition of five detached houses located at 1837-1845 Bayview Avenue. This decision clears the way for new development in the area, potentially changing the local streetscape, with the approval including conditions for site cleanup after demolition.

title: Broadlands Boulevard and Castlegrove Boulevard - All-Way Stop Control
Broadlands Boulevard and Castlegrove Boulevard - All-Way Stop Control
summary: North York Community Council decided not to install all-way stop signs at the intersection of Broadlands Boulevard and Castlegrove Boulevard. City staff reviewed the location and determined that it did not meet the necessary criteria for all-way stop control, meaning traffic flow will remain unchanged for residents in the area.

title: 173 Homewood Avenue - Application to Remove a Private Tree
summary: The North York Community Council has approved a homeowner's request to remove a private black walnut tree at 173 Homewood Avenue. This decision requires the homeowner to plant five new trees or pay money to the city instead, addressing safety concerns despite the tree being healthy.

title: 650 (650A and 652) and 664 Yonge Street - Alterations to Heritage Properties, Demolition of Attributes and Authority to Enter into a Heritage Easement Agreement
summary: The Toronto Preservation Board has approved changes to heritage properties at 650-664 Yonge Street, allowing for the construction of a 75-storey mixed-use tower. This decision permits some demolition of existing heritage elements while requiring the developer to protect others through a heritage agreement, balancing new development with historical preservation.

title: 4888 Dundas Street West - Alteration of a Property Designated under Part IV of the Ontario Heritage Act and Authority to Enter into a Heritage Easement Agreement - Request for Directions
summary: The City's Preservation Board has adopted confidential recommendations regarding a proposed 29-storey development at 4888 Dundas Street West, which involves a designated heritage building. This decision addresses a settlement offer from the developer and will determine how the historic property is integrated into the new construction, impacting the character of the Etobicoke – Lakeshore neighbourhood.

title: 294, 306 and 318 Lawrence Avenue East and 101 Mildenhall Road - Notice of Intention to Designate a Property under Part IV, Section 29 of the Ontario Heritage Act
summary: City Council intends to designate properties at 101 Mildenhall Road and 294, 306, 318 Lawrence Avenue East as heritage sites. This decision will protect buildings on the Toronto French School campus, including its original Moderne-style facility, preserving a piece of the city's architectural and cultural history.

title: 374, 376 and 378 Yonge Street - Approval of Application for Alterations to Designated Heritage Properties Under Part IV, Section 33 of the Ontario Heritage Act and Authority to Enter into a Heritage Easement Agreement
summary: City Council has approved plans for a new 85-storey tower at 374-378 Yonge Street, allowing changes to existing heritage buildings on the site. This decision means a major new mixed-use development will proceed in the downtown core, while also putting in place protections for the historic elements of these properties.

title: 171 Lowther Avenue - Alterations to a Heritage Property and Authority to Enter into a Heritage Easement Agreement
summary: Toronto's Preservation Board is recommending that City Council approve plans to alter the heritage property at 171 Lowther Avenue, allowing an 11-storey residential building to be constructed. This decision would permit significant new development while requiring a Heritage Easement Agreement to protect the site's historical elements.

title: 36-42 Maitland Street - Alterations to Heritage Properties, Demolition of Attributes and Authority to Enter into a Heritage Easement Agreement
summary: The city has approved plans to build a 56-storey tower at 36-42 Maitland Street, which involves making changes and removing parts of two existing heritage buildings. This decision allows for new development while requiring the developer to protect important historical features through a special heritage agreement.

title: 68 Wellesley Street East - Refusal of Application for Alterations to Designated Heritage Properties Under Part IV, Section 33 of the Ontario Heritage Act
summary: The Toronto Preservation Board has recommended refusing proposed alterations to the William McBean Terrace at 68 Wellesley Street East. This decision aims to protect the designated 19th-century heritage property from changes linked to a new 28-storey development. It ensures the preservation of important historical architecture in the city.

title: 64 Woodlawn Avenue West - Refusal of Repeal of Designating By-law under Section 32 of the Ontario Heritage Act and Proposed Amendment to Designating By-law under Part IV, Section 30.1 of the Ontario Heritage Act
summary: Toronto City Council has decided to maintain the heritage designation for the historic house at 64 Woodlawn Avenue West, built in 1906 by architect Eden Smith. This decision rejects the property owner's request to remove the designation and includes plans to amend the existing heritage protections, ensuring the continued preservation of this significant property.

title: 72 Church Avenue - Official Plan and Zoning By-law Amendment Application - Decision Report - Approval
summary: City Council approved changes to the Official Plan and Zoning By-laws for the property at 72 Church Avenue in Willowdale. This decision allows for a new development on the site, with a requirement to build a privacy fence for existing neighbours.

title: Long-Term Financial Plan Update: Leveraging City-Wide Real Estate Opportunities for Affordable Housing, Complete Communities and Financial Sustainability
Long-Term Financial Plan Update: Leveraging City-Wide Real Estate Opportunities for Affordable Housing, Complete Communities and Financial Sustainability
summary: City Council has approved a plan to review its properties, including parking lots and land around TTC stations, to identify opportunities for new affordable housing and community spaces. This initiative aims to make better use of public land, improve the city's financial health, and create more complete neighbourhoods across Toronto.

title: Night Economy Manifesto - by Councillor Paul Ainslie, seconded by Councillor Jennifer McKelvie
summary: City Council has sent a proposal called the "Night Economy Manifesto" to the Economic and Community Development Committee for further review. This means the committee will study ideas for improving Toronto's nightlife, cultural activities, and businesses that operate after hours, before any final decisions are made.

title: Re-Opening and Amending Item 2023.IE7.4 on Updates on Vision Zero Road Safety Initiatives to Allow Optional Polling for Traffic - by Councillor Stephen Holyday, seconded by Councillor Jennifer McKelvie
summary: A proposal to revisit City Council's Vision Zero road safety plans was not considered by Council. This means the idea of adding optional resident polling for traffic changes will not be debated or decided upon at this time.

title: Clarification on Municipal Licensing and Standards Poster Enforcement Policies and By-laws - Rules Governing Affixing and Removing of Posters - by Councillor James Pasternak, seconded by Councillor Brad Bradford
summary: City Council is exploring clearer rules for posters in public spaces, like those for events or political messages. Instead of making an immediate decision, a proposal to clarify poster enforcement policies and bylaws was sent to the Economic and Community Development Committee for further review.

title: Authority to Donate Surplus Vehicles - by Mayor Olivia Chow, seconded by Councillor Jennifer McKelvie
summary: Toronto City Council recently approved donating up to five surplus vehicles from its Fleet and Paramedic Services to the Ukrainian government for humanitarian purposes. The city also requested its agencies, like the TTC and Toronto Police, to consider donating their own end-of-life vehicles, and will provide three generators to Ukraine to support ongoing humanitarian efforts.

title: New Multi-Tenant Houses Renovation Program to Preserve and Improve Affordable Rental Homes
summary: Toronto City Council has approved a new program aimed at preserving and improving affordable rental housing by supporting renovations in multi-tenant homes, commonly known as rooming houses. The program will offer grant funding to operators for upgrades and may waive certain permit fees for up to 250 rooms, helping to ensure safer and better living conditions for tenants.

title: Response to EC6.9 - Incident and Operational Review of Serious Dog Attacks
summary: To enhance public safety and prevent serious dog attacks, City Council has approved creating a public, searchable online record of dogs that have received Dangerous Dog Orders. This new tool will inform residents about dangerous dog incidents in their area and will be supported by a proposed $500,000 public education campaign in the 2025 budget.

title: Supporting New Long-Term Care Beds in North York
summary: Toronto City Council has approved a request to the provincial government to issue a special zoning order for a new mixed-use development at 4000 Leslie Street in North York. This decision helps fast-track the creation of 528 new long-term care beds, along with medical offices and retail space, to serve the community. The Council also ensures city staff will work with the developer and the province on project agreements and oversight.

title: Eliminating Education Development Charges on Missing Middle Housing Forms
summary: Toronto City Council is asking the Toronto Catholic District School Board to review its fees for schools and consider exempting secondary units, like basement apartments, from these charges. This move aims to make it easier to create more diverse housing options across the city.

title: Authority to Accept an In-Kind Donation and Enter into an Agreement with Pride Toronto for Hanlan's Point Rainbow Road - by Deputy Mayor Ausma Malik, seconded by Councillor Chris Moise
summary: City Council has approved accepting an in-kind donation from Pride Toronto to upgrade Beach Road near Hanlan's Point Beach in Toronto Island Park. This decision allows the city to partner with Pride Toronto to refurbish the road, creating a "Rainbow Road" and improving a popular public space for residents and visitors.

title: Authorization to Release Section 42 Funds to Amend the 2024-2033 Capital Budget and Plan for Parks, Forestry and Recreation to Advance Hanlan's Beach Priority Initiatives in Ward 10 - by Deputy Mayor Ausma Malik, seconded by Councillor Chris Moise
summary: Toronto City Council approved funding for several improvements at Hanlan's Beach, including new bike parking, fencing to protect natural habitats, and better access trails. These projects, aimed at enhancing visitor experience and preserving the environment, will be fully paid for by developer contributions collected for parkland.

title: Re-opening and Amending Item MM14.16 - Summit to Solve the Epidemic of Criminal Break-and-Enters Against Small Business in the City of Toronto - by Councillor Mike Colle, seconded by Councillor Frances Nunziata

Re-opening and Amending Item MM14.16 - Summit to Solve the Epidemic of Criminal Break-and-Enters Against Small Business in the City of Toronto - by Councillor Mike Colle, seconded by Councillor Frances Nunziata
summary: City Council is taking steps to help Toronto's small businesses better protect themselves from break-ins. They have asked for a review of current city programs to see how they can better support businesses with security upgrades like cameras and improved locks, and will also ask the provincial and federal governments for funding for a new security grant program.

title: Urgent Action on the Auto Theft Crisis - by Councillor Mike Colle, seconded by Councillor Frances Nunziata
summary: Toronto City Council has decided to send an urgent motion about the auto theft crisis to its Economic and Community Development Committee for further review. This means the proposal, which calls for immediate action and stiffer penalties to address rising auto theft, will be discussed by the committee before potentially returning to Council for a decision.

title: Renaming of Dundas Toronto Transit Commission Stations
summary: City Council decided not to take any action on renaming the Dundas Toronto Transit Commission stations. This means the stations will keep their current names for the time being.

title: 1053 Don Mills Road, 2, 4, 4R, and 6 The Donway East Zoning By-law Amendment, Plan of Subdivision, and Site Plan Applications - Appeal Report
summary: Toronto City Council has decided to officially oppose a developer's proposed plans for a new project at 1053 Don Mills Road and The Donway East before the Ontario Land Tribunal. The City will also continue discussions with the developer to resolve outstanding concerns about the project's impact on local infrastructure and services, ensuring any future development is appropriate for the area.

title: 78, 80 and 86 Mimico Avenue - Notice of Intention to Designate a Property under Part IV, Section 29 of the Ontario Heritage Act
summary: Toronto City Council has decided to begin the process of officially recognizing the properties at 78, 80, and 86 Mimico Avenue as heritage sites. This designation will help protect their historical and architectural significance under the Ontario Heritage Act, affecting any future changes or development on these properties.

title: Update on Hosting FIFA World Cup 2026
Update on Hosting FIFA World Cup 2026
summary: Toronto City Council has approved further steps to prepare for hosting the FIFA World Cup 2026. This decision allows city staff to secure funding from federal and provincial governments and finalize agreements with airports, moving Toronto closer to being ready for the international event. Council also approved a budget for legal services related to the World Cup planning and operations.

title: Prioritization of Planned Higher-Order Transit Projects
Prioritization of Planned Higher-Order Transit Projects
summary: City Council has outlined a new approach for planning major transit projects, confirming that keeping the existing system in good repair is the most important investment priority. The City will review its official plans for future rapid transit corridors and road widths by late 2025, which will shape where new subways or LRTs could be built and how surface transit is prioritized. This also includes advocating for the Sheppard Subway extension and planning a Finch West LRT extension towards Pearson Airport.

title: Extending the Declaration Date for the Vacant Home Tax for the 2023 Taxation Year
summary: City Council has extended the deadline for homeowners to declare their property's occupancy status for the 2023 Vacant Home Tax. The new due date for submitting declarations is March 15, 2024, providing residents more time to comply and avoid potential penalties.

title: Technical Corrections to the Multi-tenant Housing Zoning By-law
summary: Toronto City Council approved minor technical corrections to the rules governing multi-tenant housing, also known as rooming houses. These updates clarify the city's zoning by-law for these types of homes, ensuring consistent regulations across the city and impacting housing options for residents.

title: Use of Nathan Phillips Square for Various Events - May 1, 2024 - August 31, 2024
summary: Toronto City Council has approved various organizations to host events at Nathan Phillips Square from May 1 to August 31, 2024. This means residents can look forward to a busy spring and summer at the square, with some events permitted to sell alcohol and others allowed to collect donations for their causes.

title: 6 and 8 Cawthra Square - Notice of Intention to Designate a Property under Part IV, Section 29 of the Ontario Heritage Act
6 and 8 Cawthra Square - Notice of Intention to Designate a Property under Part IV, Section 29 of the Ontario Heritage Act
summary: City Council intends to designate the historic homes at 6 and 8 Cawthra Square as heritage properties, protecting these 1892 Queen Anne Revival-style houses from significant alterations or demolition. This action helps preserve Toronto's architectural legacy and character within the Church-Wellesley village area.

title: 625 Yonge Street (Including 627, 629, 631, 633, 635 and 637 Yonge Street and 1, 3 and 5 Isabella Street) - Alterations to a Heritage Property and Authority to Enter into a Heritage Easement Agreement
summary: A major development project was approved for 625 Yonge Street, allowing alterations to several heritage properties in the block to make way for a new 70-storey tower. This decision permits significant changes to the historic streetscape while ensuring a heritage easement agreement is put in place.

title: 1779-1787 Bayview Avenue - Official Plan and Zoning By-law Amendment and Site Plan Control Applications - Heritage Matters
summary: Toronto's Preservation Board adopted confidential recommendations concerning a development proposal at 1779-1787 Bayview Avenue, which will now go to City Council for approval. These recommendations, prepared by the City Solicitor, relate to heritage concerns and ongoing appeals to the Ontario Land Tribunal, guiding the city's legal strategy against a developer's plans. This decision affects the future of a significant site that includes properties near the Leaside LRT station.

title: 49 and 51 Yonge Street - Alterations to Heritage Properties, Demolition of Heritage Attributes and Authority to Enter into a Heritage Easement Agreement
summary: The Toronto Preservation Board recently considered a plan to allow alterations and the demolition of interior features at 49 and 51 Yonge Street to make way for a 62-storey mixed-use building. The Board ultimately chose not to recommend approval of these changes to City Council. This means City Council will need to decide how to balance new high-rise development with the preservation of heritage properties at this downtown location.

title: Appointment of a Public Member to the PortsToronto Board of Directors
summary: City Council has appointed Robin Pilkey as a public member to the PortsToronto Board of Directors for a three-year term. This ensures a public voice on the board that oversees key city assets like the Port of Toronto and Billy Bishop Toronto City Airport.

title: Congestion Management Plan 2023-2026 - Update
summary: Toronto City Council has approved updates to its Congestion Management Plan, aiming to improve traffic flow and safety across the city. The plan includes developing strategies for better enforcement of blocked intersections and bike lanes, exploring new fees for road occupancy, and considering peak hour delivery restrictions. It also involves a pilot for accessible construction information and new transportation technology trials.

title: Kingsway Crescent between Bannon Avenue and Government Road - Speed Humps
summary: Residents on Kingsway Crescent can expect a new speed hump to be installed between Bannon Avenue and Government Road. The Etobicoke York Community Council approved this measure to help slow traffic and improve safety in the area.

title: 26, 28, 36 and 38 Hounslow Avenue - Residential Demolition Application
summary: North York Community Council has approved the demolition of four single-family homes at 26, 28, 36, and 38 Hounslow Avenue. The approval comes with conditions for the developers, including ensuring the site is maintained after demolition and hosting three community engagement events before new construction starts.

title: Front Yard Parking Appeal - 63 Hammersmith Avenue
committee: Toronto and East York Community Council
summary: Toronto's East York Community Council rejected a homeowner's appeal to create front yard parking at 63 Hammersmith Avenue. This decision means the homeowner cannot legally park in their front yard and must instead convert a paved area back into greenspace, upholding city rules that protect neighbourhood green spaces and manage parking.

title: 2157 to 2183 Lawrence Avenue East - Zoning Amendment Application - Decision Report - Approval
2157 to 2183 Lawrence Avenue East - Zoning Amendment Application - Decision Report - Approval
summary: City Council has approved a zoning change for the properties located at 2157 to 2183 Lawrence Avenue East. This decision paves the way for new development on these lands, which will change the type and scale of buildings allowed in this part of Scarborough.

title: Metrolinx Scarborough Subway Extension - Time Extension of Temporary Sidewalk Closure on McCowan Road
summary: City Council has approved extending the temporary closure of the east sidewalk on McCowan Road, between Sheppard Avenue East and Nugget Avenue, until December 31, 2024. This allows Metrolinx to continue tunnel construction for the Scarborough Subway Extension, with the City requiring the sidewalk to be fully restored to its original condition or better once the project is complete.

title: 4566 and 4568 Kingston Road - Zoning Amendment, Application - Decision Report - Approval
summary: Toronto City Council has approved changes to the local building rules for properties at 4566 and 4568 Kingston Road in Scarborough. This decision paves the way for a developer to construct a new 12-storey building that combines homes and businesses, shaping the neighbourhood's future development.

title: 2024 Operating and Capital Budgets
summary: Toronto City Council has approved its 2024 Operating and Capital Budgets, setting out how the city will collect and spend money for the year. This decision funds essential services, with specific amendments made to support areas like arts and culture, economic development, parks, and community safety.

title: Budget Implementation Including Property Tax Rates, User Fees and Related Matters
Budget Implementation Including Property Tax Rates, User Fees and Related Matters
Budget Implementation Including Property Tax Rates, User Fees and Related Matters
summary: City Council approved the implementation of the 2024 budget, establishing new property tax rates for the year. This decision means residential property owners will see an 8% increase, while an additional special levy will help fund priority transit and housing projects across the city.

title: Budget Implementation Including Property Tax Rates, User Fees and Related Matters
Administrative Penalty System for Red Light Camera and Automated Speed Enforcement Violations
summary: Toronto City Council has approved a new process for handling tickets from red light cameras and automated speed enforcement, effective November 1, 2024. Instead of going through the provincial court system, these violations will now be managed by the city's own administrative penalty system, including appeals to the Administrative Penalty Tribunal. This change aims to make the ticket resolution process more efficient.

title: Call to Order
