summary: This item formally opened a City Council meeting, beginning with a land acknowledgment and a moment of silence to honour community members who recently passed away. This is a standard procedural item that kicks off every Council meeting.

title: Homelessness Services Capital Infrastructure Strategy: Real Estate Strategy and Lease Extensions
Homelessness Services Capital Infrastructure Strategy: Real Estate Strategy and Lease Extensions
Homelessness Services Capital Infrastructure Strategy: Real Estate Strategy and Lease Extensions
summary: To ensure stable and adequate spaces for residents experiencing homelessness, the City has approved a real estate strategy for its shelter and support services. This allows staff to extend leases for current facilities and acquire new properties, ensuring critical infrastructure for these vital programs.

title: Ready, Set, Midtown: Zoning Review - City Initiated Zoning By-law Amendments for Select Lands Designated Apartment Neighbourhoods - Decision Report - Approval
summary: City Council has approved new zoning rules for specific apartment areas in Midtown, as part of the "Ready, Set, Midtown" review. These changes will guide how new apartment buildings can be developed in these neighbourhoods, affecting their future growth and housing options.

title: Tax Fairness for All:  An Equitable Approach to the Residential Property Tax Rate on Toronto Island - by Councillor Jon Burnside, seconded by Councillor James Pasternak
summary: City Council has referred a proposal to the Executive Committee that aims to establish a fairer way to calculate residential property tax rates for Toronto Island residents. This referral means the committee will now study the issue, which could lead to future changes in how these taxes are applied.

title: No More Subsidies for New Gas Pipelines that Drive Up Torontonians' Energy Costs - by Councillor Dianne Saxe, seconded by Councillor Alejandra Bravo
summary: Councillors proposed a motion to end city subsidies for new natural gas pipelines, aiming to prevent increases in residents' energy costs. Instead of making a decision, City Council referred this proposal to its Executive Committee for further consideration, meaning a decision on the subsidies is delayed.

title: Highway 401 Overpass Safety
summary: Toronto City Council has directed the City Manager to create new guidelines for managing and monitoring rallies and protests, specifically addressing safety concerns on highway overpasses. This policy will ensure respect for human rights while clarifying that police are responsible for enforcement and require adequate funding for this role.

title: Implementation Review of the Noise By-law
summary: City Council has updated several definitions within Toronto's Noise By-law. These changes clarify what counts as noise, how it's measured, and how certain exemptions are handled, which helps ensure more consistent enforcement and understanding for residents and businesses across the city.

title: Implementation Review of the Noise By-law
Bike Lanes that are Safe and Passable for Bikes
summary: Toronto's City Council has directed Transportation Services to ensure that designated bike lanes are safe and passable for cyclists during the winter season. This decision aims to improve winter cycling conditions, making it safer and more practical for residents to use bikes year-round.

title: Cycling Network Plan: 2024 Cycling Infrastructure Installation - First Quarter Update
Cycling Network Plan: 2024 Cycling Infrastructure Installation - First Quarter Update
summary: City Council has approved the installation of new bike lanes on several streets across Toronto as part of the 2024 Cycling Network Plan. This decision aims to expand the city's cycling infrastructure and improve safety for residents, with a priority on placing lanes off the main roadway where possible.

title: Delivering Improved Accessibility: Bike Share Toronto's Modernized Rate Structure Update
summary: To make transportation more accessible, City Council approved providing reduced-fare annual Bike Share Toronto memberships to eligible low-income residents. This decision helps people participating in the Fair Pass program access affordable bike rentals, expanding their transportation options.

title: 3585 and 3595 St. Clair Avenue East, 636 to 646 and 641 to 663 Danforth Road, and 411 and 415 Kennedy Road - Official Plan Amendment, Zoning Amendment, Draft Plan of Subdivision - Request for Direction

3585 and 3595 St. Clair Avenue East, 636 to 646 and 641 to 663 Danforth Road, and 411 and 415 Kennedy Road - Official Plan Amendment, Zoning Amendment, Draft Plan of Subdivision - Request for Direction
summary: Toronto City Council approved a settlement for a major development in Scarborough, affecting properties near St. Clair Avenue East, Danforth Road, and Kennedy Road. This decision means the developer will contribute significantly to the community, including up to $2 million for public art, a new recreation centre, and potential affordable housing, shaping the future of these neighbourhoods.

title: Going with the Snow - Returning Tobogganing to 45 Hills Across Toronto - by Councillor Brad Bradford, seconded by Mayor Olivia Chow
Going with the Snow - Returning Tobogganing to 45 Hills Across Toronto - by Councillor Brad Bradford, seconded by Mayor Olivia Chow
summary: Toronto City Council approved a plan to bring back tobogganing on 45 hills across the city, replacing "no tobogganing" signs with warnings and re-installing hay bales for safety. This decision means future bans will require approval from local councillors and the Community Council, making it harder to prohibit the activity, and the City is also asking the Province to review liability laws.

title: 3 Swift Drive - Zoning By-law Amendment and Rental Housing Demolition Applications - Decision Report - Approval
summary: Toronto City Council has approved plans for a new development at 3 Swift Drive, allowing the demolition of 61 existing rental units. This decision requires the developer to build 61 new rental homes on the site and dedicate over 500 square metres for a new public park, providing both housing and green space for the community in Don Valley East.

title: 4884-4896 Dundas Street West - Official Plan Amendment and Zoning By-law Amendment Application - Appeal Report
summary: City Council has decided to formally oppose a developer's request to change planning rules for a property at 4884-4896 Dundas Street West before the Ontario Land Tribunal. This means the city is fighting against the proposed development's current design and has set strict conditions for infrastructure, traffic, and stormwater management that must be met if the provincial tribunal ultimately approves it. This protects local services and ensures any future development fits community needs.

title: RapidTO: Surface Transit Network Plan
RapidTO: Surface Transit Network Plan
RapidTO: Surface Transit Network Plan
summary: Toronto City Council has adopted the RapidTO Surface Transit Network Plan, which aims to improve surface transit across the city. This decision endorses the plan in principle and directs city staff to start detailed studies and public consultations for potential rapid transit improvements on specific sections of Finch Avenue East, Dufferin Street, Lawrence Avenue East, and Steeles Avenue West. These efforts will help speed up bus and streetcar service, making daily commutes faster and more reliable for residents.

title: Reimbursement of Cities Forum 2023 Conference Expenses, Torino, Italy - by Councillor Nick Mantas, seconded by Councillor Jennifer McKelvie
Reimbursement of Cities Forum 2023 Conference Expenses, Torino, Italy - by Councillor Nick Mantas, seconded by Councillor Jennifer McKelvie
summary: City Council approved covering the travel costs for Councillor Nick Mantas and his Chief of Staff to attend a conference in Italy, using funds from the Councillor's existing office budget. This decision also directed the City Clerk to review current policies on travel expenses for Council members' staff, aiming for more clarity and accountability in future travel arrangements.

title: Balanced Approach to Distributing Legislative Meeting Materials - by Councillor Chris Moise, seconded by Councillor Amber Morley
Balanced Approach to Distributing Legislative Meeting Materials - by Councillor Chris Moise, seconded by Councillor Amber Morley
summary: City Council recently considered a proposal to explore or resume distributing paper copies of meeting materials to councillors. However, Council ultimately decided not to adopt this change. This means councillors will continue to primarily use digital formats for their meeting documents, avoiding potential new printing costs.

title: Municipal Non-Resident Speculation Tax on Foreign Buyers of Residential Property
summary: Toronto City Council has approved a new 10% Municipal Non-Resident Speculation Tax on residential properties bought by foreign buyers, effective January 1, 2025. This new tax aims to discourage foreign speculation in the housing market and could help make home ownership more accessible for Toronto residents.

title: Finch West Light Rail Transit: Train Operating and Services Term Sheet
summary: City Council has approved the final steps to set up the operating agreement for the new Finch West Light Rail Transit (LRT) line. This means the city will finalize an agreement with Metrolinx and the TTC to manage how the trains will run, ensuring the new transit service can begin once construction is complete.

title: 717 - 733 Mount Pleasant Road - Request for City Solicitor to Attend at the Ontario Land Tribunal - by Councillor Jaye Robinson, Seconded by Councillor Mike Colle
summary: Toronto City Council is sending the City's legal team to the Ontario Land Tribunal to represent its interests regarding a development application at 717-733 Mount Pleasant Road. The City wants to ensure that its planning recommendations are upheld if the project is approved, or to oppose the application if the plans change in a way that staff do not support. This decision helps ensure local development follows city guidelines and protects the neighbourhood.

title: Fraternities should not be exempt from the Multi-Tenant Housing Bylaw
summary: Toronto's Planning and Housing Committee rejected a proposal to make fraternities and sororities follow the new Multi-Tenant Housing By-law, which aims to ensure safe and accountable shared living conditions. This decision means these student residences will not be required to get a city licence or meet the same standards as other multi-tenant houses, potentially leaving students with fewer protections and neighbours without a clear way to address ongoing concerns.

title: 2031 Kennedy Road - Zoning Amendment Application - Decision Report - Approval
summary: Toronto City Council has approved changes to the zoning rules for the properties at 2031 and 2301 Kennedy Road in Scarborough. This decision allows for new types of development or altered uses for these lands, impacting the future shape of the Agincourt neighbourhood.

title: 54-62 Glen Everest Road - Rental Housing Demolition Application - Decision Report - Approval
summary: City Council has approved the demolition of 68 rental homes on Glen Everest Road in Scarborough. This approval comes with conditions requiring the developer to build 68 new rental units on the same site, with many designated as affordable and mid-range for at least 10 years, and to provide support for existing tenants to relocate.

title: Traffic Calming Measures on Kingsway Crescent between Thorndale Avenue and Queen Anne Road
summary: To make a residential street safer, the Etobicoke York Community Council has approved the installation of speed humps on Kingsway Crescent. This decision aims to reduce vehicle speeds and improve safety for pedestrians and residents on this local street, which has many driveways and limited sidewalks.

title: Call to Order
Reimbursement of Cities Forum 2023 Conference Expenses, Torino, Italy - by Councillor Nick Mantas, seconded by Councillor Jennifer McKelvie
Renaming the Stadium at Centennial Park - by Councillor Paul Ainslie, seconded by Councillor Shelley Carroll
Renaming the Stadium at Centennial Park - by Councillor Paul Ainslie, seconded by Councillor Shelley Carroll
Renaming the Stadium at Centennial Park - by Councillor Paul Ainslie, seconded by Councillor Shelley Carroll
summary: Toronto's City Council recently decided to rename the stadium at Centennial Park to the "Rob Ford Stadium." This decision bypasses the city's usual naming policy and will require new signage to be designed and installed at the public sports facility.

title: 55 Yeomans Road - Inclusion on the Heritage Register
Supporting the Growth of the Night Economy
Supporting the Growth of the Night Economy
summary: Toronto City Council recently adopted a plan to support and grow the city's night economy, which includes businesses like bars, restaurants, and entertainment venues operating after hours. The plan focuses on improving communication and ensuring diverse voices, including residents, gig workers, unions, and businesses from all areas of the city, are included in ongoing discussions. This aims to create a more inclusive, safe, and well-planned night-time environment for everyone.

title: Recommended Amendments to Zoning By-laws for Bars, Restaurants and Entertainment Venues as part of the Night Economy Review - Final Report
Recommended Amendments to Zoning By-laws for Bars, Restaurants and Entertainment Venues as part of the Night Economy Review - Final Report
summary: Toronto City Council has approved changes to city planning rules for bars, restaurants, and entertainment venues, effective January 1, 2025. This includes allowing nightclubs to operate in building basements and removing some area-specific restrictions on parts of Kingston Road and Queen Street East, potentially increasing nightlife options across the city.

title: Non-Competitive Contract with N. Barry Lyon Consultants for Multi-Tenant Housing Land Economics Study
summary: Toronto City Council has approved directly hiring N. Barry Lyon Consultants to conduct a study on the financial impacts of allowing multi-tenant housing, such as rooming houses, across the city. This research will help the city understand how these housing options affect property values and the broader economy, informing future housing policies for residents.

title: Display of 988 Crisis Line Information Poster - by Councillor Paul Ainslie, seconded by Councillor Jennifer McKelvie
summary: To support mental health and suicide prevention, City Council decided to display posters promoting the 988 national crisis line in all City-owned buildings. This initiative aims to make it easier for Toronto residents in crisis to access vital support, particularly during the holiday season when some may feel more alone.

title: Request for City Planning to Launch the Glencairn Subway Station Planning Study - by Councillor Michael Colle, seconded by Councillor Pasternak
summary: Toronto City Council has approved launching a planning study for the area surrounding Glencairn Subway Station. This study will identify needed community services and investigate traffic and transportation improvements in the Marlee and Lawrence Avenue West corridor, helping the neighbourhood manage future growth and reduce congestion.

title: Updates to the Constituency Services and Office Budget Policy
summary: Toronto City Council has approved a new policy that updates the rules for how city councillors manage their offices and budgets. This change provides clearer guidelines for how public funds are spent on constituent services and daily operations, affecting how councillors serve their communities.

title: Updates to the Constituency Services and Office Budget Policy
Amendments to Toronto Municipal Code Chapter 667: Rental Demolition and Conversion By-law
Amendments to Toronto Municipal Code Chapter 667: Rental Demolition and Conversion By-law
summary: Toronto City Council strengthened rules regarding the demolition and conversion of rental properties, aiming to better protect tenants. The City also requested the Province of Ontario grant it more authority to regulate the conversion of dwelling rooms and consult on new regulations. Additionally, a report will explore options for giving displaced tenants priority access to new affordable rental units.

title: 4174 Dundas Street West - Request for Directions
4174 Dundas Street West - Request for Directions
summary: Toronto City Council has settled a legal dispute regarding a new development at 4174 Dundas Street West, agreeing to support the proposal at a land use tribunal. This decision means future residents and visitors of this development will not be eligible for street parking permits, and the developer must ensure local infrastructure and school capacity can handle the project.

title: Eastbound F.G. Gardiner Expressway and Northbound Don Valley Parkway Closures - 2024 Toronto Triathlon Festival
summary: City Council has approved major road closures for the Toronto Triathlon Festival on Sunday, July 14, 2024. The eastbound Gardiner Expressway and northbound Don Valley Parkway will be closed from early morning until noon, which will significantly impact traffic flow. To help manage congestion, other road closures for events will be prohibited that day, and the triathlon organizer must cover any city costs for securing work zones.

title: Proposed Building Emissions Performance Reporting By-Law
summary: Toronto City Council has adopted a new by-law requiring buildings across the city to report their greenhouse gas emissions performance starting January 1, 2024. This move aims to help Toronto reduce its carbon footprint and address climate change, with collected data eventually becoming publicly available. The city may also introduce or increase fines for buildings that don't comply.

title: Housing Action Plan: Zoning By-law Simplification and Modernization for Low-rise Residential Zones - Phase 1 Final Report
summary: Toronto City Council has approved significant updates to the city's zoning rules for low-rise residential areas across the city. These changes, part of a housing action plan, simplify and modernize the by-laws to make it easier to build diverse housing types like multiplexes and small apartment buildings, aiming to increase housing options for residents. The city will continue consultations for a second phase of these reforms, with future proposals expected next year.

title: Crisis Looms as Aging Booms: Creating a Path Forward to Age in Place - by Councillor Josh Matlow, seconded by Councillor Vincent Crisanti
Crisis Looms as Aging Booms: Creating a Path Forward to Age in Place - by Councillor Josh Matlow, seconded by Councillor Vincent Crisanti
summary: Toronto City Council adopted a plan to better support seniors who wish to age in their current homes and communities. This includes advocating for federal and provincial funding, a national strategy, and streamlined home care services. City staff will also develop a local plan by late 2024 to enhance services and infrastructure, such as adding benches and exploring zoning changes to support amenities for older adults in their neighbourhoods.

title: Re-Opening and Amending Item MM11.34 - Opening City Hall - Regarding the Terms of Reference for the City-School Boards Advisory Committee - by Deputy Mayor Ausma Malik, seconded by Mayor Olivia Chow
Re-Opening and Amending Item MM11.34 - Opening City Hall - Regarding the Terms of Reference for the City-School Boards Advisory Committee - by Deputy Mayor Ausma Malik, seconded by Mayor Olivia Chow
summary: To ensure efficient and inclusive representation, City Council approved changes to the City-School Boards Advisory Committee. This committee, which facilitates cooperation between the city and school boards, will now have two fewer City Council members and will include trustees from the French-language school boards.

title: Update and Action Plan on the Single-Use and Takeaway Items Reduction Strategy, including the Circular Food Innovators Fund
Update and Action Plan on the Single-Use and Takeaway Items Reduction Strategy, including the Circular Food Innovators Fund
Update and Action Plan on the Single-Use and Takeaway Items Reduction Strategy, including the Circular Food Innovators Fund
summary: Toronto City Council has adopted new rules to reduce single-use and takeaway items like straws, lids, and cutlery, requiring businesses to offer them only upon request. Effective March 1, 2024, these changes also encourage businesses to accept reusable cups, with health guidelines to be provided. Importantly, the city decided not to require businesses to charge for reusable shopping bags at this time.

title: 250 Wincott Drive and 4620 Eglinton Avenue West - Authority to Amend Section 37 Agreement to Reflect the Minor Variance Decision of the Committee of Adjustment
250 Wincott Drive and 4620 Eglinton Avenue West - Authority to Amend Section 37 Agreement to Reflect the Minor Variance Decision of the Committee of Adjustment
summary: Toronto City Council has approved changes to a development project at 250 Wincott Drive and 4620 Eglinton Avenue West, adjusting the timeline for the design and construction of related community infrastructure. A key change is that future residents of these buildings will not be eligible for on-street parking permits, and developers must inform all prospective buyers and renters about this restriction and the available on-site parking.

title: Council Member Appointments to the City-School Boards Advisory Committee
summary: Toronto City Council has appointed Councillors Parthi Kandavel and Jon Burnside to the City-School Boards Advisory Committee for terms ending December 31, 2024. Councillor Kandavel will also serve as the Committee's Chair, helping to strengthen cooperation between the city and local school boards on important issues affecting students and communities.

title: Supporting Purpose Built Rental Homes in Etobicoke North
summary: City Council adopted a request to the province for a special zoning order at 65 Rexdale Boulevard to allow for new development. This order would pave the way for at least 220 new purpose-built rental homes, with 20-30% being affordable for 99 years, alongside retail, a place of worship, and a community centre in Etobicoke North. This decision aims to boost housing options, especially affordable rentals, for residents in the area.

title: 8-26 Jopling Avenue South - Request for City Solicitor to Attend at the Toronto Local Appeal Body - by Councillor Stephen Holyday, seconded by Councillor Vincent Crisanti
summary: City Council rejected a request to send the City Solicitor to the Toronto Local Appeal Body regarding the property at 8-26 Jopling Avenue South. This decision means the city will not formally participate in any appeal proceedings for this site, potentially affecting future development or planning decisions in that area.

title: Conveyance of Parkland - 2150 and 2194 Lake Shore Boulevard West and 23 Park Lawn Road - by Councillor Amber Morley, seconded by Councillor Gord Perks
summary: City Council has approved an agreement requiring a developer to dedicate two new public parks, totalling at least 12,500 square metres, along Lake Shore Boulevard West in Etobicoke-Lakeshore. This ensures the community gains valuable green space as new residential towers are built.

title: Introduction of General Bills and Confirming Bills
summary: Toronto City Council officially finalized and documented all decisions made during its December meeting by passing a series of confirmatory bills. This common procedure ensures that all actions, including new by-laws and planning changes, are legally valid and transparent.

title: Introduction of General Bills and Confirming Bills
Introduction of General Bills and Confirming Bills
685 Lake Shore Boulevard East - Applications for Zoning By-law Amendment and Site Plan Control - Appeal Report
summary: Toronto City Council has decided to oppose a proposed development at 685 Lake Shore Boulevard East, directing its legal team to fight the zoning and site plan applications at the Ontario Land Tribunal. This means the City wants to ensure any development at this waterfront site meets strict conditions related to infrastructure, flood protection, and community impacts before moving forward.

title: Advancing Eglinton East Light Rail Transit
summary: City Council has approved the route for the Eglinton East Light Rail Transit (LRT), moving forward with a crucial transit expansion for Scarborough. The decision includes allocating an additional $9.5 million to the project's capital budget to advance design, utility relocation, and ensure future connections with other transit lines. This ensures the project continues to progress and integrates effectively with Toronto's broader transit network.

title: 321 Davenport Road - Construction Staging Area
summary: Toronto City Council has approved a temporary closure of the south sidewalk, parking, bicycle, and eastbound vehicle lanes on Davenport Road near Bedford Road from December 2023 to July 2024. This change allows for construction staging, and residents can expect a safe pedestrian walkway and enhanced safety measures around the site.

title: Confronting the Legacy of the Transatlantic Slave Trade: Renaming Civic Assets Bearing the Henry Dundas Name - by Councillor Chris Moise, seconded by Mayor Olivia Chow
Confronting the Legacy of the Transatlantic Slave Trade: Renaming Civic Assets Bearing the Henry Dundas Name - by Councillor Chris Moise, seconded by Mayor Olivia Chow
Confronting the Legacy of the Transatlantic Slave Trade: Renaming Civic Assets Bearing the Henry Dundas Name - by Councillor Chris Moise, seconded by Mayor Olivia Chow
Confronting the Legacy of the Transatlantic Slave Trade: Renaming Civic Assets Bearing the Henry Dundas Name - by Councillor Chris Moise, seconded by Mayor Olivia Chow
summary: Toronto City Council has approved renaming prominent locations like Yonge-Dundas Square (to Sankofa Square), the Jane/Dundas Library, and two subway stations to remove references to Henry Dundas, acknowledging his role in delaying slavery abolition. This move aims to address historical legacies and promote a more inclusive city, supported by a public education campaign about the transatlantic slave trade.

title: Call to Order
summary: This item formally marked the beginning of the City Council meeting, where the Speaker called members to order. The meeting opened with O Canada, a moment of silence for several individuals, and an acknowledgement of Indigenous Peoples and traditional territories. Council members were also reminded about procedural rules for speaking during debates.

title: Call to Order
Federal Housing Accelerator Fund - Supporting Generational Transformation of Toronto's Housing System - by Mayor Olivia Chow, seconded by Councillor Jennifer McKelvie
Federal Housing Accelerator Fund - Supporting Generational Transformation of Toronto's Housing System - by Mayor Olivia Chow, seconded by Councillor Jennifer McKelvie
summary: Toronto City Council has approved accepting funding from the federal Housing Accelerator Fund. This money will help the city fast-track initiatives aimed at building more housing and transforming Toronto's housing system to address the housing crisis.

title: Federal Rapid Housing Initiative - Program Update and Phase Three Implementation
summary: Toronto City Council is moving forward with implementing the federal Rapid Housing Initiative, which aims to create more affordable housing. This includes preparing to lease 65 Dundas Street East to a non-profit organization for housing and authorizing data sharing with University Health Network to help connect homeless individuals with housing support.

title: Reimbursement of Cities Forum 2023 Conference Expenses, Torino, Italy - by Councillor Nick Mantas, seconded by Councillor Jennifer McKelvie
summary: City Council has postponed a decision about whether to reimburse councillors for expenses incurred during the Cities Forum 2023 conference in Torino, Italy. This item, which concerns the use of public funds for council travel, will now be discussed in February 2024.

title: Renaming the Stadium at Centennial Park - by Councillor Paul Ainslie, seconded by Councillor Shelley Carroll

Silent Nights and Peaceful Days - Providing relief to residents from Metrolinx's Whistle Sounding at Manse Road Rail Crossing on the Lakeshore East Corridor - by Councillor Jennifer McKelvie, seconded by Councillor Paula Fletcher
summary: Aiming to reduce noise for residents, City Council has asked the City Manager to request Metrolinx immediately stop train whistles at the Manse Road rail crossing. If a quick fix isn't possible, the city will ask Metrolinx to post a safety worker to prevent the whistles, providing peace to nearby communities.

title: Uber Litigation Regarding Limit on Number of Private Transportation Company Driver Licences
Uber Litigation Regarding Limit on Number of Private Transportation Company Driver Licences
summary: Toronto City Council has reversed its recent decision to limit the total number of ride-share driver licenses, like those for Uber. This means the city will resume issuing new licenses under the previous system while city staff prepare a report by March 2024 on new options for managing driver numbers, considering traffic congestion and environmental goals.

title: 55 Yeomans Road - Inclusion on the Heritage Register
summary: Toronto City Council has added the property at 55 Yeomans Road to the City's Heritage Register. This decision protects the historical character of the Beth David Synagogue building and will guide any future changes or development, with city staff working with the synagogue on its future use and potential preservation support.

title: 975 Danforth Avenue - Ontario Land Tribunal Hearing - Request for Directions
summary: Toronto City Council has agreed to a settlement regarding the proposed development at 975 Danforth Avenue. This means the City will support the development at the provincial planning tribunal, but only after the developer meets specific conditions for zoning, engineering, tree protection, and establishes a construction management plan that includes a committee for local residents and businesses.

title: 413 The Kingsway - Request for City Solicitor to Attend at the Toronto Local Appeal Body - by Councillor Stephen Holyday, seconded by Councillor Vincent Crisanti
summary: Toronto's City Council is directing its legal team to challenge an appeal concerning a development at 413 The Kingsway before a local appeals tribunal. This action aims to defend the city's planning decisions for the property and could influence what gets built in this neighbourhood.

title: Toronto Fire Services 2022 Annual Report
summary: Drawing from the 2022 Toronto Fire Services Annual Report, City Council approved several initiatives to enhance public safety and equity. They requested Toronto Fire Services to address 2SLGBTQ+ recruitment and report on fire safety for people living outdoors, while also exploring firefighting career paths for Toronto Community Housing tenants. This aims to foster a more inclusive fire service and improve safety for vulnerable residents.

title: Advancing Generational Transformation of Toronto's Housing System - Aligning Housing Mandates and Strategic Efforts
summary: Toronto City Council has updated the mandates for its housing agencies, Toronto Community Housing Corporation (TCHC) and CreateTO, to boost the supply of new affordable and market-rent housing across the city. The agencies are now directed to build more diverse homes on city-owned properties and ensure these lands stay publicly owned. This aims to create more complete, mixed-income communities and accelerate housing development to meet Toronto's housing goals.

title: Operating Variance Report for the Nine Months Ended Sept 30, 2023
summary: Toronto City Council has approved internal adjustments to the 2023 operating budget, reallocating funds based on actual spending over the first nine months of the year. These changes help manage the city's finances by aligning departmental budgets with their spending, without altering the overall net budget for the City.

title: Cummer Station - Response to Council Motions
summary: Toronto City Council has urged the Province of Ontario to fund and include Cummer Station as part of the Yonge North Subway Extension project. This decision aims to secure a new subway stop for residents, improving transit access for thousands living and working in the area.

title: Review of the Order Paper
Review of the Order Paper
Renaming Dundas Street
summary: City Council recently reviewed an inquiry about renaming Dundas Street and the City Manager's reply. They decided to simply acknowledge this information without taking any further steps, effectively halting any immediate action on the street's renaming from this specific item.

title: Birchmount Road and Chaldean Street/Glendower Circuit - Pedestrian Crossing Protection
summary: Toronto City Council has approved installing new traffic lights at Birchmount Road and Chaldean Street/Glendower Circuit. This decision aims to improve pedestrian safety for residents in the area, even though city staff had initially suggested it wasn't strictly necessary based on technical guidelines.

title: Balanced Approach to Distributing Legislative Meeting Materials - by Councillor Chris Moise, seconded by Councillor Amber Morley
summary: City Council has postponed its discussion and decision on how legislative meeting materials are distributed. Residents interested in changes to how city documents are shared will need to wait until the February 2024 Council meeting for this topic to be addressed.

title: Ontario-Toronto New Deal Agreement
summary: Toronto City Council has endorsed a "New Deal" agreement with the Province of Ontario, designed to strengthen the city's finances and improve services. This partnership outlines significant provincial funding for shelters and new Line 2 subway trains, contingent on the federal government providing matching funds. It matters to residents as it could bring substantial investments in critical city services and transit.

title: Wilson Avenue and Maniza Road - Traffic Control Signals
summary: City Council is exploring options to make pedestrian crossings safer at the intersection of Wilson Avenue and Maniza Road. City staff will investigate various improvements, including traffic control signals, and report back on their findings in February 2024.

title: Review of the Order Paper
summary: City Council finalized its agenda, known as the Order Paper, for its meetings on December 13, 14, and 15, 2023. This action formally approved the list of topics and proposals that councillors would discuss and vote on during those sessions.

title: Revisions to the Fair Wage Policy and Updating the Fair Wage Schedule to Include 2022-2024 Wage Rates
summary: City officials are reviewing Toronto's fair wage policy, which sets pay standards for workers on city contracts. A city committee sent a proposal back to staff, asking for more detailed financial analysis and options to ensure small businesses can continue to compete for city projects. This review affects how workers are paid on city-funded jobs and could influence which companies win these contracts.

title: Etobicoke Coat of Arms Displayed in the Etobicoke Civic Centre
summary: Following complaints that the Etobicoke Coat of Arms displayed in the Civic Centre is offensive or racist, a city committee reviewed information on the matter, including a report suggesting its removal aligns with the city's reconciliation plan. However, the committee simply received the item for information, deciding to take no further action on the complaints or the potential removal of the Coat of Arms. This means the display will remain in place for now.

title: Direct Investment Request 2023 E
Direct Investment Request 2023 E
Direct Investment Request 2023 E
summary: The Toronto Atmospheric Fund has approved an investment of up to $3.5 million to help drivers of vehicles for hire, such as taxis, purchase electric vehicles. This initiative aims to accelerate the adoption of electric vehicles in Toronto, supporting the city's climate goals and making it easier for these drivers to transition to greener transportation.

title: 822-838 Richmond Street West Official Plan and Zoning By-law Amendment Applications - Decision Report - Approval
committee: Toronto and East York Community Council
summary: City Council has approved changes to the Official Plan and Zoning By-law for properties located at 822-838 Richmond Street West. This decision paves the way for a new development project at this site, altering what can be built and how the land can be used in this part of the city.

title: North York "Better Than All the Rest"
summary: North York Community Council has asked for a report on creating a branding strategy for the North York area. This initiative aims to highlight the neighbourhood's diverse culture, restaurants, and local businesses, encouraging more Toronto residents to explore the area and fostering community spirit, with plans to survey residents for a new slogan.

title: Re-opening NY6.20: 23 Laurentia Crescent - Request for a Fence Exemption
summary: North York Community Council approved a request from the owner of 23 Laurentia Crescent, allowing them to keep an existing fence that doesn't meet city rules. This means the fence can stay as it is for now, but any future replacement must follow current by-laws.

title: 7 Marwill Street - Application to Remove a Private Tree
summary: Toronto's North York Community Council has denied a request to remove a healthy Norway spruce tree from a private property at 7 Marwill Street. This decision upholds the city's tree protection by-laws, indicating a commitment to preserving the urban canopy even on private land.

title: 2023 Update on the Toronto Community Crisis Service and Proposed Expansion Plan
2023 Update on the Toronto Community Crisis Service and Proposed Expansion Plan
summary: Toronto City Council has endorsed the city-wide expansion of the Toronto Community Crisis Service, recognizing it as the city's fourth emergency service. This means residents across Toronto will soon have access to non-police, community-led mental health and crisis support, improving how the city responds to emergency calls. The city will now proceed with negotiating funding agreements to implement this critical service expansion.

title: Area-Specific Amendment to the Sign By-law: 195 Galaxy Boulevard
summary: City Council has approved a change to the sign by-law for the property at 195 Galaxy Boulevard, allowing for a new large electronic sign at this specific location. This decision also reclassifies a portion of the property's sign district from Open Space to Employment, which permits different types of signs.

title: Advancing Waterfront East Light Rail Transit
summary: Toronto City Council has approved the detailed design and planning for the Waterfront East Light Rail Transit (LRT) project, including its full route from Queens Quay to Villiers Loop. This decision moves the project closer to construction by securing environmental approvals and coordinating with other major waterfront developments, with an increased capital budget allocated for this design work.

title: Etobicoke Coat of Arms Displayed in the Etobicoke Civic Centre - by Councillor Stephen Holyday, seconded by Councillor Vincent Crisanti
summary: A proposal to display the Etobicoke Coat of Arms in the new Etobicoke Civic Centre was sent to the Executive Committee for further review. This means the decision on whether to showcase this symbol of Etobicoke's heritage in a prominent civic building has been postponed for now.

title: Endorsing Toronto as a Signatory of the Strong Cities Network Fourth Global Summit  New York City Mayoral Declaration in Fighting Hate, Extremism and Polarisation - by Councillor James Pasternak, seconded by Mayor Olivia Chow
summary: Toronto has officially joined a global effort to combat hate, extremism, and polarization by endorsing the New York City Mayoral Declaration. This commitment means the city will work to build stronger, more inclusive communities, address misinformation, and collaborate with other cities worldwide to foster safety and belonging for all residents.

title: Leveraging the Power of Interfaith Harmony Week in Toronto February 1 to 7, 2024 - by Councillor James Pasternak, seconded by Councillor Mike Colle
summary: To promote community understanding and celebrate diverse faiths, Toronto City Council has decided to support Interfaith Harmony Week, running from February 1 to 7, 2024. The City will develop a guide for councillors, helping them organize events and engage residents across the city during this important week.

title: 169 Parkmount Road - Request for City Solicitor to Attend at the Toronto Local Appeal Body - by Councillor Paula Fletcher, seconded by Councillor Alejandra Bravo
summary: City Council decided to send its legal team to an independent appeals body to defend the city's original decision to reject a property change application at 169 Parkmount Road. This action helps ensure that local planning rules are upheld and allows the legal team to negotiate a resolution on the city's behalf.

title: Updates on Vision Zero Road Safety Initiatives - New Traffic Calming Policy, Community Safety Zone Criteria, Zebra Marking Policy, Approach to Area-Based Speed Limit Reductions and Related Council Requests

Updates on Vision Zero Road Safety Initiatives - New Traffic Calming Policy, Community Safety Zone Criteria, Zebra Marking Policy, Approach to Area-Based Speed Limit Reductions and Related Council Requests

Updates on Vision Zero Road Safety Initiatives - New Traffic Calming Policy, Community Safety Zone Criteria, Zebra Marking Policy, Approach to Area-Based Speed Limit Reductions and Related Council Requests
summary: To make Toronto streets safer, the city has approved updates to its Vision Zero road safety initiatives. This includes a new policy for how traffic calming measures like speed cushions are put in place and an expanded framework for creating Community Safety Zones, especially around schools, where traffic fines are higher. The city also renewed its call for provincial action on motorcycle blood alcohol limits and area-based speed limits.

title: Response to MM11.39 - Protecting the Process for Tree Removal at Ontario Place
summary: Toronto City Council is taking steps to protect the city's interests regarding tree removal at Ontario Place. Council has directed the City Solicitor to explore intervening in the Province's federal environmental assessment process and to prepare for potential legal action related to the site.

title: Generational Transformation of Toronto's Housing System to Urgently Build More Affordable Homes
Generational Transformation of Toronto's Housing System to Urgently Build More Affordable Homes
summary: City Council has approved a comprehensive plan to transform Toronto's housing system, aiming to urgently build more affordable homes and protect existing non-profit and co-operative housing. This initiative involves reviewing current housing programs, developing new strategies for community housing, and engaging with various partners to identify lands for new home creation.

title: Need for Long-Term Investments to Address Refugee and Humanitarian Crisis
summary: Toronto City Council has formally asked the Federal government for urgent financial and operational support to address the ongoing refugee and humanitarian crisis. The city is specifically seeking federal funding for emergency housing and reception centres, full reimbursement for costs incurred supporting refugees, and an increase in housing benefits, including $6.67 million for 2023.

title: 2024 Shelter Infrastructure Plan and the Homelessness Services Capital Infrastructure Strategy (HSCIS)
summary: City Council has approved a new long-term strategy and a specific 2024 plan to address homelessness and expand shelter services. This initiative aims to improve and develop new facilities, secure existing temporary sites, and create a comprehensive infrastructure for supporting people experiencing homelessness across Toronto.

title: Shelter System Pressures and Responses, including Planning for Winter 2023/2024
summary: Toronto City Council is urging the federal government to provide immediate funding and open emergency shelter sites on federal lands for people seeking asylum, and to create a new reception centre near Pearson Airport. The city is also asking both federal and provincial governments for financial support to expand Toronto's winter shelter services, including warming centres and extra spaces, to help address the city's overwhelmed shelter system.

title: 25 Old York Mills Road - Official Plan and Zoning By-law Amendment Application - Ontario Land Tribunal Hearing - Request for Directions
summary: City Council has decided to settle a dispute regarding a development application for the property at 25 Old York Mills Road. This means the city will support a new agreement for the project at the Ontario Land Tribunal, rather than continue to oppose it. This decision helps resolve a contested building proposal, which impacts future development in the area.

title: Free-Floating Car-Share Program - Increase Access to the Number of Permits
summary: Toronto City Council has doubled the maximum number of permits for free-floating car-share companies, raising it from 500 to 1000 per company. This decision aims to expand access to car-sharing services for residents across the city, with a focus on encouraging more electric and non-gas powered vehicles.

title: 48 Grenoble Drive - Zoning By-law Amendment and Rental Housing Demolition Applications - Decision Report - Approval
summary: City Council has approved zoning changes for a new development at 48 Grenoble Drive, allowing for the demolition of 109 existing rental units. This decision requires the developer to build at least 110 replacement rental units on the site for 20 years and create a new publicly accessible outdoor space for the community.

title: Support for a Canada-Wide Universal Student Nutrition Program
summary: Toronto City Council is advocating for a Canada-wide student nutrition program. They are urging the federal government to allocate funds in the 2024 budget to establish a national program that would help more children in Toronto and across Canada receive healthy food at school.

title: Transition of Toronto's Blue Bin Recycling Program to Extended Producer Responsibility
summary: Toronto City Council has decided that the city will stop collecting blue bin recycling materials from homes and facilities starting January 1, 2026, as the province takes over this service through a new program. The City plans to sell its existing recycling carts, and the funds will help keep solid waste utility rates stable for residents.

title: Transition of Toronto's Blue Bin Recycling Program to Extended Producer Responsibility
Extension of the Alcohol in Parks Pilot - by Councillor Paula Fletcher, seconded by Councillor Paul Ainslie
summary: Toronto City Council has decided to extend the Alcohol in Parks pilot program, allowing residents to continue legally drinking in designated parks until at least March 31, 2024. This extension gives staff more time to evaluate the program and potentially adjust the list of participating parks or add new safety rules for the winter season.

title: Mechanical Vacuum Leaf Collection Program - by Councillor Stephen Holyday, seconded by Councillor Brad Bradford
summary: A proposal to develop a mechanical vacuum leaf collection program for 2024 was not approved by Toronto City Council. This means the city will not be moving forward with plans to use specialized equipment for leaf pickup, impacting how leaves are managed in neighborhoods next year.

title: Protecting the Process for Tree Removal at Ontario Place - by Deputy Mayor Ausma Malik, seconded by Councillor Dianne Saxe
summary: Toronto City Council has decided to ensure that proper procedures are followed before any trees are removed on City-owned land at Ontario Place. Council directed staff to report on all requirements for tree removal, including heritage and environmental assessments, and authorized the City Solicitor to take legal steps to protect the City's interests if trees are removed without permits. This aims to safeguard the area's natural heritage amidst planned development.

title: Keeping Toronto Safe from Hate - by Mayor Olivia Chow, seconded by Councillor Frances Nunziata
summary: To combat rising hate, City Council has adopted a plan to ensure Toronto is a safe and welcoming city for everyone. This includes directing staff to create an online 'anti-hate package' with information on reporting hate crimes and available support resources. Council also asked the Toronto Police to establish community safety zones around vulnerable locations like places of worship and provide public updates on their hate crime efforts.

title: Our Plan Toronto: Recommendations on Five Deferred Employment Area Conversion Requests - Final Report
summary: Toronto City Council has largely completed its review of city land use, confirming the zoning for many employment areas. However, decisions on converting several key employment lands, including sites in East Harbour and along Finch Avenue West, have been put on hold and will require further consideration before their future use (for jobs or housing) is decided.

title: Our Plan Toronto: Recommendations on Five Deferred Employment Area Conversion Requests - Final Report
Bill 109 Implementation, Phase 3 - Recommended Official Plan and Municipal Code Amendments respecting Site Plan Control
summary: Toronto City Council has updated its planning rules and processes to comply with the provincial Bill 109, which aims to accelerate housing development approvals. These changes affect how the city reviews new building projects and conducts early consultations with developers, and the city will now track how quickly these applications are processed. This shift could impact the pace of new housing construction across the city.

title: Official Plan Amendment 309 - 18 Champlain Boulevard - Tippett Road Regeneration Area - Ontario Land Tribunal Hearing - Request for Directions
summary: The City has accepted a settlement offer regarding proposed changes to the official plan for a property at 18 Champlain Boulevard, part of the Tippett Road Regeneration Area. This agreement will be presented to the provincial land planning body, avoiding a potentially lengthy dispute and providing clear direction for development in that neighborhood.

title: Increase in Parking Violation Notice Penalty Amount on Municipal / Private Property
summary: Toronto City Council has increased the penalty for parking without consent on municipal or private property. Starting December 1, 2023, the fine for these violations will rise from $30 to $75, aiming to deter unauthorized parking across the city.

title: On-Street Paid Parking Pilot - Mobile Only Zones
summary: City Council has approved a one-year pilot project to create "Mobile Only Zones" at some on-street paid parking spots across the city. In these specific locations, drivers will only be able to pay for parking using the Green P App, text-to-pay, or by scanning a QR code, moving away from traditional parking meters. New parking offenses related to these zones will be introduced, carrying a fine to help ensure compliance.

title: Designation of a Portion of the Property at 15 San Romanoway used by Doug Philpott Inner City Children's Tennis as a Municipal Capital Facility - by Councillor Anthony Perruzza, seconded by Mayor Olivia Chow
summary: Toronto City Council approved a plan to help improve the tennis courts at 15 San Romanoway, used by Doug Philpott Inner City Children's Tennis. This means a bubble can be built over the courts, allowing more free or low-cost tennis programs for local youth and public use. The City is supporting this by exempting the project from certain fees.

title: ConnectTO Program Update: City Assets for Efficiency and Connectivity
ConnectTO Program Update: City Assets for Efficiency and Connectivity
ConnectTO Program Update: City Assets for Efficiency and Connectivity
summary: Toronto City Council has adopted an update to the ConnectTO program, which aims to expand public Wi-Fi access across the city. This initiative focuses on closing the digital divide for residents in low-income housing and other underserved communities by leveraging existing city network infrastructure. The plan directs city staff to collaborate with community groups and other governments to improve affordable internet access and promote economic development.

title: Last Chance to Urge Province to Build Cummer Station for the Future of North York - by Councillor Lily Cheng, seconded by Councillor Mike Colle
summary: Toronto City Council has officially asked the Province of Ontario to fully fund and construct the proposed Cummer Station for the Yonge North Subway Extension. This decision aims to secure a crucial transit stop for 15,000 current and future North York residents, with the city also preparing a business case to strengthen its appeal.

title: Introduction of General Bills and Confirming Bills
summary: City Council formally enacted a series of by-laws, including those that confirm past meeting decisions and others that authorize special charges on specific properties. These special charges are typically levied to recover costs for local improvements that directly benefit those properties.

title: 5, 6 and 9 Jopling Avenue South - Zoning By-law Amendment - Decision Report - Approval
summary: City Council has approved a zoning change for the properties at 5, 7, and 9 Jopling Avenue South, paving the way for new development in this area. This approval comes with conditions, including a requirement for the developer to provide a relocation and financial assistance plan for the tenant of the existing rental unit slated for demolition, and to ensure necessary upgrades to local storm and sewer infrastructure.

title: Longbourne Drive between Willowridge Road and Redgrave Drive - Implementation of Permit Parking
summary: To help residents dealing with limited parking, City Council approved a step towards implementing permit parking on Longbourne Drive. This decision temporarily exempts the area from certain rules, allowing the local community council to consider formal requests for permit parking there.

title: Vacant Home Tax: Status Update
Vacant Home Tax: Status Update
summary: Toronto City Council updated its Vacant Home Tax, clarifying rules and making it more expensive to keep homes empty. This aims to encourage owners to rent or sell their properties, rather than leave them vacant, while also adjusting some exemptions for new builds and inherited properties.

title: Transitioning the Vehicle-for-Hire Industry to Net Zero Emissions by 2030
Transitioning the Vehicle-for-Hire Industry to Net Zero Emissions by 2030
Transitioning the Vehicle-for-Hire Industry to Net Zero Emissions by 2030
Transitioning the Vehicle-for-Hire Industry to Net Zero Emissions by 2030
summary: Toronto City Council has approved new rules requiring all taxis, limousines, and ride-sharing vehicles to be zero-emission by 2031. This initiative aims to reduce air pollution in the city, and a $10 million grant program will be established to help vehicle operators transition their fleets.

title: 220 Cowan Avenue and 1313 Queen Street West - Alterations to Designated Heritage Properties
summary: Toronto City Council has approved changes to two designated heritage properties at 220 Cowan Avenue and 1313 Queen Street West. This decision paves the way for the new Parkdale Hub, ensuring that the historical character of these buildings is preserved through specific conservation plans.

title: 104 Scarboro Beach Boulevard - Application to Injure a City Tree
committee: Toronto and East York Community Council
summary: Toronto's East York Community Council has denied a resident's request to move a healthy city-owned tree at 104 Scarboro Beach Boulevard. The resident sought to injure the red maple to build a new parking pad, but city bylaws protect healthy, maintainable trees, reinforcing efforts to preserve Toronto's urban forest.

title: 104 Scarboro Beach Boulevard - Application to Injure a City Tree
committee: Toronto and East York Community Council

title: Government Road - Traffic Calming Poll Results
summary: Etobicoke York Community Council has approved the installation of nine speed humps on Government Road, between Thorndale Crescent/Mill Cove and The Kingsway. This decision to slow traffic and improve safety for residents was made despite city staff advising against it because the street did not fully meet the City's technical criteria for traffic calming.

title: Petitions
summary: Toronto City Council recently acknowledged a petition submitted by residents concerned with saving the Marian Shrine of Gratitude. The Council received the petition for information, meaning they formally noted residents' concerns but did not take further action on it through this specific agenda item.

title: Request to Consider Heritage Designation at 3100 Weston Road - by Councillor Anthony Perruzza, seconded by Councillor James Pasternak
summary: Toronto's City Council has asked its planning department to evaluate the property at 3100 Weston Road for official heritage designation. This step could lead to the building being legally protected, helping to preserve its historical character and importance for the neighbourhood.

title: Immediate Federal Government Support Required for Refugee Claimants in Toronto
Immediate Federal Government Support Required for Refugee Claimants in Toronto
summary: City Council has approved an urgent request to the Federal Government for significant financial and logistical support for refugee claimants in Toronto. The city is asking for full reimbursement of an estimated $200 million spent in 2023 and ongoing funding for projected annual costs of $250 million in 2024 and beyond, to alleviate the strain on Toronto's shelter system.

title: Urgently Building More Affordable Homes
Urgently Building More Affordable Homes
summary: Toronto City Council has adopted an ambitious plan to address the city's housing crisis by committing to build 25,000 new rent-controlled homes. This target includes increasing the supply of affordable housing by 7,500 units, with 2,500 designated as rent-geared-to-income. City staff will now develop a detailed strategy, timeline, and cost analysis, working with various partners to make more housing options available for residents.

title: 20 Broadoaks Drive and 11 Catford Road - Ontario Land Tribunal Hearing - Request for Directions
summary: Toronto City Council has approved a settlement offer for a development proposal at 20 Broadoaks Drive and 11 Catford Road. This agreement resolves a dispute at the provincial land planning tribunal, allowing the project to proceed once specific planning and technical requirements are met.

title: Updated Long-Term Financial Plan
Updated Long-Term Financial Plan
Updated Long-Term Financial Plan
Updated Long-Term Financial Plan
summary: Toronto City Council has approved an updated long-term financial plan to address a substantial $46.5 billion financial challenge facing the city. Beginning January 1, 2024, higher Municipal Land Transfer Tax rates will apply to residential properties sold for over $3 million, with the highest rate reaching 7.5% for properties over $20 million. These new graduated taxes aim to generate more revenue and primarily affect buyers and sellers of high-value homes.

title: Introduction of General Bills and Confirming Bills
summary: At its September 6th meeting, City Council formally passed a series of new by-laws, making previous decisions official. These by-laws cover a range of topics, including designating several properties as culturally significant, amending city financial reserves, and adjusting land use rules for specific areas.

title: Declaring Gender-Based Violence and Intimate Partner Violence an Epidemic in the City of Toronto
summary: Toronto City Council has formally declared gender-based violence and intimate partner violence an epidemic within the city. This decision aims to draw attention to the crisis, urging provincial and federal governments to also declare an epidemic and implement key recommendations to prevent violence and femicide. The City will also explore ways to make meaningful investments in programs and services that support victims and prevent future violence.

title: Traffic Control Signals - Carlton Street and Homewood Avenue
summary: City Council has approved the installation of new traffic lights at the intersection of Carlton Street and Homewood Avenue. This decision aims to improve pedestrian safety and better connect the Garden District, creating a continuous and safer path for residents.

title: Traffic Control Signals - Yonge Street and Grenville Street/Wood Street
summary: City Council has approved new traffic lights for the intersection of Yonge Street and Grenville Street/Wood Street. This decision came after a community review revealed a strong resident desire for better traffic control, aiming to improve safety and traffic flow in the area.

title: Traffic Control Signals - Yonge Street and Maitland Street/Breadalbane Street
summary: City Council has approved the installation of new traffic lights at the intersection of Yonge Street and Maitland Street/Breadalbane Street. This decision responds to community input, aiming to improve safety and traffic flow for pedestrians, cyclists, and drivers in this busy downtown area.

title: 325 Gerrard Street East (Regent Park Phases 4 and 5) - Zoning By-law Amendment and Rental Housing Demolition Applications - Decision Report - Approval
summary: City Council has approved updated zoning rules for the next phases of redevelopment at 325 Gerrard Street East, part of Regent Park. This decision allows for new buildings and requires the developer to create over 3,700 square metres of community space for local residents, ensuring public benefits are included in the project.

title: Protecting the Science Centre to Support Thorncliffe and Flemingdon Park - by Councillor Josh Matlow, seconded by Councillor Jon Burnside
Protecting the Science Centre to Support Thorncliffe and Flemingdon Park - by Councillor Josh Matlow, seconded by Councillor Jon Burnside
summary: Toronto City Council has requested a detailed report on the future of the Ontario Science Centre. This report will explore the provincial lease terms for the current site and investigate if the City could operate the Science Centre at its Don Mills location, including potential revenue from events, which is important for the surrounding communities of Thorncliffe and Flemingdon Park.

title: 64 Mildenhall Road - Request for City Solicitor to Attend at the Toronto Local Appeal Body - by Councillor Jaye Robinson, seconded by Councillor Mike Colle
summary: City Council has instructed its legal team to defend a city planning committee's decision to reject a proposed development at 64 Mildenhall Road. This means the City will argue against the project at an independent appeal body, and can hire outside experts to support its case.

title: 2801 Jane Street - Zoning By-law Amendment Application - Decision Report - Approval
summary: City Council has approved a zoning change for 2801 Jane Street to allow for a new development, with specific conditions to protect current residents. The developer must maintain the existing 164 rental units for at least 20 years, provide tenants with access to new amenities, and fund repairs to the current building's parking and concrete at no cost to them.

title: City of Toronto Recommendations for Metrolinx's Ontario Line Construction within the Toronto and East York District
summary: City Council adopted recommendations for Metrolinx's Ontario Line construction in Toronto. These recommendations aim to ensure local residents, particularly Indigenous, Black, and equity-deserving groups, benefit from job opportunities and local procurement during the project. The recommendations also seek to support businesses impacted by construction through better communication, signage, and traffic management.

title: Safety Improvements to Complete Streets, Ward 3 and Ward 4 - by Councillor Gord Perks, seconded by Councillor Amber Morley
summary: Toronto City Council has approved changes to traffic and parking rules in Wards 3 and 4 to implement safety improvements on local streets. These updates will help create "Complete Streets" designed for safer use by drivers, cyclists, and pedestrians, with installation work scheduled to begin in August.

title: Deadly Tanker Truck Crashes on Highway 401 - by Councillor Mike Colle, seconded by Councillor James Pasternak
Deadly Tanker Truck Crashes on Highway 401 - by Councillor Mike Colle, seconded by Councillor James Pasternak
summary: Toronto City Council is looking into the risks of deadly tanker truck crashes on Highway 401. City emergency services officials will prepare a report on how to keep residents living near the highway safe and suggest actions for the city and recommendations for provincial and federal governments.

title: Request to Review the Nomination Requirements for Mayoral Candidates - by Councillor Stephen Holyday, seconded by Councillor Nick Mantas
summary: Toronto City Council recently decided against reviewing the current requirements for mayoral candidates. This means that rules like nomination fees and the number of signatures needed to run for mayor will remain unchanged, keeping the existing framework for who can qualify to run in future elections.

title: Our Plan Toronto: Recommendations on Seventy Employment Area Conversion Requests and Chapter 7 Site and Area Specific Policy Review - Final Report
summary: Toronto City Council has changed the planning rules for several properties, converting land previously reserved for employment uses into areas where homes, businesses, and community spaces can be built. This decision, part of "Our Plan Toronto," guides future development across the city and helps shape how various neighborhoods will grow.

title: Our Plan Toronto: Recommendations on Seventy Employment Area Conversion Requests and Chapter 7 Site and Area Specific Policy Review - Final Report
Introduction of General Bills and Confirming Bills
summary: At its July 19-20 meeting, City Council formally approved a range of decisions from previous meetings by passing numerous by-laws. This standard procedural step makes previously adopted items, such as new zoning rules or special charges, legally binding.

title: 750 Huntingwood Drive - Zoning Amendment - Decision Report - Approval
summary: Toronto City Council has approved changes to the zoning rules for the property at 750 Huntingwood Drive. This decision allows for specific types of development on this site, which will impact what can be built and how the land can be used in the local neighbourhood.

title: 875 Morningside Avenue (840 Military Trail) Official Plan and Zoning Amendment - Decision Report - Approval
summary: City Council approved changes to Toronto's long-term development plan and local zoning rules for 875 Morningside Avenue (840 Military Trail). This decision allows for new construction on the property, which will change the character and use of the area.

title: Review of the Order Paper
Review of the Order Paper
summary: City Council formally approved its meeting agenda for July 19 and 20, including adjustments to the order of business and which items would be discussed. This ensures that important city matters, such as the refugee crisis and gender-based violence, are prioritized for debate by councillors.

title: Securing Shelter and Support for Refugees - by Mayor Olivia Chow, seconded by Deputy Mayor Jennifer McKelvie
summary: Toronto City Council is taking steps to support refugees by immediately providing temporary shelter for hundreds of individuals and committing funds to help 1,350 households find permanent housing. The city approved a $6.67 million contribution from its reserve fund to match provincial funding for the Canada-Ontario Housing Benefit. The city will also work with the federal government to establish a welcome centre at Pearson Airport for new arrivals, while urging higher levels of government for more funding.

title: Amendment to Non-Competitive Blanket Contract Number 47024284 with Canadian Red Cross Society
summary: City Council has increased funding and extended a contract with the Canadian Red Cross to continue providing emergency lodging and related support services. This additional $10 million ensures vital assistance for vulnerable residents, likely refugees and asylum claimants, through the end of 2023.

title: 2 Cora Crescent - Request City Solicitor to appeal Committee of Adjustment decision to the Toronto Local Appeal Body - by Councillor Michael Thompson, seconded by Deputy Mayor Jennifer McKelvie
summary: Toronto City Council has decided to appeal a local planning board's decision regarding a property at 2 Cora Crescent. The city's legal team will challenge a request for a minor change to building rules for this address, aiming to ensure development aligns with city planning guidelines and protects the neighbourhood.

title: Alcohol in Parks Pilot Program
Alcohol in Parks Pilot Program
Alcohol in Parks Pilot Program
summary: Toronto City Council has approved a temporary pilot program allowing adults to drink alcohol in 27 designated city parks from August 2 to October 9, 2023. This change to park rules means residents can enjoy alcoholic beverages responsibly in these select public spaces for a limited time this summer and fall.

title: Two-Stroke Engine Small Equipment: Steps to Pursue a Ban
Two-Stroke Engine Small Equipment: Steps to Pursue a Ban
summary: Toronto City Council has expressed support for a potential ban on two-stroke small engine equipment, like leaf blowers and lawnmowers, to protect human health and the environment. City staff will now identify the resources needed to develop and implement this ban, with a goal to introduce a by-law if funding is secured in the 2024 budget. The city will also engage with residents and businesses to explore support programs for transitioning to cleaner equipment.

title: Planning for an E-Scooter Pilot
summary: Toronto City Council is exploring how e-scooters and other micromobility devices could safely operate in the city. Council has directed staff to develop a comprehensive Micromobility Strategy by early 2024, including recommendations on launching potential pilot projects. This strategy will focus on pedestrian safety, accessibility, climate benefits, liability insurance, and the mandatory use of helmets, aiming to ensure safety and order on city streets.

title: Planning for an E-Scooter Pilot
2022 Performance Appraisal - Medical Officer of Health
summary: Toronto's Board of Health has approved the 2022 performance review for the city's Medical Officer of Health. While the details of the appraisal remain confidential to protect the individual's privacy, this action ensures accountability for a key public health official.

title: 31 Ellington Drive - Application for Fence Exemption
summary: The Scarborough Community Council approved an exemption for the owners of 31 Ellington Drive, allowing them to keep a backyard wood fence that is currently taller than the city's 2-meter height limit. This decision means the existing fence can remain as long as it is maintained, but any future replacement must follow city bylaws.

title: 146 Three Valleys Drive - Application to Remove a Private Tree
summary: The North York Community Council has approved a resident's request to remove a large, healthy Norway spruce tree from their property at 146 Three Valleys Drive, overturning a recommendation from City staff to deny the permit. To offset the loss of the tree, the resident is required to provide five replacement trees, either by planting them on-site or by making a payment to the City for tree planting elsewhere. This decision impacts the local urban tree canopy and how private tree removals are handled in the community.

title: 23 Laurentia Crescent - Request for a Fence Exemption
summary: The North York Community Council refused a request from the owner of 23 Laurentia Crescent to keep a fence that does not meet city standards. This means the property owner must change the fence to comply with Toronto's fence by-laws.

title: Seneca Hill Drive and Kingslake Road - Enhancing Traffic Safety
summary: North York Community Council has approved the installation of an all-way stop sign at Seneca Hill Drive and Kingslake Road. This decision aims to improve pedestrian safety for local residents and children walking to Seneca Hill Public School, addressing long-standing concerns about speeding vehicles and poor visibility on the curved road.

title: Call to Order
SmartTrack Stations Program - Provincial Funding Update
SmartTrack Stations Program - Provincial Funding Update
summary: Toronto City Council has approved accepting up to $226 million in provincial funding for the SmartTrack Stations Program. This decision helps move forward the construction of new transit stations across the city, aiming to improve connectivity and public transit options for residents.

title: Temporary Signs By-law Review
Temporary Signs By-law Review
Temporary Signs By-law Review
Temporary Signs By-law Review
summary: Toronto City Council has updated its rules for temporary signs like A-frames and portable signs displayed on public property. The new regulations require signs to be placed closer to the curb, maintain specific distances from street infrastructure, and ensure a wider 2.5-metre clear path for pedestrians on many busy streets. These changes aim to improve sidewalk accessibility, reduce clutter, and ensure public safety for residents.

title: Creation of a Woodbine Special Sign District
summary: City Council has created a new "Woodbine Special Sign District" for the area around 555 Rexdale Boulevard, home to Woodbine Racetrack and Casino. This change allows for and regulates various types of electronic and static advertising signs within this designated district, which may alter the visual landscape for local businesses.

title: 4, 6, 8 and 10 Beamish Drive - Zoning By-law Amendment Application - Ontario Land Tribunal Hearing - Request for Directions
4, 6, 8 and 10 Beamish Drive - Zoning By-law Amendment Application - Ontario Land Tribunal Hearing - Request for Directions
summary: Toronto City Council has approved a settlement for a proposed development at 4, 6, 8, and 10 Beamish Drive. This means the city will support revised building plans at a provincial tribunal, aiming to resolve a dispute over the property's zoning rules and ensure a planned outcome for the neighbourhood.

title: Governance Review of Yonge-Dundas Square - by Councillor Chris Moise, seconded by Councillor Shelley Carroll
Governance Review of Yonge-Dundas Square - by Councillor Chris Moise, seconded by Councillor Shelley Carroll
Governance Review of Yonge-Dundas Square - by Councillor Chris Moise, seconded by Councillor Shelley Carroll
summary: Toronto City Council has approved a review of how Yonge-Dundas Square is managed and operated, exploring options like public operation similar to Nathan Phillips Square. This decision aims to find the best future model for the prominent public space, with recommendations expected early next year. During this review, the current management board is asked to pause new external funding agreements that might impact its future structure.

title: Request to Implement an Adequate Temperature By-law - by Councillor Shelley Carroll, seconded by Councillor Amber Morley
summary: Toronto City Council has decided to explore new rules for rental housing temperatures. Staff will investigate creating a by-law that would require landlords to maintain specific heating and cooling temperatures in rental units, and to offer cooling options for tenants during heatwaves, with a report due early next year. This initiative aims to improve tenant health and comfort in extreme weather conditions.

title: Request to Implement an Adequate Temperature By-law - by Councillor Shelley Carroll, seconded by Councillor Amber Morley
Critical Measures to Restore Ecology and Preserve the History and Culture of Hanlan's Point Beach and Surrounding Area - by Councillor Ausma Malik, seconded by Deputy Mayor Jennifer McKelvie
summary: City Council has approved plans to expand the clothing-optional beach at Hanlan's Point and will review changes to local rules to support this. The city will also start planning urgent projects to restore an eroded section of the beach, create a new paved trail, and install fencing to protect the environment and commemorate its historical significance to 2SLGBTQI+ communities.

title: Exploring Toronto as a Charter City - by Councillor Jamaal Myers, seconded by Deputy Mayor Jennifer McKelvie
Exploring Toronto as a Charter City - by Councillor Jamaal Myers, seconded by Deputy Mayor Jennifer McKelvie
Exploring Toronto as a Charter City - by Councillor Jamaal Myers, seconded by Deputy Mayor Jennifer McKelvie
summary: Toronto City Council decided to explore becoming a "Charter City," a move that would give the city more direct control over its own affairs, such as planning, taxation, and elections. City staff will now investigate the legal process and how to engage residents on potentially enshrining these powers within the Canadian Constitution.

title: Requesting Federal Government Support for the City's Climate Goals - by Councillor Paula Fletcher, seconded by Councillor Alejandra Bravo
Requesting Federal Government Support for the City's Climate Goals - by Councillor Paula Fletcher, seconded by Councillor Alejandra Bravo
summary: Toronto City Council has asked the federal government to issue Clean Electricity Regulations that prevent any increase in gas-fired power generation at the Portlands Energy Centre. This move supports the city's climate goals by aiming to reduce carbon emissions and promote cleaner energy sources for Toronto residents.

title: Graphic Image Delivery - by Councillor Dianne Saxe, seconded by Councillor Paula Fletcher
summary: City Council decided to send a motion regarding the delivery of graphic images, like flyers, to the Executive Committee for further review. This means the committee will discuss potential rules or changes related to how these materials are distributed in the city before Council makes a final decision.

title: City Comments on the Proposed Provincial Planning Statement
summary: City Council formally submitted its concerns and requested changes to the Province's proposed planning statement, which will significantly impact how Toronto develops. The city is advocating for policies that maintain strong affordable housing requirements, promote dense growth around transit, and prioritize building within existing areas over urban sprawl.

title: Appointment of Public Member to the Toronto Hydro Board
