summary: Toronto City Council has appointed Nicole Martin to the Toronto Hydro Board of Directors. This ensures qualified oversight for Toronto's electricity distribution company, which delivers power to homes and businesses across the city.

title: Redirecting the Proceeds of the Sale of a Residual Portion of City-owned land at 276 Lauder Avenue - by Councillor Alejandra Bravo, seconded by Councillor Ausma Malik
summary: Toronto City Council has decided that the money from selling a city-owned property at 276 Lauder Avenue will be set aside for a specific purpose. These funds will be used to purchase new parkland in Ward 9 (Davenport), ensuring more green spaces for residents in that area.

title: Relocation of Proposed Modular Housing and Designation of 175 Cummer Avenue Green Space as Parkland - by Councillor Lily Cheng, seconded by Councillor Nick Mantas
Relocation of Proposed Modular Housing and Designation of 175 Cummer Avenue Green Space as Parkland - by Councillor Lily Cheng, seconded by Councillor Nick Mantas
summary: Council decided not to approve a proposal to move planned modular housing from 175 Cummer Avenue and formally protect the green space there as parkland. This means the modular housing project at this location is likely to continue as planned, and the green space will not gain official park status through this motion.

title: Improvements to the Modular Housing at 175 Cummer Avenue - by Councillor Lily Cheng, seconded by Councillor Jamaal Myers
summary: Toronto City Council did not approve a motion that proposed improvements for the modular housing site at 175 Cummer Avenue. This means the suggested upgrades will not proceed, which could affect residents and impact the ongoing zoning bylaw appeal for this site at the Ontario Land Tribunal.

title: Staying on Top of Sinkholes: Coordinating Toronto Transit Commission and Toronto Water Preventative Inspections - by Councillor Paula Fletcher, seconded by Councillor Ausma Malik
summary: City Council has approved a plan to improve coordination between the Toronto Transit Commission and Toronto Water. They will work together to proactively inspect sewers along streetcar lines, aiming to prevent sinkholes and ensure safer roads and transit.

title: Improving CaféTO - by Councillor Paula Fletcher, seconded by Councillor Alejandra Bravo
summary: City Council has requested a comprehensive review of the popular CaféTO program, which allows restaurants to expand their patios onto public spaces. The review, due by the end of 2023, aims to improve permit processing, communication with businesses, and the program's overall operations for 2024, including considering financial assistance for patio setup costs and potential changes to program fees.

title: 4500 Jane Street - Zoning By-law Amendment Application - Decision Report - Approval
summary: Toronto City Council has approved new zoning rules for a property at 4500 Jane Street, ensuring the existing 164 rental units remain protected for at least 20 years. This decision prevents their conversion to condos or demolition, providing stability for current residents. The property owner must also undertake significant building improvements at no cost to tenants and mitigate construction impacts.

title: 40 kilometres per hour Speed Limit on Major Arterial Roads
summary: Toronto City Council has approved reducing the speed limit on many major arterial roads in central Toronto wards, including Parkdale-High Park, Davenport, University-Rosedale, Toronto-St. Paul’s, Toronto Centre, and Toronto-Danforth. This change will set the maximum speed to 40 kilometres per hour, aiming to improve safety for pedestrians, cyclists, and drivers in these areas.

title: 1-3 Concorde Gate and 10-12 Concorde Place - Zoning By-law Amendment - Ontario Land Tribunal Appeal - Request for Directions
summary: The City Council has approved a settlement for a development proposal at Concorde Gate and Concorde Place, which was under appeal. This decision allows the development to proceed, but only after the developer meets specific conditions, including fully covering the cost of any necessary infrastructure upgrades in the area.

title: Call to Order
summary: City Council formally began its meeting by observing a moment of silence for community members who passed away, and acknowledging the traditional Indigenous territories where Toronto is located. This procedural item officially opened the Council meeting, setting the stage for subsequent decisions and discussions.

title: Appointment of Deputy City Manager, Development and Growth
summary: Toronto City Council has appointed Jag Sharma as the new Deputy City Manager for Development and Growth, effective August 14, 2023. This crucial role oversees how the city plans for new housing, infrastructure, and overall urban development, impacting how Toronto grows and evolves.

title: Appointment of Deputy City Manager, Infrastructure Services
summary: Toronto City Council has approved the appointment of Will Johnston as the new Deputy City Manager for Infrastructure Services. In this key role, he will oversee the city's vital infrastructure projects and services, impacting everything from roads to water systems across Toronto.

title: Our Plan Toronto: Recommendations on Forty-Five Employment Area Conversion Requests - Final Report
summary: City Council adopted a new Official Plan Amendment, which makes decisions about the future use of 45 specific employment areas across Toronto. This amendment determines which lands will continue to be protected for jobs and businesses, and which can be considered for conversion to other uses like housing, influencing the city's future development.

title: 880-882 and 888 Eastern Avenue and 74-80 Knox Avenue - Zoning By-law Amendment Application - Decision Report - Approval
summary: New development is moving forward at Eastern Avenue and Knox Avenue after City Council approved a zoning change. The approval requires the developer to include six affordable rental housing units in the project, which will be rented at prices targeting 80% of average market rent for 25 years. This decision will create more affordable housing options in the community.

title: Appointment of Public Members to the Toronto Transit Commission Board
Appointment of Public Members to the Toronto Transit Commission Board
summary: City Council has appointed two new public members, Fenton Jagdeo and Julie Osborne, to the Toronto Transit Commission (TTC) Board for a four-year term. These individuals will help oversee and guide decisions for Toronto's public transit system, influencing service and operations for riders across the city.

title: Canada-Wide Early Learning and Child Care Agreement Implementation in Toronto - Update
summary: Toronto City Council is urging the provincial and federal governments to improve the Canada-Wide Early Learning and Child Care program in the city. Council wants more funded spaces to meet demand, updated income thresholds for fee subsidies to help low-income families, and increased funding for staff wages and benefits. They are also requesting capital funding to build more non-profit child care facilities.

title: Cycling Network Plan - 2023 Cycling Infrastructure Installation - Third Quarter Updates
Cycling Network Plan - 2023 Cycling Infrastructure Installation - Third Quarter Updates
summary: Toronto City Council has approved the installation of several new bikeway projects, including cycle tracks and bike lanes, on various streets across the city. This decision expands Toronto's cycling network, aiming to provide more safe and accessible routes for residents to cycle.

title: 94 Cortleigh Boulevard - Inclusion on the Heritage Register
summary: Toronto has added the property at 94 Cortleigh Boulevard to the City's Heritage Register. This decision recognizes the cultural heritage value of the 1914 Arts and Crafts style home and provides it with interim protection from demolition or significant alteration.

title: 38 Walmer Road - Notice of Intention to Designate a Property under Part IV, Section 29 of the Ontario Heritage Act
summary: Toronto is moving to protect the Walmer Road Baptist Church at 38 Walmer Road by declaring its intention to designate it as a heritage property. This decision aims to preserve the building's significant historical and architectural value, ensuring this over 130-year-old community institution remains a key part of the city's heritage.

title: Mid-Rise Buildings Rear Transition Performance Standards Review & Draft Update
summary: A city committee has approved public consultations on potential changes to design rules for mid-rise buildings, specifically how they are built next to other properties. These updates aim to provide more flexibility in design, which could help increase housing supply and offer more housing options across Toronto. This work is part of the city's plan to streamline housing development.

title: Mid-Rise Buildings Rear Transition Performance Standards Review & Draft Update
Award of Negotiated Request for Proposal Doc3823446646 to Various Suppliers for the Provision of School Crossing Guard Services at various City of Toronto locations
summary: Toronto City Council has approved contracts to secure school crossing guard services across the city. This decision ensures children's safety around schools for an initial period of two years, with options to extend these services for three additional one-year terms.

title: 64 Wellesley Street East - Alterations to a Heritage Property and Authority to Enter into a Heritage Easement Agreement
summary: City Council is allowing alterations to the heritage-protected building at 64 Wellesley Street East. This decision requires the owner to enter a legal agreement to preserve the building's historic elements, ensuring its character is maintained while allowing for new development.

title: 1404 and 1420 Yonge Street - Alterations to Heritage Properties and Authority to Enter into a Heritage Easement Agreement and 1404 and 1406 Yonge Street - Demolition of Heritage Properties
summary: The Toronto Preservation Board has recommended approving significant changes to heritage properties on Yonge Street, allowing for the construction of a 50-storey building at 1420 Yonge Street. This decision also includes the demolition of some heritage features at 1404 and 1406 Yonge Street, with conditions requiring a conservation plan and a Heritage Easement Agreement. These changes will impact the character and future development of this important Yonge Street corridor.

title: St. Lawrence Centre Redevelopment - Sponsorship Naming Rights Consultant
summary: TO Live's Board has approved hiring a consultant to find sponsors and secure naming rights for the redeveloped St. Lawrence Centre. This move aims to generate revenue through corporate partnerships, helping to fund the major renovation of this important city cultural venue.

title: 136 Craighurst Avenue - Application to Remove a Private Tree
summary: North York Community Council has decided to deny a resident's appeal to remove a privately owned black walnut tree at 136 Craighurst Avenue. Despite reasons given by the homeowner like structural issues and allergies, the City's Urban Forestry deemed the tree healthy and maintainable, meaning it will remain on the property.

title: Extending 2023 Application Due Dates for Property Tax and Utility Relief programs for Low-Income Seniors and Low-Income Disabled Persons  - by Councillor Anthony Perruzza, seconded by Councillor James Pasternak
summary: Toronto City Council has extended the application deadline for property tax, waste collection, and water bill relief programs for low-income seniors and disabled persons. The new deadline is October 31, 2023, giving eligible residents more time to apply for financial assistance with these household expenses.

title: A Clean Energy Future for Toronto - by Councillor Paula Fletcher, seconded by Councillor Gord Perks
summary: Toronto City Council has taken a stand against new fossil fuel power plants, specifically opposing any new proposals to burn natural gas for electricity within city limits. This decision pushes for a cleaner energy future for residents by urging the Province of Ontario to invest in energy efficiency and conservation programs instead of expanding fossil fuel production.

title: Follow Up Report on the UrbanHensTO Backyard Hens Pilot Program
summary: Toronto has decided to end its UrbanHensTO backyard hens pilot program, meaning residents can no longer apply to keep hens. Existing participants are allowed to keep their current hens under the original program rules, and the city will study backyard hen programs in other cities for a potential relaunch in 2025, pending Avian Flu risks.

title: Employment Services Transformation: Recommendations on the City's Role
summary: Toronto City Council has expressed its willingness to potentially manage local employment services for the Province, but only if the Province updates its performance targets and improves financial and training support for job seekers. This aims to better coordinate workforce development programs and provide more effective job support for low-income residents and other vulnerable communities across the city.

title: Supporting Small Businesses: Calling on the Federal Government to Forgive a Portion of the Canada Emergency Business Account Loans - by Councillor Paula Fletcher, seconded by Councillor Alejandra Bravo
summary: Toronto City Council has urged the Federal government to help small businesses struggling to repay their Canada Emergency Business Account (CEBA) loans. The Council is requesting a multi-year, no-interest repayment plan and for the government to forgive $20,000 of the loan for businesses that agree to this plan. This initiative aims to support local businesses, helping them recover from the pandemic and continue contributing to Toronto's economy.

title: Micro-mobility Couriers - by Councillor Dianne Saxe, seconded by Councillor Shelley Carroll
Micro-mobility Couriers - by Councillor Dianne Saxe, seconded by Councillor Shelley Carroll
summary: Toronto City Council is addressing public safety concerns related to the growing use of micro-mobility vehicles like e-bikes and scooters, particularly by delivery couriers. To make sidewalks safer for pedestrians and ensure compliance with road rules, the city will explore options for requiring commercial delivery vehicles to have unique identifiers, ask courier companies to educate their staff, and research ways to deter dangerous sidewalk riding.

title: Status Report on the Gerrard-Carlaw North Transit Oriented Community
summary: In a recent decision, City Council adopted a report on the Gerrard-Carlaw North development, urging the provincial government and developers to commit to 20% affordable housing for 50 years. Council also requested an assessment of how 1098 new housing units would impact the Matty Eckler Community Centre by the end of 2023.

title: Review of policies and procedures related to Warming Centres
Review of policies and procedures related to Warming Centres
summary: Responding to a declared homelessness emergency, Toronto City Council moved to improve winter services and secure critical funding. The city will develop a long-term strategy for warming centres, prioritize 24-hour respite sites, and requested $5 million from other levels of government for additional warming centres and $20 million for housing benefits to help 1,600 to 2,000 households leave shelters. This decision aims to enhance support for Toronto residents experiencing homelessness.

title: Reducing Toronto Transit Commission Vehicle Delays for Streetcar and RapidTO Routes - by Councillor Chris Moise, seconded by Councillor Jon Burnside
summary: To improve transit reliability, Toronto City Council has directed a review of traffic light adjustments aimed at reducing delays for streetcars and RapidTO buses. This means staff will investigate adding or extending left-turn restrictions or dedicated left-turn signal phases at intersections to help public transit move more efficiently.

title: Update on Alcohol in Parks Pilot - by Councillor Shelley Carroll, seconded by Councillor Chris Moise
Update on Alcohol in Parks Pilot - by Councillor Shelley Carroll, seconded by Councillor Chris Moise
Update on Alcohol in Parks Pilot - by Councillor Shelley Carroll, seconded by Councillor Chris Moise
Update on Alcohol in Parks Pilot - by Councillor Shelley Carroll, seconded by Councillor Chris Moise
summary: Toronto City Council has decided to move forward with a pilot program that will allow responsible personal alcohol consumption in some city parks. City staff will consult with Councillors to identify suitable parks in interested wards and develop a plan for the pilot to run from August 5 to October 9, 2023. This initiative aims to explore if residents can safely enjoy alcohol in designated park areas, while also considering public health, safety, and operational needs.

title: 2023 Education Property Tax Levy and Clawback Rate By-Law
summary: Toronto City Council has set the 2023 property tax rates that will fund education, collecting over $2.14 billion from local property owners. While most of these funds go to the provincial government for schools, the City keeps a small portion to cover administrative costs. Council also adopted rules to adjust tax decreases for some commercial and industrial properties, and asked the province to let Toronto keep all property taxes it collects.

title: Data on the Health of People Experiencing Homelessness
Data on the Health of People Experiencing Homelessness
summary: Toronto City Council has approved a partnership with Unity Health Toronto and Inner City Health Associates to explore developing a shared platform aimed at improving health and housing outcomes for people experiencing homelessness. This decision also requires city staff to provide more frequent and aligned reports on the deaths of people experiencing homelessness, increasing transparency and accountability for this vulnerable population.

title: Pedestrian Safety on Avenue Road, Bloor Street to St. Clair Avenue West
summary: City Council has adopted several measures to improve pedestrian safety on Avenue Road, reducing the speed limit between Bloor Street and St. Clair Avenue West to 40 km/h. The city will also install physical barriers near a church and has asked police to increase speed enforcement, with further studies planned for additional safety upgrades.

title: A Request to Review City Partnership Programs - by Councillor Stephen Holyday, seconded by Councillor Shelley Carroll
summary: Toronto City Council has approved a request for the City Manager to investigate how the city can earn more money through sponsorships, naming rights, and advertising. This review aims to find new revenue opportunities from public assets and materials, helping the city diversify its income and fund services.

title: 15, 19, 21 and 23 Toryork Drive - Ontario Land Tribunal Hearing - Request for Directions
summary: Toronto City Council has settled a land dispute concerning properties on Toryork Drive, avoiding a longer hearing at the Ontario Land Tribunal. As part of the agreement, a developer will receive a credit on their parkland fees if they construct new park improvements, potentially benefiting local residents with new green space.

title: Customer Experience Division Annual Report - 2022
summary: After reviewing the Customer Experience Division's 2022 report, City Council approved plans to improve how residents interact with city services like 311. These plans include studying new ways to engage residents, especially vulnerable communities, and ensuring their feedback is better incorporated into service delivery.

title: High Park Movement Strategy - Final Report
High Park Movement Strategy - Final Report
High Park Movement Strategy - Final Report
summary: City Council has decided to permanently close High Park to private visitor vehicles on Saturdays, Sundays, and statutory holidays, making the park more accessible for pedestrians and cyclists. This strategy will include developing a recreational cycling pilot program for 2023 and requesting the TTC to explore improved transit service within and around the park.

title: Update Downsview: Draft Secondary Plan and Draft Community Development Plan Priorities - Status Report
summary: City Council has approved a plan to guide the future growth and development of the Downsview area, using a draft secondary plan and community development priorities as a starting point. This decision means city staff will now use these drafts for more public consultations, discussions with stakeholders, and to assess new development proposals. Staff will also develop a full Community Development Plan and identify its budget needs for consideration in 2025, ensuring a focus on accessible, seniors-friendly, and diverse housing options.

title: Tourist Area Exemptions for Holiday Shopping for Yorkdale Shopping Centre - Amendment to Toronto Municipal Code Chapter 510-5
summary: City officials are developing new rules for when stores can open on holidays, especially in designated "tourist areas." This means places like Yorkdale Shopping Centre could eventually be allowed to open on more holidays, changing shopping options for residents.

title: Delta Bingo & Gaming Proposed Relocation to 360 Evans Avenue - by Councillor Amber Morley, seconded by Councillor Frances Nunziata
summary: Toronto City Council has approved the relocation of the Delta Bingo & Gaming charitable bingo hall. This moves the "Class A" facility from 1799 St. Clair Avenue West to a new address at 360 Evans Avenue, affecting where residents can participate in charitable gaming that supports local community groups.

title: Extending 2023 Application Due Dates for Property Tax and Utility Relief programs for Low-Income Seniors and Low-Income Disabled Persons  - by Councillor Anthony Perruzza, seconded by Councillor James Pasternak
Honouring Canadian Music Legend Gordon Lightfoot - by Councillor Mike Colle, seconded by Deputy Mayor Jennifer McKelvie
summary: Toronto City Council has taken the first step to honour Canadian music icon Gordon Lightfoot by asking for a plan to create a statue. This plan will explore possible locations, such as Massey Hall, and suggest funding sources like developer contributions or private donations, aiming to fund it without directly using general city funds.

title: Initiating a Mayor's Roundtable to Develop a Response Plan to Reduce and End Homelessness - by Councillor James Pasternak, seconded by Councillor Dianne Saxe
summary: Toronto City Council has requested the incoming Mayor to establish a "Mayor's Roundtable to End Homelessness." This group will bring together people with lived experience, advocates, and experts to develop a city-wide plan to reduce and ultimately end homelessness in Toronto. This initiative aims to create a coordinated and informed strategy to tackle a critical social issue facing the city.

title: Deeming Members of Council Ineligible for Jury Duty - by Councillor James Pasternak, seconded by Councillor Paula Fletcher
Deeming Members of Council Ineligible for Jury Duty - by Councillor James Pasternak, seconded by Councillor Paula Fletcher
summary: City Council has formally asked the Ontario government to amend provincial law to make City Councillors ineligible for jury duty while they are serving in office. This request aims to ensure that elected officials can fully dedicate their time and attention to city matters without interruption.

title: A Clean Energy Future for Toronto - by Councillor Paula Fletcher, seconded by Councillor Gord Perks

Recognizing the Importance of Queer History and the Ongoing Two-Spirit, Lesbian, Gay, Bisexual, Transgender and Queer (2SLGBTQ+) Community Presence at Hanlan's Point Beach and Surrounding Area, Ward 10 - by Councillor Ausma Malik, seconded by Councillor Chris Moise
summary: City Council has officially recognized the long and important queer history and community presence at Hanlan's Point Beach. To ensure this legacy continues, the city will involve 2SLGBTQ+ community groups in planning for Toronto Island and establish an advisory group to help safeguard the area from discrimination and violence. This decision helps preserve the historical significance of Hanlan's Point and makes the space more inclusive and safe for everyone.

title: Proclaiming November 17 as Gordon Lightfoot Day in the City of Toronto - by Councillor James Pasternak, seconded by Deputy Mayor Jennifer McKelvie
summary: Toronto City Council has officially proclaimed November 17 as "Gordon Lightfoot Day" in the city. This decision honours the late legendary Canadian singer-songwriter, recognizing his profound musical legacy and connection to Toronto.

title: Redirecting the Proceeds of the Sale of a Residual Portion of City-owned land at 276 Lauder Avenue - by Councillor Alejandra Bravo, seconded by Councillor Ausma Malik
summary: A proposal to redirect money from the sale of City-owned land at 276 Lauder Avenue was not discussed by City Council. Councillors voted against introducing the motion, meaning the decision on how to use these funds will be delayed or require a new proposal.

title: Diversifying Civic Appointees on the Toronto Transit Commission Board - by Councillor Jamaal Myers, seconded by Councillor Amber Morley
summary: City Council sent a proposal to the Executive Committee for further review, which aims to increase the diversity of non-councillor members appointed to the Toronto Transit Commission (TTC) board. This initiative seeks to bring a wider range of perspectives to the leadership overseeing Toronto's public transit system.

title: Diversifying Civic Appointees on the Toronto Transit Commission Board - by Councillor Jamaal Myers, seconded by Councillor Amber Morley
Authorization to Release Section 37 Funds to Rebuild the Community Garden at 133 Broadway Avenue - by Councillor Josh Matlow, seconded by Councillor Amber Morley
summary: Toronto City Council has approved allocating $50,000 from developer contributions to rebuild the community garden at 133 Broadway Avenue. These funds, collected from a nearby development as a community benefit, will be given to Toronto Community Housing to create a better green space for residents.

title: 73-83 Brooklawn Avenue - Request for City Solicitor to Appeal Committee of Adjustment Decision to the Toronto Local Appeal Body - by Councillor Gary Crawford, seconded by Councillor Paul Ainslie
summary: City Council has directed the City Solicitor to challenge a decision made by the Committee of Adjustment regarding development proposals for 73-83 Brooklawn Avenue. This action means the city will oppose certain plans for property changes, such as minor variances or subdivisions, to uphold local planning standards for residents in the Scarborough neighbourhood. The City Solicitor can also negotiate a settlement on the city's behalf.

title: Getting the Gardiner Right: Moving Forward with a New Study on Better Options for the Gardiner East - by Councillor Josh Matlow, seconded by Councillor Amber Morley
Getting the Gardiner Right: Moving Forward with a New Study on Better Options for the Gardiner East - by Councillor Josh Matlow, seconded by Councillor Amber Morley
Getting the Gardiner Right: Moving Forward with a New Study on Better Options for the Gardiner East - by Councillor Josh Matlow, seconded by Councillor Amber Morley
summary: Toronto City Council turned down a proposal to study new options for the Gardiner Expressway East. Instead, councillors voted to request the provincial government take over all future construction and maintenance costs for both the Gardiner and the Don Valley Parkway, aiming to alleviate a significant financial burden on city taxpayers.

title: Transit Priority Measures to Support Scarborough SRT Bus Replacement
Transit Priority Measures to Support Scarborough SRT Bus Replacement
Transit Priority Measures to Support Scarborough SRT Bus Replacement
summary: Toronto City Council has approved new priority bus lanes and traffic signal changes on several major roads in Scarborough. These measures will help the new bus service run more smoothly and quickly as it replaces the Scarborough RT, aiming to improve transit reliability for residents in the area.

title: Housing Now Initiative - 2023 Progress Update
summary: City Council has approved confidential plans for new affordable housing projects at several sites, including locations on Dundas Street West, Wilson Heights Boulevard, and Merton Street, as part of its Housing Now initiative. The Council also adopted instructions conditional on the Province of Ontario fully reimbursing the city for financial losses caused by the Bill 23, "More Homes Built Faster Act." This decision aims to advance housing goals while protecting city finances from provincial policy changes.

title: Call to Order
summary: City Council opened its meeting by acknowledging the traditional territories of Indigenous Peoples and observing moments of silence for community members who passed away, including former Deputy Mayor Michael Feldman, and for the discovery of unmarked graves at a former residential school. This formal beginning sets the stage for Council's work, reflecting on important community and historical contexts.

title: Fine Equity: Balancing Toronto Transit Commission Fare Evasion and Parking Fines - by Councillor Paula Fletcher, seconded by Councillor Alejandra Bravo
Fine Equity: Balancing Toronto Transit Commission Fare Evasion and Parking Fines - by Councillor Paula Fletcher, seconded by Councillor Alejandra Bravo
Fine Equity: Balancing Toronto Transit Commission Fare Evasion and Parking Fines - by Councillor Paula Fletcher, seconded by Councillor Alejandra Bravo
summary: Toronto City Council has directed the City Manager to ask the TTC to consider reducing the fines for not paying transit fares. The goal is to make these tickets more in line with other city transportation fines, promoting fairness for all riders.

title: 400 Rockcliffe Boulevard - Pedestrian Crossing Protection
summary: City Council has approved a new pedestrian crossing on Rockcliffe Boulevard, near Rockcliffe Court. This will create a safer way for pedestrians, especially students from the nearby middle school, to cross the street.

title: Nominal Licence Agreement with Conseil scolaire Viamonde for Non-exclusive use of Felstead Park
summary: City Council has approved an agreement allowing Conseil scolaire Viamonde, a French school board, to use a section of Felstead Park. This permits the school to use part of the park for its operations, while also requiring the school board to pay for improvements like new fencing and updated pathways for the entire park.

title: 4888 Dundas Street West - Notice of Intention to Designate a Property under Part IV, Section 29 of the Ontario Heritage Act
summary: Toronto City Council has moved to protect the property at 4888 Dundas Street West by initiating its designation under the Ontario Heritage Act. This action means the building is recognized for its cultural heritage value, ensuring its preservation and preventing significant changes or demolition without city approval.

title: TransformTO 2022 Annual Report: Laying the Foundation for Net Zero
TransformTO 2022 Annual Report: Laying the Foundation for Net Zero
summary: Toronto City Council has endorsed its TransformTO 2022 annual report, outlining progress towards achieving net-zero emissions. The city will advocate for federal and provincial funding to support its climate initiatives and update building standards to discourage the use of natural gas in new developments. These actions aim to reduce Toronto's carbon footprint and secure necessary resources for climate action.

title: Toronto Hydro and City of Toronto Memorandum of Understanding for Toronto Hydro's Climate Advisory Services
summary: To boost accountability on climate goals, Toronto's City Council decided that Toronto Hydro's climate actions will be included in the city's annual climate reporting. This means residents will soon have access to a new dashboard showing how Toronto Hydro is contributing to the city's overall climate strategy.

title: Expanding Housing Options in Neighbourhoods: Multiplex Study - Final Report
Expanding Housing Options in Neighbourhoods: Multiplex Study - Final Report
Expanding Housing Options in Neighbourhoods: Multiplex Study - Final Report
Expanding Housing Options in Neighbourhoods: Multiplex Study - Final Report
summary: City Council has approved changes to city rules that now allow multiplexes (buildings with 2-4 units) to be built in all residential areas across Toronto. This decision aims to create more housing options and diverse living arrangements within existing neighbourhoods, helping address the city's housing shortage.

title: Review of the Order Paper
summary: City Council finalized its daily agendas for the May 10-12 meetings, determining the order in which items would be debated and decided. This procedural step ensured that important issues like housing options and climate action reports were scheduled for discussion, setting the stage for major decisions affecting Toronto residents.

title: Our Plan Toronto: Employment Area Conversion Requests Status Update
summary: Toronto's Planning and Housing Committee recently received an update on "Our Plan Toronto," an ongoing review of requests to change how certain employment lands across the city can be used. This process considers proposals to convert areas currently designated for businesses and jobs into other uses, like housing, impacting the city's future development. A previous set of over 30 conversion requests is still awaiting approval from the provincial government.

title: Refund of Double-Repayment of Property Taxes - 50 Cumberland Street/37 Yorkville Avenue (Municipal Car Park 15)
summary: The Toronto Parking Authority (TPA) has approved a payment to a company's receiver to resolve a complex issue regarding property tax refunds for a municipal car park. This payment untangles a situation where property taxes were previously refunded to the TPA, and ensures correct financial accounting for the city's assets.

title: Summary of Legal Disputes - 2023 First Quarter Update
summary: The Toronto Parking Authority Board recently reviewed a confidential report outlining its ongoing legal disputes for the first quarter of 2023. This report provides updates on various legal challenges related to parking operations, Bike Share Toronto, and property management, ensuring the Authority continues to manage its risks with suitable insurance and legal counsel.

title: Facilities Management Services
summary: City Council has decided to restore cleaning hours for employees who clean city facilities, while also introducing new standardized cleaning methods that fit within the 2023 budget. This aims to ensure city buildings remain clean, address staffing impacts in collaboration with the union, and adapt to new office arrangements like shared desks as employees return to work.

title: Amendment to Blanket Contract Number 47021736 with Toronto Drydock Limited for the Dry Docking Ship Repair Services and/or Onsite Ship Repair Services
summary: City officials have approved an additional $2 million for repairs to the William Inglis passenger vessel. This funding is needed due to unexpected severe damage found during drydocking, requiring extensive repairs to its hull, roof, and other critical components to ensure it remains safe and certified for operation by Transport Canada.

title: Ontario Place Redevelopment - Declaration of Surplus
summary: A City committee has postponed a decision to declare city-owned land and water at Ontario Place as 'surplus,' a necessary step for a potential land exchange with the Province of Ontario. This means the City will not move forward with this part of the Ontario Place redevelopment until Council approves official plan changes and receives more information from the Province and Federal government.

title: Final Report from Black Scientists' Task Force on Vaccine Equity
summary: Toronto's Board of Health adopted recommendations from the Black Scientists’ Task Force on Vaccine Equity to address health inequities in Black communities. These actions include directing the Medical Officer of Health to integrate community-led research into health reporting and to explore establishing a permanent Black Health Task Force and ongoing mental health supports. The Board also requested the Province of Ontario and Ontario Health to collect race-based health data and tackle long-term COVID-19 effects impacting Black Torontonians.

title: Developing a Mental Health and Addictions Strategy for Toronto
summary: Toronto's public health board is developing a comprehensive strategy to address mental health and addictions across the city. This plan will include specific actions for substance use, integrate Indigenous perspectives, and consider the impact of social isolation. The board also calls on federal and provincial governments to increase support for mental health and harm reduction services, and to decriminalize simple drug possession.

title: 25 St. Mary Street - Official Plan Amendment,  Zoning Amendment, and Rental Housing Demolition Applications - Decision Report - Approval
committee: Toronto and East York Community Council
summary: Toronto City Council has approved the necessary planning and zoning changes for a new development at 25 St. Mary Street. As part of this approval, the developer will provide a non-profit child care facility designed to accommodate at least 62 children, contributing a valuable service to the community.

title: 25 St. Mary Street - Official Plan Amendment,  Zoning Amendment, and Rental Housing Demolition Applications - Decision Report - Approval
committee: Toronto and East York Community Council

title: Ontario Place - Official Plan Amendment and Zoning By-law Amendment - Status Report
committee: Toronto and East York Community Council
summary: Toronto's local councillors have endorsed a set of guiding principles to shape the future redevelopment of Ontario Place. These principles will guide city planners in reviewing development applications, ensuring public access to the waterfront, enhancing green spaces, improving connections with Exhibition Place, and incorporating Indigenous placemaking.

title: Metrolinx Lease
summary: Exhibition Place has approved a five-year lease agreement, allowing Metrolinx to rent office space starting May 2023, with an option to extend. This lease helps Metrolinx align its operations with the Ontario Line project's completion and will generate revenue from City-owned land.

title: 59 Palm Drive - Application to Remove a Private Tree
summary: Residents at 59 Palm Drive were denied permission to remove a healthy white spruce tree from their private property. The North York Community Council upheld the city's tree protection bylaws, which do not allow the removal of healthy and maintainable trees, prioritizing urban forest preservation.

title: 76 Covewood Street - Application to Remove Three Private Trees
summary: North York Community Council made a decision regarding an application to remove three private trees from a property. The Council denied the removal of two healthy spruce trees, but approved the removal of one pine tree. To protect the urban tree canopy, the homeowner is required to plant five new trees or pay money to the city instead.

title: Report on Councillor Josh Matlow's Tweets Criticizing City Staff
summary: Toronto City Council has found Councillor Josh Matlow in violation of its Code of Conduct for criticizing city staff on social media. As a result, Council has reprimanded him and decided to suspend his pay for 10 days, starting May 1. This action holds elected officials accountable for their conduct when interacting with and speaking about City employees.

title: Modernizing Bike Share Toronto Rate Structure
summary: Toronto City Council has updated the fees for Bike Share Toronto, affecting annual memberships, casual rides, and the cost of using e-bikes. These changes, effective April 3, 2023, also introduce new low-income options and allow installment payments to make the service more accessible to residents.

title: College Street Bikeways Extension
summary: Toronto City Council has approved a plan to study and design new bike lanes and pedestrian safety improvements along College Street, specifically between Lansdowne Avenue and Manning Avenue. This decision directs city staff to conduct public consultations with local residents and businesses, aiming to include the project in the city's 2025-2027 cycling network plan.

title: Federal Electoral Districts Redistribution affecting North York
summary: Toronto City Council has formally objected to a federal plan that would reduce the number of voting areas in the city. Council believes this change would further reduce Toronto residents' representation in government, where they are already underrepresented. They also requested that the federal commission provide more public notice and opportunities for residents to give input on any proposed boundary changes.

title: Federal Electoral Districts Redistribution affecting North York
Review and Consideration for a Housing Commissioner Role or Function at Ombudsman Toronto
summary: Toronto City Council has approved the creation of a new unit within Ombudsman Toronto, led by a Deputy Ombudsman for Housing. This unit will investigate systemic issues like housing discrimination and problems with the city's housing services, aiming to improve fairness and accessibility for residents.

title: Terms of Reference for the Housing Rights Advisory Committee for 2022-2026 City Council Term
summary: City Council has established a new Housing Rights Advisory Committee to provide advice on housing policy and independently monitor the city's housing programs. This committee will help ensure Toronto's housing initiatives are effective and advance residents' right to housing.

title: Making Electronic Participation for Council and its Local Boards Permanent
summary: Toronto City Council has made permanent the option for councillors and the public to participate electronically in all its meetings and those of its local boards. This decision aims to increase accessibility for both elected officials and residents, making it easier to engage in city governance. Additionally, the time allowed to achieve a quorum before meetings begin has been extended from 15 to 30 minutes.

title: Making Electronic Participation for Council and its Local Boards Permanent
Making Electronic Participation for Council and its Local Boards Permanent
Amendments to Existing Policies Related to Services to Members During an Election Period
Amendments to Existing Policies Related to Services to Members During an Election Period
summary: City Council updated rules on how Council Members can use city resources when they campaign for Mayor. Now, limits on using services like office staff or budgets will begin earlier once a Council Member announces their intent to run for Mayor, aiming to prevent taxpayer-funded resources from being used for election campaigns.

title: 1-3 Concorde Gate and 10-12 Concorde Place - Zoning By-law Amendment - Ontario Land Tribunal Appeal - Request for Directions
summary: Toronto City Council chose not to accept a request for directions regarding a settlement offer for a major development at 1-3 Concorde Gate and 10-12 Concorde Place. This means the city will continue to fight the developer's appeal at the Ontario Land Tribunal, affecting the future of this project and the surrounding community.

title: Accessing Justice and Courthouse Safety - Pausing the Closure of the Neighbourhood Courthouses in North York and Scarborough - by Councillor James Pasternak, seconded by Councillor Gary Crawford
summary: City Council has formally objected to the provincial government's plan to close local courthouses in North York and Scarborough. Councillors are asking the Attorney General of Ontario to pause these closures until the new downtown courthouse's capacity is fully assessed, as consolidating services could make it harder for residents to access justice.

title: Provincial/Municipal Bill 23 Audit - by Deputy Mayor Jennifer McKelvie, seconded by Councillor Gary Crawford
summary: Toronto City Council has approved an audit to examine the financial impacts of the provincial government's Bill 23. This audit will help the city understand how the new provincial housing law affects Toronto's finances and its ability to pay for new services and infrastructure related to growth.

title: Ombudsman Toronto Report: Investigation into the City's Processes for Clearing Encampments in 2021
Ombudsman Toronto Report: Investigation into the City's Processes for Clearing Encampments in 2021
Ombudsman Toronto Report: Investigation into the City's Processes for Clearing Encampments in 2021
summary: Toronto City Council has adopted a report from Ombudsman Toronto, directing city officials to implement its recommendations for improving how the city handles encampments. This decision aims to ensure residents in encampments receive proper social and health supports, improve communication, and prioritize their needs during clearing processes.

title: Determining Impact of Service Hours Changes at City Hall, Metro Hall and Civic Centres
Determining Impact of Service Hours Changes at City Hall, Metro Hall and Civic Centres
summary: Toronto City Council has decided to immediately pause any planned reductions in service hours at City Hall, Metro Hall, and all Civic Centres. This pause allows for a thorough review of how service changes would affect essential services, public access, safety, and wait times for residents before any final decisions are made.

title: Getting to the Root of the Issues - February 2023 Performance Update of Urban Forestry Contractors and City Crews
Getting to the Root of the Issues - February 2023 Performance Update of Urban Forestry Contractors and City Crews
summary: Toronto City Council is looking into ways to improve the city's tree maintenance services, including whether the City should provide these services directly instead of using contractors. This aims to ensure better care for urban trees across the city, with a report on options expected later this year.

title: Legal Advice in response to Provincial Order Requiring Individual Environmental Assessment for new Multi-Use Trail
summary: Toronto City Council has approved legal advice from its City Solicitor regarding a provincial order that demands a more extensive environmental assessment for a new multi-use trail. This decision will guide the city's response to the provincial requirement, which could impact the trail project's timeline and expenses for residents.

title: Limiting Provincial Influence in Council Decision-Making - by Councillor Alejandra Bravo, seconded by Councillor Paula Fletcher
summary: Toronto City Council has urged the incoming Mayor to reject new powers granted by provincial Bill 3, which allow the Mayor to veto Council decisions. This move aims to protect Council's collective decision-making authority and ensure that hiring and firing of senior city staff remain with the non-political City Manager.

title: Restoring Majority Rule to City Council - by Councillor Alejandra Bravo, seconded by Councillor Amber Morley
summary: Toronto City Council has approved a motion calling on the city's future mayor to publicly commit to protecting local decision-making. This means the mayor would not support major changes to how City Council operates or governs if they come from the provincial government, without first getting full support from City Council and Toronto residents.

title: Introduction of General Bills and Confirming Bills
summary: Toronto City Council passed a series of new city laws (by-laws) during its extended meeting. This included a significant decision to require a by-election for the Mayor's office, directly affecting the city's leadership. Council also formally approved other decisions made during the meeting, ensuring their legal validity.

title: Support for the City of Montreal's Motion regarding a High-speed Train in the Quebec-Toronto Corridor - by Councillor Paul Ainslie, seconded by Deputy Mayor Jennifer McKelvie
summary: Toronto City Council has officially supported Montreal's call for a high-speed train system to connect major cities like Toronto and Quebec City. This initiative, advocated over a high-frequency train, aims to significantly cut travel times, offer more reliable service, and provide environmental and economic benefits for the entire region.

title: Fine Equity: Balancing Toronto Transit Commission Fare Evasion and Parking Fines - by Councillor Paula Fletcher, seconded by Councillor Alejandra Bravo
summary: City Council has postponed a decision on a motion seeking to compare and balance fines for Toronto Transit Commission fare evasion with parking fines. This discussion, focused on 'Fine Equity,' aims to ensure penalties are fair and consistent for residents across different types of infractions, and will be revisited at the May 2023 City Council meeting.

title: Improving Road Safety by Expanding the Number of Automated Speed Enforcement Cameras and Operational Hours - by Deputy Mayor Jennifer McKelvie, seconded by Councillor Mike Colle
summary: To make Toronto's roads safer, the city will significantly expand its automated speed enforcement program. City staff are now tasked with exploring options to acquire 75 new speed cameras and will report back on the costs needed for the 2024 budget. This initiative aims to deter speeding and reduce traffic incidents across the city.

title: Exploring an Iranian Cultural District in Willowdale - by Councillor Lily Cheng, seconded by Councillor Brad Bradford
summary: City Council has decided to explore the creation of an Iranian Cultural District along Yonge Street in Willowdale, specifically between Steeles Avenue and Drewry Avenue. This initiative aims to recognize the area's vibrant Iranian culture and could lead to increased support for local businesses and cultural events once the city's cultural district criteria are established.

title: Getting the Gardiner Right: Moving Forward with a New Study on Better Options for the Gardiner East - by Councillor Josh Matlow, seconded by Councillor Amber Morley
summary: A proposal to study new options for the Gardiner East expressway was put on hold by City Council. Council decided to postpone its discussion and vote on this motion until its May 2023 meeting, delaying any immediate decisions on potential changes or studies for the Gardiner.

title: Protecting Access to Healthcare for all Ontarians - by Councillor Shelley Carroll, seconded by Councillor Chris Moise
summary: Toronto City Council has urged the provincial government to reverse its decision to end the Physician and Hospital Services for Uninsured Persons Program. This program provides essential healthcare to vulnerable residents who do not have health insurance, and its cancellation would impact many in the city.

title: Community Safety Issues and Response
Community Safety Issues and Response
summary: Toronto City Council has approved allocating up to $15 million from a reserve fund to improve safety on the Toronto Transit Commission (TTC). This funding will support a new strategy that focuses on community services, including mental health crisis response and expanding outreach teams, to address the root causes of violence and enhance rider safety. The Council also requested investigations into transit security in other cities and a plan to educate riders on reporting hate incidents.

title: Call to Order
summary: Toronto City Council formally opened its multi-day meeting, starting with O Canada and observing moments of silence to honour individuals and the Tseshaht First Nation. Council also acknowledged the traditional Indigenous territories on which the meeting was held, setting a respectful tone for its proceedings.

title: Intergovernmental Advocacy Efforts on Toronto's Refugee Claimant Shelter Response
summary: Toronto City Council is calling on the federal and provincial governments to provide more support for refugee claimants arriving in the city. Council is asking for extended funding for shelter services, a coordinated plan for housing new arrivals across Ontario, and faster processing of refugee protection claims, which helps ensure Toronto can continue to offer necessary assistance and receive fair compensation.

title: Islington Avenue and Prince George Drive/Ridgevalley Crescent - Pedestrian Crossing Protection
summary: To make crossing safer, Toronto City Council has approved the installation of new traffic lights at the intersection of Islington Avenue and Prince George Drive/Ridgevalley Crescent in Etobicoke Centre. This decision aims to improve pedestrian protection for local residents.

title: Traffic Control Signals - Morningside Avenue and Warnsworth Street
summary: City Council has approved installing new traffic control signals at Morningside Avenue and Warnsworth Street. This decision aims to improve safety and manage traffic flow more effectively for everyone using this intersection in Scarborough.

title: 399-405 Yonge Street - Zoning By-law Amendment - Ontario Land Tribunal Hearing - Request for Directions Report
summary: Toronto City Council has taken a stance in an upcoming land dispute, deciding to support a revised development plan for properties on Yonge Street near Gerrard. This plan allows for a tall building at 409-415 Yonge Street and, importantly, requires that any future tall building at 399-405 Yonge Street include a social housing program. This decision influences future building heights in the area and promotes the provision of affordable housing.

title: 2023 Financial Update and Outlook
summary: Toronto City Council has endorsed a plan to strongly advocate to the Federal and Provincial governments for a new financial framework and additional revenue tools. The city highlighted a significant strain on its budget, with $1.1 billion in property tax revenue currently used to fund services that are the responsibility of other government levels. This push aims to secure Toronto's long-term financial health and ensure the city can adequately fund essential services for residents.

title: Update on Alcohol in Parks Pilot - by Councillor Shelley Carroll, seconded by Councillor Chris Moise
summary: Toronto City Council has postponed its discussion on an update to the 'Alcohol in Parks Pilot' until its meeting in May 2023. This delay means that any potential new rules allowing residents to drink alcohol in city parks will also be pushed back, impacting what's allowed for the upcoming summer season.

title: SmartTrack Stations Program - Update
SmartTrack Stations Program - Update
summary: Toronto City Council has decided to negotiate with the provincial government to cover an estimated $234 million in increased costs for the SmartTrack Stations Program. This aims to ensure that Toronto residents are not solely responsible for these cost overruns for new transit stations. The City also put a condition on a new station's construction, stating it won't proceed until the Province commits to paying its share.

title: Review of the Order Paper
summary: At its meetings in late March, City Council approved the official agenda, known as the Order Paper, for the days, with some changes to the order of business. This decision set the priorities and sequence for important discussions, including financial updates and major infrastructure projects.

title: 153 and 185 Eastern Avenue - Notice of Intention to Designate a Property under Part IV, Section 29 of the Ontario Heritage Act
summary: Toronto City Council has decided to designate the properties at 153 and 185 Eastern Avenue as heritage sites. These historic industrial buildings are valued for their role in Toronto's rail history, reflecting the West Don Lands' past as an important industrial centre and ensuring their preservation.

title: 5, 7, 9, 11, 13 Charles Street West; 11, 13 Irwin Avenue; 10 Isabella Street, 145 St. Luke Lane and 750 and 756 Yonge Street - Inclusion of 11 Properties on the Heritage Register
summary: The city approved adding 11 properties in the Charles Street West, Irwin Avenue, Isabella Street, St. Luke Lane, and Yonge Street areas to its Heritage Register. This decision means these buildings are recognized for their historical importance and will have protections against demolition or significant changes, helping preserve Toronto's heritage for everyone.

title: Appointment of Public Members to the Toronto Police Services Board
summary: Toronto City Council has approved the appointment of Nadine Spencer as a public member to the Toronto Police Services Board until November 2026. This decision helps ensure public oversight and community representation in the governance of the city's police services.

title: Award of Ariba Document Number 3448368603 to CH2M Hill Canada Limited and Stantec Consulting Limited for Professional Engineering Services for Phase 5 of the Basement Flooding Protection Program
summary: To protect homes from flooding, City Council has authorized agreements with engineering firms CH2M Hill Canada Limited and Stantec Consulting Limited. This move secures professional engineering services for Phase 5 of the Basement Flooding Protection Program.

title: Striking a Sub-committee on Metrolinx's Ontario Line Construction
committee: Toronto and East York Community Council
summary: Councillors from areas affected by the Ontario Line construction have created a new committee. This committee will gather public feedback on Metrolinx's plans and develop recommendations to ensure the project best serves residents and addresses concerns about transparency and community impact.

title: Property Taxes, User Fees and Related Matters
Property Taxes, User Fees and Related Matters
Property Taxes, User Fees and Related Matters
summary: Toronto City Council has set the property tax rates for 2023, including a 5.50% increase for residential properties. This decision outlines how property tax increases will apply to different property types and determines the total amount of property tax revenue the city will collect to fund essential services.

title: 2023 Operating and Capital Budgets
summary: City Council has finalized Toronto's 2023 annual budget, outlining how the city will spend funds on services and infrastructure for the year. This adopted budget includes additional funding to expand programs for youth culture, rent assistance, homeless shelters, and various community initiatives.

title: Introduction of General Bills and Confirming Bills
summary: City Council formally approved several by-laws, including the annual property tax levy for 2023. These decisions also adjust the Municipal Accommodation Tax rate, update eligibility for property tax relief programs for low-income seniors and disabled persons, and modify administrative dates for the Vacant Home Tax. This ensures city operations are funded and revises various tax and service rules for residents.

title: Cold weather and the effects on those experiencing homelessness
Cold weather and the effects on those experiencing homelessness
summary: Toronto City Council has approved several measures to help people experiencing homelessness during cold weather. This includes calling on provincial and federal governments for more shelter space and refugee funding, and exploring new 24/7 drop-in and warming centres, potentially partnering with faith-based organizations. These actions aim to improve support and safety for vulnerable residents in the city.

title: F.G. Gardiner Expressway and Don Valley Parkway Closure - Bike for Brain Health
summary: Toronto City Council has approved the annual closure of the F.G. Gardiner Expressway and Don Valley Parkway for the 'Bike for Brain Health' event on specific Sundays in June for the next three years (2023-2025). This will mean significant traffic impacts on those days, and the city has asked the TTC to avoid subway closures to help manage congestion. Event organizers will cover any city costs related to securing construction zones during the closures.

title: Updating the Fair Wage Schedule, 2019-2022
summary: Toronto City Council has approved updated Fair Wage Schedules, which define the minimum pay rates for workers on City-funded contracts across various industries. Effective March 1, 2023, these updates ensure fair compensation for contractors' employees and establish clear rules for how the new rates apply to current and upcoming city projects.

title: Cycling Network Plan: 2021 ActiveTO Cycling Network Expansion Project Updates
Cycling Network Plan: 2021 ActiveTO Cycling Network Expansion Project Updates
Cycling Network Plan: 2021 ActiveTO Cycling Network Expansion Project Updates
summary: Toronto City Council has made the temporary ActiveTO cycling routes on Yonge Street and Bayview Avenue permanent. This decision means the bike lanes and multi-use trail installed in 2021 will remain in place, and the city will also study and implement improvements to address traffic and safety concerns on nearby residential streets.

title: Review and Consideration for a Housing Commissioner Role or Function at Ombudsman Toronto
summary: City Council decided to postpone a discussion about creating a new Housing Commissioner role within Ombudsman Toronto. Council requested more details from the Ombudsman on how this new function would operate and align with the city's ongoing efforts to ensure a right to housing for residents. This delay allows for further review of how best to address housing-related concerns.

title: Ensuring Transparency in the 2022 Election: Amending the Contribution Rebate Program
Ensuring Transparency in the 2022 Election: Amending the Contribution Rebate Program
summary: To boost transparency in future Toronto elections, City Council has approved a new rule for candidates. Candidates for Mayor and City Councillor will now be required to submit copies of all their campaign invoices along with their financial statements. This change provides residents with more detailed insight into how election campaigns are funded and where money is spent, promoting greater accountability.

title: Creating New Affordable and Supportive Homes for People Experiencing Homelessness
summary: To address homelessness, Toronto City Council adopted a plan to create new affordable and supportive homes. This involves asking the provincial and federal governments to dedicate surplus land for housing, directing Toronto Community Housing to lease sites to the City, and developing a plan to evaluate and improve the city's supportive housing program. The city will also report on funding allocations from the federal Rapid Housing Initiative to advance new projects.

title: Appointment of Public Members to the Board of Health
Appointment of Public Members to the Board of Health
Appointment of Public Members to the Board of Health
summary: City Council has appointed six new public members to the Board of Health, which oversees Toronto's public health services. These appointments also include school board trustees, ensuring the Board has the necessary leadership to guide important health initiatives for residents until November 2026.

title: Introduction of General Bills and Confirming Bills
summary: City Council formally approved a series of new by-laws, including those that confirm the decisions made at its recent meetings. This legal step ensures that all resolutions passed by Council are officially recognized and binding.

title: Regarding the Gardiner East Project
summary: City Council has sent an inquiry about the Gardiner East Project to the Infrastructure and Environment Committee for review. This means the committee will now discuss the project in more detail, ensuring ongoing consideration of this major infrastructure before any final decisions are made.

title: Extending the UrbanHensTO Pilot Project
summary: Toronto City Council has extended the UrbanHensTO pilot project. This means residents currently registered in the program can continue to keep up to four hens for personal egg production on their property until May 31, 2023.

title: 5238 Dundas Street West and 28 Jopling Avenue South - Official Plan and Zoning By-law Amendment Application - Request for Direction
summary: City Council has approved a settlement regarding a proposed development at 5238 Dundas Street West and 28 Jopling Avenue South. This means the city is agreeing to a revised plan for new construction in this Etobicoke-Lakeshore neighbourhood, pending review by an independent tribunal and ensuring local infrastructure can handle the changes. This decision helps shape the type of development permitted in the community.

title: Construction Staging Area Time Extension - 75 Broadway Avenue
summary: The City Council has approved an extension for a construction staging area on Broadway Avenue, allowing the continued closure of a sidewalk and a portion of the curb lane until June 30, 2025. This means local residents, pedestrians, and drivers in the area will experience ongoing disruptions for an extended period, though the developer is directed to maintain safety, cleanliness, and clear communication.

title: Reviewing Revenue Tool Options for Toronto - by Mayor John Tory, seconded by Councillor Chris Moise
Reviewing Revenue Tool Options for Toronto - by Mayor John Tory, seconded by Councillor Chris Moise
Reviewing Revenue Tool Options for Toronto - by Mayor John Tory, seconded by Councillor Chris Moise
summary: Toronto City Council recently decided to explore new ways to raise money for the city, asking for a detailed report by late 2023 on various revenue-generating options. This includes a comprehensive study on potentially implementing a new parking levy on commercial, paid, and unpaid spaces across the city. The goal is to find new funding sources for city services and transit, which could impact residents and businesses.

title: COVID-19 Shelter Transition and Relocation Plan Update 2023
summary: To ensure continued support for residents experiencing homelessness, City Council has approved extending contracts for COVID-19 related shelter services until April 2024. This decision also involves increasing the value of some existing contracts to cover the ongoing costs of these vital services.

title: CaféTO 2023 and Beyond
CaféTO 2023 and Beyond
summary: City Council has updated its rules for the popular CaféTO program, making it easier for restaurants and cafés to set up outdoor patios in curb lanes. These changes remove a requirement for direct consent from neighbouring properties when patios want to extend their outdoor space in Business Improvement Areas, potentially leading to more flexible and expanded dining options.

title: 1625, 1633 and 1641 Kingston Road and 52 Birchcliff Avenue - Rental Housing Demolition Application - Final Report
summary: A developer has received approval from City Council to demolish 33 rental homes near Kingston Road and Birchcliff Avenue. This decision is conditional on the developer replacing all 33 units with new rental homes, with 32 offered at affordable rents and one at mid-range rent for at least 10 years. Current tenants will also receive a relocation plan, including financial assistance and the option to return to the new building.

title: Changing Parking Regulations for Mosques in Toronto Centre
committee: Toronto and East York Community Council
summary: Toronto and East York Community Council has asked city staff to study allowing free one-hour parking on Fridays between 12 p.m. and 3 p.m. along parts of Parliament Street. This change aims to make parking easier for residents attending Friday prayers at local mosques, promoting equity by offering similar parking flexibility to what's provided for Sunday church services.

title: 20 Pinemore Crescent - Application to Remove a Private Tree
summary: North York Community Council denied a resident's request to remove a large, healthy bur oak tree at 20 Pinemore Crescent. This decision protects the city's urban forest by upholding bylaws that prevent the removal of maintainable trees on private property.

title: 20 Pinemore Crescent - Application to Remove a Private Tree
31 Norden Crescent - Application to Remove a Private Tree
summary: North York Community Council has approved the removal of a large, healthy black walnut tree from a private property at 31 Norden Crescent. This decision goes against city staff recommendations, who argued the tree should be protected as it was only being removed to make space for a new swimming pool. This action impacts the local tree canopy and the city's guidelines for private tree protection.

title: 2023 Capital and Operating Budgets
summary: Toronto's Budget Committee recommended a 2023 budget for the Mayor to propose to City Council, which includes a 5.5% property tax increase for residents. This plan also dedicates over $615 million to various housing-related programs and extends the City Building Levy for 10 years to fund important infrastructure projects across the city.

title: Update on 2023 Toronto Public Health Operating Budget Submission and Service Levels
summary: Toronto's Board of Health approved an update to the 2023 spending plan and services provided by Toronto Public Health. This decision makes public previously confidential information about proposed budget efficiencies and asks for a review of existing public health services, ensuring they reflect lessons learned from the COVID-19 pandemic.

title: Election of Chair for 2023 - Board of Health
summary: The Board of Health has elected Chris Moise to serve as its Chair for the year 2023. This decision is important because the Chair leads the Board in making key decisions about public health services and initiatives for Toronto residents.

title: 2023 Capital and Operating Budgets
2023 Capital and Operating Budgets
summary: The Budget Committee has postponed its decision on the city's 2023 Capital and Operating Budgets, moving the discussion to a later date. This delay means the final plans for property taxes, city services, and major investments for the year are still pending. The committee also requested more information on various topics, including childcare centres, housing grants, and the financial impact of delaying climate initiatives.

title: Implementing Bill 109, the More Homes for Everyone Act, 2022
summary: Toronto City Council has approved changes to implement the province's Bill 109, "More Homes for Everyone Act," which aims to speed up housing development approvals. The city will streamline its application processes, hire 150 new staff to handle faster timelines, and asked the province to delay mandatory fee refunds for missed deadlines. These increased staffing and operational costs will be covered by existing development application fees, not property taxes.

title: Operating Variance Report for the Nine Months Ended September 30, 2022
summary: Toronto City Council has formally requested that the federal government provide funding to help cover the city's 2022 budget shortfall. Council also decided to use 2023 property tax notices to inform residents across the city about whether this federal support is received, highlighting the financial impact of inter-governmental funding decisions.

title: Legislative Changes to City Governance - Bill 3 and Bill 39
Legislative Changes to City Governance - Bill 3 and Bill 39
Legislative Changes to City Governance - Bill 3 and Bill 39
summary: Toronto City Council responded to the Province's new "Strong Mayor" powers by requesting the Mayor to not use certain new authorities that would allow motions to pass with less than a majority vote. Council also asked the Province to give Toronto more local decision-making power on issues like housing and revenue, and to consult with the city on future governance changes. This item is about preserving Council's role in local democracy.

title: Request for Metrolinx Accountability and Financial Compensation Regarding the Ongoing Construction Delays of the Eglinton Crosstown - by Councillor Mike Colle, seconded by Councillor Josh Matlow
summary: Toronto City Council is demanding accountability and financial compensation from the Province and Metrolinx for the long-standing delays and disruptions caused by the Eglinton Crosstown LRT construction. They've requested an independent review and an explanation from Metrolinx officials regarding the project's issues and new completion timeline. The Council also called for Metrolinx and the Province to fund property tax rebates for affected residents, provide free TTC bus fares along Eglinton, and deploy traffic wardens to help alleviate the burden on communities.

title: Request for Metrolinx Accountability and Financial Compensation Regarding the Ongoing Construction Delays of the Eglinton Crosstown - by Councillor Mike Colle, seconded by Councillor Josh Matlow
360 Evans Avenue - Minor Variance Appeal at the Toronto Local Appeal Body - by Councillor Frances Nunziata, seconded by Councillor Amber Morley
360 Evans Avenue - Minor Variance Appeal at the Toronto Local Appeal Body - by Councillor Frances Nunziata, seconded by Councillor Amber Morley
summary: City Council has decided to drop its challenge against the approval of a bingo hall at 360 Evans Avenue. This means the city will no longer oppose the decision by the Committee of Adjustment to allow the bingo hall, making it more likely to proceed.

title: Improving Safety on Bloor Street Bike Lanes during Construction - by Councillor Dianne Saxe, seconded by Deputy Mayor Jennifer McKelvie
Improving Safety on Bloor Street Bike Lanes during Construction - by Councillor Dianne Saxe, seconded by Deputy Mayor Jennifer McKelvie
summary: Toronto City Council has directed city staff to investigate and propose solutions for improving safety on Bloor Street bike lanes during construction. This means staff will look into ways for cyclists and people using motorized wheelchairs to safely navigate construction zones without having to move into car traffic or onto pedestrian sidewalks, aiming to make bike lanes safer for everyone.

title: Ensuring Transparency in the 2022 Election: Amending the Contribution Rebate Program - by Councillor Paula Fletcher, seconded by Councillor Chris Moise
summary: City Council sent a proposal to the Executive Committee that aims to increase transparency in the 2022 election. The proposal suggests changes to the Contribution Rebate Program, which helps candidates get back some of their campaign donation costs, to make the process more open to the public.

title: 2023 Tax and Rate Supported Interim Operating and Capital Spending Authorities
summary: Toronto City Council approved an interim spending plan of $7.488 billion to keep essential city services running smoothly at the start of 2023. This temporary funding ensures city programs and agencies can continue operating without disruption until the full annual budget is finalized.

title: 2023 Housing Action Plan
2023 Housing Action Plan
2023 Housing Action Plan
summary: Toronto City Council has directed city staff to create a "2023 Housing Action Plan" with the ambitious goal of building 285,000 new homes over the next decade. This plan will tackle housing shortages by updating zoning rules, reviewing city policies, and partnering with various organizations to increase housing options across all neighbourhoods.

title: Sponsorship Program Update
summary: Toronto Parking Authority has approved a plan to secure a title sponsor for Bike Share Toronto. This partnership aims to generate new revenue and in-kind contributions, helping to offset operating and capital costs and improve the overall Bike Share service for residents.

title: 51 Panorama Court - Notice of Intention to Designate a Property under Part IV, Section 29 of the Ontario Heritage Act and Authority to Enter into a Heritage Easement Agreement
summary: Toronto City Council has decided to protect the property at 51 Panorama Court under the Ontario Heritage Act. This action, which takes effect when the provincial government transfers the property to a new owner, ensures the historic former Hospital for Sick Children site is preserved for its cultural heritage value.

title: 2 Amberjack Boulevard - Application for Fence Exemption
summary: Scarborough Community Council has approved a homeowner's request at 2 Amberjack Boulevard to keep their fence and hedges taller and denser than city bylaws typically allow. This decision was made due to safety concerns raised by the resident, highlighting how the city can make exceptions to property rules for specific circumstances.

title: 415 Broadview Avenue - Official Plan and Zoning By-law Amendment - Request for Direction Report
committee: Toronto and East York Community Council
summary: Toronto City Council has decided to oppose a proposed development at 415 Broadview Avenue, instructing city lawyers to argue against it at the provincial planning tribunal. This action ensures that any future development on the site adheres to city planning rules, addresses potential impacts on local services like water and traffic, and protects local heritage.

title: 25 Ames Circle - Application for Fence Exemption
summary: North York Community Council approved a special request for a homeowner at 25 Ames Circle. This allows them to continue using their house wall as part of their pool enclosure, even though it doesn't fully meet city fence rules, provided the existing fence is kept in good repair. This decision addresses a specific property issue and indicates how similar exemption requests might be handled by the council in the future.

title: 415 Broadview Avenue - Notice of Intention to Designate a Property under Part IV, Section 29 of the Ontario Heritage Act
summary: Toronto City Council has decided to begin the process of officially designating the property at 415 Broadview Avenue as a heritage site. This action aims to protect the building's historical or architectural significance, ensuring its preservation for future generations. If there are no objections, the property will receive formal heritage status, limiting future alterations or demolition.

title: Update on Bill 23, More Homes Built Faster Act, 2022
Update on Bill 23, More Homes Built Faster Act, 2022
summary: Toronto City Council has formally asked the Province of Ontario to reverse parts of Bill 23, the More Homes Built Faster Act, 2022. The city is particularly concerned about proposed provincial changes that would reduce the funds collected from developers, which are used to pay for new infrastructure, services, and parks. Council also requested the province amend the Bill to preserve the city's green building standards and its ability to require affordable housing.

title: Election of the Speaker and Deputy Speaker
summary: Toronto City Council has elected its new leadership team for the term ending in November 2026. Councillor Frances Nunziata will serve as Speaker and Councillor Stephen Holyday as Deputy Speaker. This decision means they will preside over Council meetings, guiding discussions and ensuring proper procedure for important city matters.
